💼 ProcessCore Proposal Builder
Build professional consulting proposals without leaving WW Pro-Calc.
5
Engagement Templates
Live
Fee Calculation
PDF
Print to Client Proposal
30
Saved Draft Proposals
How to Access
1
Open WW Pro-Calc as usual in your browser.
2
Click the green 💼 button in the top-right header bar (next to the 💾 Scenarios button).
3
The builder opens as a full-screen overlay. Use the three tabs at the top — Builder, Preview, and Saved.
Engagement Type Cards (Section 1)
Click a card to select it — the scope table reloads automatically with pre-built tasks for that engagement type.
Card Best For Pre-loaded Tasks
🏭 Plant AssessmentNew clients, annual evals9 tasks: data review, site visit, process calcs, TCEQ compliance, interviews, written report, implementation plan, findings meeting, 30-day support
🔬 Process TroubleshootingActive permit violations, upsets6 tasks: emergency site visit, data analysis, root cause, corrective action support, written report, follow-up visit
⚙️ Process OptimizationReduce costs, improve quality6 tasks: baseline analysis, aeration optimization, sludge handling, chemical feed, optimization report, operator coaching
📋 TCEQ ComplianceNOV response, inspection prep6 tasks: DMR review, permit interpretation, corrective action plan, TCEQ correspondence, pre-inspection audit, compliance training
🎓 Operator TrainingOn-site or remote education5 tasks: needs assessment, full-day training, materials development, virtual session, competency assessment
✏️ Custom ScopeAnything else5 generic tasks: mobilization, field work, technical analysis, written report, project management
⚠️ Switching engagement types reloads the scope table. Custom lines you added will be lost. Choose your type first, then customize.
Client Info (Section 2) & Billing Rate (Section 3)
Client Info — Fill in facility name, contact, location, plant size, treatment type, and a one-sentence project background. These populate the printed proposal automatically.
Billing Rate — Click a preset button ($125–$225/hr) or type a custom rate. The fee summary bar updates instantly as you change the rate.
Scope of Work Table (Section 4)
✓ CheckboxesChecked = included in fee total. Unchecked = shown in proposal as optional but excluded from NTE. Use this to show optional add-ons without inflating the number.
Edit hoursClick any number in the Hrs column and type a new value. Fee column and summary bar update immediately.
Delete a taskClick the ✕ button at the far right of any row.
Add custom linesType description in the "Add custom line item" field at the bottom, enter hours, click "+ Add". New line appears at bottom of the table.
💡 For Plant Assessments, the 30-day post-report support task (4 hrs) is unchecked by default — it shows the client it exists as an optional add-on without inflating the NTE.
Expenses & Terms (Section 5)
Travel / MileageEstimated vehicle, hotel, or airfare costs. Shown separately in the fee summary.
Lab / TestingProcess sampling or bench testing you arrange on the client's behalf.
Contingency %Added to labor + expenses subtotal. Default 15%. Printed proposal shows a ±5% fee range around the total — standard practice for T&M consulting.
Payment TermsDropdown selection prints verbatim in the proposal Terms section.
Fee Summary Bar
The green bar at the bottom of the Builder tab updates in real time.
Total HoursSum of hours for checked scope items only.
Labor FeeTotal hours × your selected hourly rate.
ExpensesTravel + lab + other expenses combined.
ContingencyContingency % × (labor + expenses).
TOTAL ESTIMATELabor + Expenses + Contingency. This is your not-to-exceed number.
Preview Tab & Printing to PDF
Click "Generate Proposal" or the Preview tab to see the fully formatted client-ready document. The proposal includes:
• ProcessCore Systems header
• Client info & project background
• Auto-generated proposal number
• Numbered scope of work table
• Fee summary with line items
• ±5% fee range
• Payment terms
• Standard T&C paragraph
• Dual signature block
•
To save as PDF:
1
Click 🖨️ Print / Save PDF at the top of the Preview tab.
2
In Chrome: change Destination to "Save as PDF". Check "Background graphics".
3
Name the file with client name and date, then save.
Save & Load Drafts
Up to 30 proposals saved to your browser's localStorage. Persist between sessions in the same browser.
Save a draftClick "💾 Save Draft" in the Builder action row. Saves immediately — newest at top of list.
Load a draftClick the Saved tab, find by client name / date / total, click "Load". All fields restore exactly.
Delete a draftClick the ✕ button next to any saved proposal.
Export to CSVClick "📊 Export CSV" from Builder or Preview tab. Downloads a spreadsheet of all scope lines with hours, rate, fee, and included/excluded status.
⚠️ Proposals save to your browser only. Clearing browser data removes them. Always generate a PDF immediately after building important proposals.
Recommended Workflow (Plant Assessment)
1
Select "Plant Assessment" — 9-task scope loads automatically.
2
Fill in client info — name, contact, location, plant size, treatment type, one-sentence background.
3
Set your rate — $175 or $200/hr depending on client and complexity.
4
Adjust the scope — edit hours, check/uncheck tasks, add custom lines.
5
Enter expenses — travel if outside Houston, lab if you're doing process sampling. Set contingency to 15%.
6
Review the fee bar — typical Plant Assessment: 35–45 hrs, $6,000–$9,000 NTE at $175/hr.
7
Save the draft — so you can reload if the client has questions.
8
Generate & print PDF — Preview tab → Print / Save PDF → email to client.
Pricing Benchmarks
EngagementEst. HoursNTE Range
Plant Assessment — small plant (< 1 MGD)25–35 hrs$4,500–$6,500
Plant Assessment — medium plant (1–5 MGD)35–50 hrs$6,500–$10,000
Process Troubleshooting (active upset)20–35 hrs$3,500–$7,000
TCEQ Compliance / NOV Response15–25 hrs$2,500–$5,000
Operator Training (full day, with materials)18–22 hrs$3,200–$4,400
Quick Reference
Change typeClick a different engagement card in Section 1
Add a taskUse "Add custom line item" field at bottom of scope table
Remove a taskClick ✕ on the right side of any scope row
Exclude (keep visible)Uncheck the checkbox — shows in proposal but doesn't add to fee
Change hoursClick the Hrs number and type a new value
See fee totalCheck the green fee summary bar at the bottom of Section 5
Print to PDFPreview tab → 🖨️ Print / Save PDF → Save as PDF in browser
Save a draftClick 💾 Save Draft in the Builder action row
Load a draftSaved tab → find client → click Load
Export to ExcelClick 📊 Export CSV from Builder or Preview tab
New proposalClick 🔄 New Proposal — confirms before clearing
Close builderClick ✕ in top right or click outside the overlay
ProcessCore Systems · WW Pro-Calc v9.25 · Proposal Builder

💾 Saved Scenarios

📚 SCENARIO MANAGER — USER GUIDE
What Are Scenarios?
Scenarios let you save all your calculator inputs so you can recall them later. Perfect for:
• Comparing different operating conditions (summer vs winter)
• Saving "what-if" calculations for process optimization
• Documenting baseline conditions before making changes
• Quickly switching between multiple plants or processes
💾 How to Save a Scenario
Step 1: Enter values in any calculators you want to save
Step 2: Click the 💾 button in the header
Step 3: Type a descriptive name (e.g., "Summer High Flow" or "Plant A Baseline")
Step 4: Click Save Current
✓ All inputs from all calculators are saved automatically
📂 How to Load a Scenario
Step 1: Click 💾 to open the Scenario Manager
Step 2: Find your saved scenario in the list
Step 3: Click Load
✓ All saved values are restored to the calculators
📊 How to Compare Scenarios
Compare two scenarios side-by-side to see how changes affect results:

Step 1: Click Compare on your first scenario
Step 2: Click Compare on your second scenario
Step 3: A comparison panel appears showing results side-by-side
💡 Great for: Before/After, Summer/Winter, High Flow/Low Flow comparisons
🗑️ How to Delete a Scenario
Click the ✕ button on any scenario to delete it.
⚠️ This cannot be undone!
💡 Pro Tips
• Naming: Use descriptive names with dates → "2026-02-12 Morning Baseline"
• Limit: Up to 20 scenarios stored (oldest auto-deleted)
• Storage: Saved in your browser's localStorage — stays on this device
• Compare Use Case: Save current conditions, make changes, save again, compare!
• TCEQ Prep: Save practice exam scenarios to review calculations later

📊 Compare Scenarios

💡 Tip: Differences are highlighted. Click ✕ to close comparison. Load either scenario to work with those values.
✓ Scenario Saved
📌
Workspace Pin up to 3 calculators here for side-by-side reference

Preset:
▼ 📖 How to Use This Assessment
5-part quick guide
① What this tool does
Enter today's plant readings one time — the tool computes all 19 key process parameters (F/M, SRT, SOR, SLR, OLR, WLR, HRT, RAS/WAS needed, BOD/TSS removal, effluent compliance) and flags anything out of range. Designed for daily shift rounds and exam prep.
② Workflow in 4 steps
Step 1 — Plant Fixed Data (one time only)
Scroll to the gray 🏭 Plant Fixed Data section and enter basin volume, clarifier area, weir length, and targets from your O&M manual or as-built drawings. Set once, persists forever. Unlocks SOR, SLR, WLR, HRT, Clarifier DT tiles.
Step 2 — Today's readings
Enter shift data in the 4 colored sections: Flow, Aeration, Clarifier/RAS/WAS, Effluent. Enter only what you have — blank fields are skipped, not errors. Results auto-switch open once data is available.
Step 3 — Review results
19 tiles grouped by Process Control / Clarifier Hydraulics / Sludge Management / Effluent & Compliance. Color-coded with live status. Tap any tile to jump to the full calculator for deeper analysis.
Step 4 — Save / Report
💾 Save Snapshot stores to history (last 60 shifts). 🖨 Report opens a printable HTML report with inputs, all tiles, permit compliance, and notes. ↺ Load Last restores your last shift for quick comparison.
③ Tile color states — quick reference
■ Green — value in normal range
■ Yellow — watch, review
■ Red — action required
■ Blue — informational
⋯ Grey — missing input(s)
~ Dashed — value estimated
④ What to enter first if you're in a hurry
Tap 💧 Quick Check preset — highlights the 4 highest-leverage inputs: Flow, MLSS, SV₃₀, DO. These 4 values unlock SVI, partial F:M (with MLVSS estimate), and DO status. Add MLVSS and SRT target to unlock F:M and WAS Needed. Add clarifier area + weir length (fixed data) to unlock the entire Clarifier Hydraulics group.
⑤ Pro tips
  • Use as shift handoff: Save snapshot at end of shift + type notes. Incoming operator taps ↺ Load Last.
  • Set up Permit Profile first (📋 Permit module): inline permit badges will appear on effluent tiles automatically.
  • Trust the estimates, but verify: Estimated tiles show the assumption (~ MLVSS est. 0.78 × MLSS). When you get real MLVSS data, enter it to replace the estimate.
  • Print the Sources reference (section below) and keep it at your lab bench or sample point.
  • Auto-save is on: Your draft persists if you navigate away. Don't worry about losing entries.
⚠ Important: Results reflect the values you enter. They are not live-linked to SCADA. Always verify permit-critical decisions against your actual instrumentation and lab reports.
▼ 📋 Input Sources — Where to Find Each Value
33 fields · printable reference
💡 Each row below tells you where the value comes from, how often it's sampled, how to sample it, and common mistakes to avoid. Tap 🖨 on your browser to print this reference and keep it at your lab bench.
💧 Flow & Loading
FieldSourceFrequencySample methodNotes / common mistakes
Flow (MGD)SCADA / magmeter totalizerContinuous; read daily avgPrevious 24-hr total ÷ 1 dayIf meter is GPM: × 1440 ÷ 1,000,000 = MGD. Always use influent flow, not effluent.
Inf. BOD (mg/L)Lab BOD₅ 5-day test1–7×/wk per permit24-hr composite sampler at influentHeld 5 days at 20°C; result lags 5 days. Use most recent result. Use CBOD₅ if permit specifies.
Inf. TSS (mg/L)Lab TSS gravimetricDaily or weekly per permitSame composite bottle as BODGravimetric (dried filter) not turbidity. Results available same day.
Inf. Temp (°C)SCADA probe or handheldDailyGrab at influent channelCritical for nitrification SRT temp correction. °F → °C: (F−32) × 5/9.
🌊 Aeration Basin
FieldSourceFrequencySample methodNotes / common mistakes
MLSS (mg/L)Lab TSS of mixed liquorDailyGrab sample mid-basin; avoid inlet or RAS returnUse gravimetric (oven-dried filter) not optical. Optical reads 10–30% high.
MLVSS (mg/L)Lab volatile solids burnWeekly oftenSame sample as MLSS; muffle furnace 550°CIf blank, tool auto-estimates 0.78 × MLSS. Real value typically 0.72–0.82.
DO (mg/L)Aeration basin DO probeContinuousSCADA average or spot handheld checkTarget 2–3 mg/L at far-end sensor. Calibrate probe weekly. Below 1.0 = filamentous risk.
pHBench pH meter or inline probeDaily grab or continuousGrab at basin endTypical 6.8–7.4. Below 6.5 = alkalinity depletion, check nitrification.
SV₃₀ (mL/L)Settleometer 1L coneDailySame grab as MLSS → settle 30 min → read volumeRead at exactly 30 minutes. Not 20, not 60. Drives SVI calc.
Basin Temp (°C)In-basin probe or handheldDailyGrab mid-basinDrives nitrification SRT temp correction factor: SRT × 1.072^(20−T).
🥛 Clarifier, RAS & WAS
FieldSourceFrequencySample methodNotes / common mistakes
RAS Flow (MGD)RAS magmeter or pump runtimeContinuousSCADA avg, or GPM × 1440 ÷ 10⁶If no meter: pump nameplate GPM × runtime fraction × 1440 ÷ 10⁶.
RAS Conc (mg/L)Lab TSS of RASWeekly oftenGrab at RAS pump dischargeTypically 2–3× MLSS. If blank, auto-estimated 2.5 × MLSS.
Sludge Blanket (ft)Sludge judge or sonar probeDailyLower judge until you see floc beginKeep blanket 1–3 ft below water surface. Rising blanket = SVI or SLR problem.
WAS Flow (GPD)WAS magmeter or pump runtimeDaily totalSum today's pump hrs × rated GPM × 60Read SCADA totalizer if available. Otherwise: pump hours × GPM × 60 = GPD.
WAS Conc (mg/L)Lab TSS of WASDaily or weeklyGrab at WAS pump dischargeSimilar to RAS (2–3× MLSS). If blank, auto-estimated 8,000 mg/L typical.
🧪 Effluent Quality
FieldSourceFrequencySample methodNotes / common mistakes
Eff. BOD (mg/L)Lab BOD₅Per permit (1–7×/wk)24-hr composite at effluentResults lag 5 days. Use most recent. Use CBOD if permit specifies.
Eff. TSS (mg/L)Lab TSSDaily or per permit24-hr composite at effluentSame sample bottle as effluent BOD.
Eff. NH₃ (mg/L)Lab nesslerization or ISE probeWeekly or per permitGrab or composite at effluentCritical for nitrification permits. Rising NH₃ = nitrifier stress or washout.
Eff. NO₃ (mg/L)Lab nitrate test or ISEWeeklyGrab at effluentRising = good nitrification. Falling with high NH₃ = incomplete nitrification.
Eff. TP (mg/L)Lab total P digestPer permitComposite at effluentLeave blank if no TP permit limit.
Eff. pHEffluent pH probeContinuous or daily grabSCADA or handheldTCEQ typical permit 6.0–9.0.
E. coli (MPN/100mL)Lab IDEXX Colilert or platePer permitGrab at effluent after disinfectionMonthly geomean ≤126 typical. Daily max ≤576. Check sample time matches permit window.
Cl₂ Residual (mg/L)DPD test kit or continuous analyzerDaily grab + continuousGrab after contact chamber, before dischargeMinimum typically 0.2 mg/L (check permit). Dechlorination may require max cap too.
Turbidity (NTU)Turbidity meterContinuous if requiredGrab or inline sensorUsually only tracked on filtered effluent (tertiary plants).
🏭 Plant Fixed Data (set once)
FieldSourceFrequencySample methodNotes / common mistakes
Aeration Vol (MG)O&M manual or as-built drawingsOnceDesign specIf only dimensions: L × W × SWD × 7.48 ÷ 10⁶ = MG. Include all in-service basins.
Aer. Vol (1,000 ft³)SameAuto-computed if blankMG × 133.681Auto-fills from MG. Override if you prefer native ft³ spec.
# Basins ActiveCurrent operational statusUpdate on basin bypassVisual / SCADAAffects per-basin loading. Reduce if taking a basin offline.
Clarifier Area (ft²)O&M manualOnceRound: π × (D/2)². Rect: L × W. Sum all in-serviceUse surface area at top water level. NOT cross-section. NOT per-unit — use total.
Clarifier SWD (ft)O&M manualOnceSide water depthWater column depth only, not total tank depth. Usually 10–14 ft for secondary.
Weir Length (ft)O&M manualOnceMeasure effluent weir(s), totalCircular: π × D (peripheral). Don't include scum or influent baffles.
# Clarifiers ActiveCurrent operational statusUpdate on bypassVisual / SCADAUsed for per-unit loading QA. Total area above already covers all in service.
Target SRT (days)O&M manual or process strategyOn process changeDesign basis4–8 d CAS, 10–15 d nitrification, 18–25 d extended aeration. Drives WAS Needed target.
Design MLSS (mg/L)O&M manualOnceDesign basisTypical CAS 2,000–3,000; extended aeration 3,000–5,000; MBR 8,000–12,000.
📝 Shift Notes (free text box at bottom) — Record equipment issues, process actions taken, unusual odors, visible sludge condition, foam observations, bulking events. Notes save with every snapshot and appear in the printable report.

SVI Interpretation: 80–150 mL/g = good settling | >150 = bulking risk | >200 = severe bulking | <80 = pin floc/old sludge. Track 7-day trends to catch developing problems early.

🔑 Core Process Calculators — Pounds Formula, SVI, F/M, SRT, WAS/RAS, Clarifier Loading

1 Pounds Formula

Convert concentration to daily mass load — or reverse-solve for concentration from a known load. Use for BOD, TSS, ammonia, phosphorus, and any mg/L parameter.
POUNDS FORMULA — OPERATOR GUIDE
The Formula
lbs/day = Flow (MGD) × Concentration (mg/L) × 8.34
The 8.34 factor converts gallons to pounds of water (1 gallon of water weighs 8.34 lbs). This formula is the foundation of wastewater math — everything from chemical dosing to permit compliance reporting uses it.
5 Daily Uses at Your Plant
① DMR Reporting — Convert effluent BOD and TSS mg/L lab results into lbs/day for your monthly discharge monitoring report. Your permit has both a concentration limit AND a mass limit.

② Chemical Dosing Verification — Confirm your chlorine, alum, or polymer feed rate matches the dose you intended. Enter flow + target dose (mg/L) → get required lbs/day → convert to your feed pump setting.

③ Loading Analysis — Calculate influent BOD and TSS mass loading to size processes, compare to design capacity, or document wet weather loading events.

④ Sludge Production — Estimate daily dry solids production from MLSS data for hauling and land application scheduling.

⑤ Permit Compliance Margin — Know how many lbs/day of headroom you have before hitting a mass-based permit limit.
Reverse Solve
Use the Reverse Mode below: enter your permitted lbs/day limit + your flow → get the maximum allowable mg/L concentration. Useful when your permit specifies a mass limit and you want to know the equivalent concentration limit at your current flow.
Key Notes
• 8.34 is for water at 68°F. For most wastewater applications this is close enough — do not adjust for temperature unless doing precise engineering calculations
• Flow must be in MGD. To convert: GPD ÷ 1,000,000 | GPM × 1,440 ÷ 1,000,000
• TCEQ exam: They almost always give flow in MGD and ask for lbs/day. Memorize: MGD × mg/L × 8.34 = lbs/day. They may also give GPD — just divide by 1,000,000 first
• lbs/MG (pounds per million gallons) = concentration × 8.34 — useful for comparing loading across different flow days
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Flow (MGD)Flow meter totalizer ÷ 1,000,000. Daily operator log or SCADA daily average.
Concentration (mg/L)Lab result from composite or grab sample. Lab bench sheet or LIMS report.
Known Load (lbs/day)Reverse-calculate from a known result; or permit limit converted to mass.
Parameter selectorChoose the pollutant matching your lab report (BOD, TSS, NH₃, etc.).
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Flow meter totalizer ÷ 1,000,000. Daily operator log or SCADA daily average.
Concentration (mg/L)Lab result from composite or grab sample. Lab bench sheet or LIMS report.
Known Load (lbs/day)Reverse-calculate from a known result; or permit limit converted to mass.
Parameter selectorChoose the pollutant matching your lab report (BOD, TSS, NH₃, etc.).
Million gallons per day

Enter data...

Flow (MGD) × Conc (mg/L) × 8.34 = lbs/day

2 SVI – Sludge Volume Index DIAGRAM

Measures sludge settleability. Perform 30-min settleometer test.
InputWhere to Get It
30-min Settled (mL/L)Settleometer test — 1L graduated cylinder, 30-min reading. Daily log sheet.
MLSS (mg/L)Lab test on mixed liquor grab sample. Filter/dry at 103°C. Daily log or LIMS.
Max 1000 mL/L
From lab
0.00 mL/g
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(Settled Vol × 1000) ÷ MLSS
SVI — SLUDGE VOLUME INDEX
What This Calculates
SVI (mL/g) is the single most important sludge settleability indicator in activated sludge. It tells you how much volume 1 gram of MLSS occupies after 30 minutes of settling. Lower SVI = denser, better-settling sludge. Higher SVI = fluffy, bulking sludge that carries over the weir.
SVI Interpretation
SVI (mL/g)StatusTypical Cause
< 80ExcellentDense, well-oxidized sludge. High SRT.
80–150GoodNormal range for conventional CAS.
150–200ElevatedEarly bulking. Check F:M and microscopy.
200–300BulkingFilamentous organisms dominant. Sludge blanket rising.
> 300SevereCritical — effluent TSS will violate permit. Reduce MLSS, chlorinate RAS.
SVI Exam Tips
• SVI = (mL settled ÷ mL sample) × (1000 ÷ MLSS mg/L) — two equivalent forms
• 30-min settleometer test: 1-liter graduated cylinder, fill to 1000 mL, read settled volume at 30 min
• If settled volume > 500 mL: dilute MLSS by 50%, re-test, multiply result by 2
• SVI > 150 = use Wizard: High SVI / Bulking for diagnosis
• SSVI (stirred SVI) is more reproducible — same procedure with gentle stirring

3 Mass Balance

Find the mixed concentration when two flow streams combine — RAS + influent, chemical blending, or any two-stream mix.
InputWhere to Get It
Stream A FlowFlow meter for that stream. Influent meter, RAS meter, or pump curve × runtime.
Stream A ConcentrationLab result for that stream. Same parameter and units across both streams.
Stream B FlowSecondary stream flow meter or calculated from pump capacity × runtime.
Stream B ConcentrationLab result for Stream B. RAS MLSS: use aeration basin MLSS × 1.5–2×.
MASS BALANCE — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculator Does
When two streams of different concentrations mix together, the result isn't a simple average — it's a flow-weighted average. A large dilute stream dominates over a small concentrated one. This calculator tells you exactly what concentration comes out the other end of any two-stream mix.
The 4 Most Common Uses at Your Plant
① RAS + Influent mixing (most common)
Stream A = Influent flow & MLSS (usually near 0 mg/L coming in)
Stream B = RAS flow & RAS suspended solids (typically 6,000–12,000 mg/L)
Result = Mixed liquor TSS entering the aeration basin
Example: 1.5 MGD influent at 200 mg/L TSS + 0.6 MGD RAS at 8,000 mg/L → mixed MLSS entering basin
② Chemical feed dilution
Stream A = Plant flow & baseline chemical concentration
Stream B = Chemical feed flow & stock solution concentration
Result = Resulting concentration in the process stream
Example: Mixing 12% sodium hypochlorite feed into effluent — what's the resulting Cl₂ concentration?
③ Blending two effluent streams
Stream A = Primary clarifier effluent flow & BOD
Stream B = Trickling filter or bypass flow & BOD
Result = Combined BOD entering secondary — helps predict loading
④ Digester blending / sludge mixing
Stream A = Primary sludge flow & solids %
Stream B = WAS flow & solids %
Result = Combined feed solids % entering the digester
Inputs Explained
Stream A & B Flow — Use the same units for both (MGD, GPM, or GPD — doesn't matter which, just be consistent). The calculator uses the ratio, not the absolute values.

Stream A & B Concentration — Use mg/L for both. If you're working with % solids, convert first: 1% = 10,000 mg/L.
Critical Operator Notes
• Flow units must match: Don't mix MGD and GPM — convert one first
• Small high-conc stream matters a lot: 0.5 MGD RAS at 10,000 mg/L has the same mass as 5.0 MGD influent at 1,000 mg/L
• Zero concentration is valid: If Stream A is clean water, enter 0 for its concentration
• TCEQ exam tip: The formula is (Q₁C₁ + Q₂C₂) ÷ (Q₁ + Q₂). They love giving you 3 of the 4 variables and asking you to solve for the missing one — this calc gives you the mixed result directly
• Verify your RAS ratio: RAS flow ÷ influent flow should be 25–75% for conventional activated sludge. If your mixed MLSS is coming out wrong, check your RAS rate first
Worked Example — RAS Mixing
Influent: Q = 2.0 MGD, TSS = 180 mg/L
RAS: Q = 0.8 MGD, TSS = 9,500 mg/L

Mixed = (2.0 × 180) + (0.8 × 9,500) ÷ (2.0 + 0.8)
      = (360 + 7,600) ÷ 2.8
      = 7,960 ÷ 2.8
      = 2,843 mg/L MLSS

→ This is your mixed liquor entering the aeration basin.
→ Compare to your target MLSS (typically 1,500–4,000 mg/L).
→ If too high, reduce RAS rate. If too low, increase RAS or reduce WAS.
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Stream A FlowFlow meter for that stream. Influent meter, RAS meter, or pump curve + runtime.
Stream A ConcentrationLab result for that stream. Use same parameter and units across both streams.
Stream B FlowSecondary stream flow meter or calculated from pump capacity × runtime.
Stream B ConcentrationLab result for Stream B. If estimating RAS MLSS: use aeration basin MLSS × 1.5–2×.
MGD, GPM, or GPD
mg/L (0 = clean water)
Same unit as Stream A
mg/L (RAS ≈ 6,000–12,000)
Quick reference: RAS TSS: 6,000–12,000 mg/L · Typical RAS ratio: 25–75% of influent flow · 1% solids = 10,000 mg/L
0.00 mg/L
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(Q₁×C₁ + Q₂×C₂) ÷ (Q₁ + Q₂)
MASS BALANCE — FLOW-WEIGHTED MIXING
What This Calculates
The mixed concentration when two (or more) flow streams combine. You cannot simply average concentrations — a stream with twice the flow has twice the influence. The mass balance accounts for this proportionally.
The Formula
C_mix = (Q₁ × C₁ + Q₂ × C₂) ÷ (Q₁ + Q₂)
Flows must be in the same units. Concentrations must be in the same units.
Daily Uses
RAS mixing: Aeration basin MLSS = f(WAS concentration, influent flow, RAS rate)
Digester blending: Primary + WAS sludge blend concentration
Receiving stream: Effluent mixing with stream flow (Calc #63 is the dedicated version)
Chemical dosing check: Diluted chemical concentration after mixing with plant flow
📝 TCEQ Exam Tip
The exam frequently asks: "Two streams combine — what is the mixed concentration?" Always use the mass balance formula. The most common trap: averaging concentrations without weighting by flow. That trap answer is always one of the multiple choice options.

4 F/M Ratio DIAGRAM

Food to Microorganism ratio. Key process control parameter.
Process Type — sets target F/M range
CAS target: 0.10 – 0.40 lbs BOD/lb VSS·day
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter daily average. SCADA or paper log.
BOD (mg/L)24-hr composite influent sample. BOD₅ incubation result. Lab bench sheet.
Aeration Vol (MG)As-built drawings. Length × Width × Depth ÷ 1,000,000 (convert ft³ to MG).
MLVSS (mg/L)Lab: filter MLSS sample, ignite at 550°C. MLVSS ≈ MLSS × 0.75–0.85 if no VSS test.
Influent flow
Influent BOD₅
Million gallons
Or MLSS × 0.75
Typical CAS: 0.4–0.8 g VSS/g BOD
Tip: MLVSS ≈ MLSS × 0.75 if not measured directly
0.000
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ (Vol × MLVSS × 8.34)
F/M RATIO — FOOD TO MICROORGANISM
What This Calculates
F/M (lbs BOD/lbs MLVSS/day) is the organic loading per unit of active biomass. It controls sludge age, effluent quality, and sludge settleability. Low F/M = old sludge, better settling, better effluent. High F/M = young sludge, filaments, variable effluent.
Target Ranges by Process
ProcessF/M RangeSRT (days)
Extended Aeration0.04–0.1020–30+
Conventional CAS0.10–0.405–15
High-Rate (BOD only)0.40–1.002–5
MBR0.05–0.1515–30
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• F/M = (Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ (Vol × MLVSS × 8.34) — 8.34 cancels! F/M = (Q × BOD) ÷ (Vol × MLVSS) in compatible units
• MLVSS ≈ 0.75 × MLSS for activated sludge (use 0.8 if VSS/TSS not given)
• F/M and SRT are inversely related — higher F/M = shorter SRT
• F/M too low (<0.05) → over-oxidized, pin floc, poor settling
• F/M too high (>0.5 CAS) → young sludge, filaments, foaming risk

5 SRT – Sludge Age DIAGRAM

Solids Retention Time. Critical for nitrification (need >8 days).
InputWhere to Get It
Aeration Vol (MG)As-built drawings. O&M manual specs.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab grab sample from aeration basin. Log sheet.
WAS Flow (MGD)WAS pump runtime × pump GPD rating ÷ 1,000,000. Or WAS flow meter.
WAS Conc (mg/L)Same as MLSS if wasting from aeration basin. RAS MLSS if wasting from RAS line.
Effluent Flow (MGD)Effluent flow meter. SCADA daily average.
Effluent TSS (mg/L)Effluent composite lab result. Required for DMR.
Water Temp (°C)Basin temperature probe. SCADA or field measurement.
Min SRT at 20°C (days)Design basis from O&M manual. Typically 4–8 days BOD removal, 8–15 days nitrification.
Basin volume
In aeration
Waste sludge flow
WAS or RAS conc
Discharge flow
Solids over weirs
🌡️ Temperature Correction (Nitrification SRT)
Enter temp and min SRT at 20°C to see winter/summer correction.
0.0 days
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
Solids in System ÷ Solids Out per Day
SRT — SLUDGE RETENTION TIME (SLUDGE AGE)
What This Calculates
SRT (days) is the average time a microorganism spends in the activated sludge system before being wasted out. It is THE fundamental design and control parameter for biological treatment — it determines nitrification, sludge production, effluent quality, and oxygen demand simultaneously.
SRT Targets by Treatment Goal
BOD removal only: 5–10 days (no nitrification needed)
Nitrification at 20°C: minimum 8–10 days (2–3× safety factor → 16–25 days)
Nitrification at 15°C: minimum 12–15 days → 24–35 days with safety factor
Nitrification at 10°C: minimum 20+ days → 40+ days with safety factor
Biological P removal: 5–12 days (too long → P release in anaerobic zones)
MBR: 15–30 days (membrane replaces clarifier, can run higher SRT)
The SRT Formula
SRT = (Vol × MLSS) ÷ (WAS_Q × WAS_C + Eff_Q × Eff_TSS)
Effluent TSS is often neglected — for good secondary treatment (<10 mg/L) it matters only at very long SRTs.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SRT = lbs MLSS in system ÷ lbs MLSS wasted per day (mass balance form)
• Minimum SRT for nitrification at 20°C: approximately 8–10 days
• For winter operation: use Calc #112 to correct minimum SRT for temperature
• SRT controls sludge production: longer SRT = less sludge (endogenous decay)
• WAS rate = (Vol × MLSS) ÷ (SRT × WAS_conc) — solving for WAS flow

6 Detention Time

Hydraulic retention time in any tank or process.
InputWhere to Get It
Volume (gallons)Tank Volume calc (#66) or as-built drawings. Length × Width × Depth × 7.48 gal/ft³.
Flow (GPD)Flow meter. Convert MGD × 1,000,000 if needed.
Tank volume
Gallons per day
Existing or design volume
Min required detention
Quick:
Enter volume and target HRT → max allowable flow.
Volume: Rect = L×W×D×7.48 | Circ = 0.785×D²×H×7.48
0.00 hours
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(Volume ÷ Flow) × 24
DETENTION TIME — HYDRAULIC RETENTION TIME
What This Calculates
Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) is the theoretical time water spends in a basin or reactor. It determines contact time for biological treatment, settling in clarifiers, and disinfection CT. Unlike SRT (solids retention), HRT applies to the liquid fraction only.
Typical HRT Ranges
Aeration basin (CAS): 4–8 hours
Primary clarifier: 1.5–2.5 hours
Secondary clarifier: 1.5–2.5 hours
Chlorine contact chamber: 20–90 minutes (for CT compliance)
Anaerobic digester: 15–30 days (HRT ≈ SRT for complete-mix)
Aerobic digester: 10–30 days
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• HRT = Volume ÷ Flow (units must match!) — Volume in gallons + Flow in GPD = HRT in days. Multiply by 24 for hours.
• Peak flow HRT = volume ÷ peak flow — always shorter than average flow HRT
• CT = Cl₂ residual × T10, where T10 = HRT × baffling factor (not total HRT)
• Exam trap: volume in MG and flow in MGD gives HRT in days — convert to hours by × 24
• For digesters: if HRT = SRT, solids don't accumulate (complete-mix, no recycle)

7 Removal Efficiency

Percent removal for BOD, TSS, ammonia, or any parameter.
InputWhere to Get It
Influent (mg/L)Influent composite sample lab result. Any parameter (BOD, TSS, NH₃, etc.).
Effluent (mg/L)Effluent composite sample lab result. Same parameter and units.
Before treatment
After treatment
0.0 %
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(In - Out) ÷ In × 100
REMOVAL EFFICIENCY — PERCENT REMOVAL
What This Calculates
Percent removal = (influent − effluent) ÷ influent × 100. Used for BOD, TSS, ammonia, phosphorus, and any other parameter to report treatment performance and verify permit compliance.
Typical Treatment Efficiencies
ParameterPrimary OnlySecondary (CAS)
BOD25–40%85–95%
TSS50–70%85–95%
TKN10–20%15–50% (without nitrif.)
Total P10–20%20–30% (without chem P)
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• % removal = (In − Out) ÷ In × 100 — effluent is ALWAYS subtracted FROM influent
• If given % removal and influent, find effluent: Out = In × (1 − removal%/100)
• Secondary treatment standard: 85% BOD and TSS removal OR 30/30 mg/L — whichever is more stringent
• % removal does NOT equal lbs/day removal — a small flow with high concentration can remove large lbs

8 WAS Pumping Rate

Calculate required WAS flow to achieve target SRT. Solve for wasting rate.
InputWhere to Get It
Current MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab grab sample from aeration basin midpoint.
Target MLSS (mg/L)O&M manual process targets. Or calculated from target SRT.
Current F:M RatioOutput from F:M calc (#4). Or calculate: BOD load ÷ (MLVSS × basin volume).
Recovery Time GoalYour operational decision. 3–7 days typical.
Aeration Vol (MG)As-built drawings. O&M manual.
Target SRT (days)O&M manual or design basis. 8–15 days for nitrification; 4–8 days BOD only.
WAS Conc (mg/L)MLSS if wasting from aeration basin. RAS MLSS if wasting from return line.
⚡ SHOCK LOAD RESPONSE — WAS Decision Tool
Enter shock load parameters above.
Basin volume
In aeration
Desired sludge age
WAS/RAS solids
Your WAS pump nameplate GPM
Note: This simplified calc ignores effluent TSS loss. For precise SRT, account for solids over weirs.
0 GPD
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
WAS Flow = (Vol × MLSS) ÷ (SRT × WAS Conc)
WAS PUMPING RATE — WASTE ACTIVATED SLUDGE
What This Calculates
The WAS flow required to achieve a target SRT, or the resulting SRT from a current WAS setting. WAS is your primary process control lever — it controls MLSS, SRT, F:M, and sludge production simultaneously.
Two Ways to Control WAS
SRT-based wasting (best practice):
WAS (GPD) = (Vol × MLSS) ÷ (SRT_target × WAS_conc)
Maintains consistent sludge age regardless of flow or loading changes. Requires knowing MLSS and WAS concentration daily.

MLSS-based wasting (simpler but reactive):
Waste more if MLSS rises above target; waste less if it drops. Reacts to problems rather than preventing them. Slow to stabilize after loading changes.
Practical WAS Adjustment Rule
Change WAS rate by no more than 10–15% per day. Biomass responds slowly — aggressive changes in either direction cause process instability. If SRT is 15 days, a 10% WAS change shifts SRT by ~1.5 days.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• WAS flow formula: Q_WAS = (Vol_basin × MLSS) ÷ (SRT × WAS_conc)
• Wasting from RAS line: WAS_conc ≈ RAS_conc (typically 6,000–12,000 mg/L)
• Wasting from aeration basin: WAS_conc = MLSS (typically 2,000–4,000 mg/L)
• If WAS concentration is unknown on exam, assume wasting from RAS at 10,000 mg/L

9 RAS Return Rate DIAGRAM

Calculate RAS flow rate based on sludge blanket or settleability method.
InputWhere to Get It
Influent Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA or daily log.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily aeration basin lab sample.
RAS Conc (mg/L)Lab grab sample from RAS return line or secondary clarifier underflow. Typically 6,000–12,000 mg/L.
Plant flow
In aeration
Return sludge TSS
⊕ Optional: Pump comparison & runtime
Your current pump GPM
Max pump rate
0.00 MGD
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
RAS = (Q × MLSS) ÷ (RAS Conc - MLSS)
RAS RETURN RATE — RETURN ACTIVATED SLUDGE
What This Calculates
RAS (Return Activated Sludge) rate is the flow of settled sludge returned from the clarifier to the aeration basin to maintain the MLSS concentration. RAS keeps the biomass in the system; WAS removes it. Getting RAS rate right prevents clarifier blanket buildup and maintains the MLSS target.
RAS Rate from Settleometer
RAS% = SV₃₀ ÷ (1000 − SV₃₀) × 100
SV₃₀ = settled volume at 30 min (mL/L from settleometer). This gives you the minimum RAS rate needed to maintain the current MLSS.
Typical RAS Rates
Conventional CAS: 25–100% of influent flow (Q_RAS = 0.25–1.0 × Q)
Extended aeration: 50–150% of influent flow
High MLSS (MBR): 200–400% — pumped continuously
Too low RAS → MLSS drops, blanket builds in clarifier → TSS carryover
Too high RAS → short-circuits clarifier hydraulics, dilutes MLSS
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• RAS% from SV₃₀: RAS% = SV₃₀ ÷ (1000 − SV₃₀) × 100
• RAS concentration from MLSS balance: RAS_conc = MLSS × (Q + Q_RAS) ÷ Q_RAS
• SVI and RAS are linked — high SVI requires higher RAS to prevent blanket rise
• Exam: "What RAS% is needed if SV₃₀ = 250 mL/L?" → 250 ÷ 750 × 100 = 33.3%

⚙ MLSS Control Center

Daily MLSS process control — inventory, trend prediction, target recovery, and WAS adjustment. The command center for activated sludge.
InputWhere to Get It
Process TypeYour design basis or permit. Check O&M manual or as-built drawings.
Current MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab test: mixed liquor grab sample, filter/dry at 103°C. Log sheet or LIMS.
Basin Volume (MG)As-built drawings. Also in your O&M manual or TCEQ permit application.
WAS Flow (GPD)WAS pump runtime × pump capacity (GPD). Or WAS flow meter.
WAS Conc (mg/L)Same as MLSS if wasting from aeration. RAS MLSS if wasting from RAS line.
Influent Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter daily average. SCADA or paper log.
Influent TSS (mg/L)Lab result from 24-hr composite influent sample.
Influent BOD (mg/L)Lab result from 24-hr composite influent sample. BOD₅ incubation.
Effluent BOD (mg/L)Lab result from 24-hr composite effluent sample.
Target MLSS (mg/L)O&M manual process targets or calculated from target SRT.
⚙ MLSS CONTROL CENTER — STEP-BY-STEP GUIDE
0
Pick Your Process Type first
Tap one of the 5 buttons — CAS, Ext. Aeration, Ox. Ditch, MBR, or High-Rate. This sets the target MLSS range, SRT range, and F/M range used throughout the card. Every status color and warning is calibrated to your process. If you're not sure, CAS is the default for most Texas activated sludge plants.
CAS = 1,500–3,000 mg/L · EA/OD = 2,500–5,000 · MBR = 8,000–15,000 · High-Rate = 1,000–2,000
A
Section A — Enter today's MLSS and basin volume
Current MLSS — pull this from this morning's lab TSS result on a mixed liquor sample from the aeration basin. If you have multiple basins, use the combined or average value.

Basin Volume — total aeration volume in million gallons. Check your O&M manual. For multiple basins in parallel, add them. Don't include clarifier volume.

What you get: Total lbs of solids in your system right now, and a range bar showing whether you're inside, below, or above your target range. Green = on target. Amber = watch it. Red = act now.
Example: MLSS = 2,800 mg/L, Vol = 0.5 MG → Inventory = 2,800 × 0.5 × 8.34 = 11,676 lbs of biomass in your basin
B
Section B — Enter today's operating data to predict tomorrow's MLSS
This is the core of the card. Fill in 6 fields and the card runs a full solids mass balance to show you exactly where your MLSS will be tomorrow morning.

WAS Flow (GPD) — how many gallons per day you wasted today. Check your WAS pump runtime and GPM rating, or read your flowmeter. If you waste intermittently (e.g. 2 hrs/day at 20 GPM), that's 20 × 120 min = 2,400 gallons.

WAS Concentration (mg/L) — TSS of your waste stream. If wasting from the RAS line, use your RAS TSS (typically 6,000–12,000 mg/L). If wasting from the aeration basin directly (gravity thickening), use your MLSS value.

Influent Flow (MGD) — today's average plant flow. Use your totalizer or flow recorder average.

Influent TSS (mg/L) — raw influent suspended solids from composite sample. If not measured daily, use a recent value or 200 mg/L as a typical estimate for domestic wastewater.

Influent BOD (mg/L) and Effluent BOD (mg/L) — used to calculate how much new biomass is being grown. The difference is BOD removed. Multiply by observed yield to get new solids entering the system per day.
Observed Yield quick-select: Tap EA=0.2 for extended aeration plants, CAS=0.5 for conventional activated sludge, or HR=0.7 for high-rate processes. If you've never calculated yours, use the button matching your process type.

What you get: Three boxes showing lbs/day IN, lbs/day OUT, and net change. Then the main result — projected MLSS tomorrow. Also shows a 7-day projection at your current WAS rate so you can see the trend, not just tomorrow.
Reading the result: If it says ↑ +180 mg/L, your MLSS is climbing — consider increasing WAS. If it says ↓ −240 mg/L, you're losing inventory fast — reduce WAS or check for a loading drop.
C
Section C — Set a target and get the exact WAS rate to hit it
Already have Sections A and B filled in. Now enter two more values:

Target MLSS — where you want to be. Use the midpoint of your process type's range. For CAS, 2,500 mg/L is a good center target. For extended aeration, 3,500–4,000 mg/L.

Days to Reach Target — how fast you need to get there. Typical answers: 3–5 days for routine adjustment, 1–2 days for urgent correction, 7–10 days for slow intentional shift.

What you get: The exact GPD and GPM WAS rate required. Plus a built-in safety check — if the required wasting would crash your SRT below safe limits, you'll see a red warning with the implied SRT. Green means safe to proceed. Don't skip this check — over-wasting is the most common cause of washout events.
Example: MLSS = 3,500 (too high), target = 2,500, 5 days. Section C calculates you need to waste 38,000 GPD. SRT check says ≈ 9.2 days — safe. Set your WAS pump accordingly.
🤖
AI Explain — get a plain-language interpretation of your specific numbers
Once Section B is filled and you have a projected MLSS result, tap 🤖 Explain this result. The AI receives all your actual inputs — current MLSS, WAS rate, loading, yield, process type, target — and gives you a field-level explanation of what's happening and what to do. Unlike the status text, it explains the biology, not just the threshold.
Key Rules Every Operator Must Know
🔑 WAS controls MLSS. RAS does not. RAS only controls the clarifier blanket position — it doesn't change total solids inventory. To raise MLSS → decrease WAS. To lower MLSS → increase WAS.
🔑 Nitrification requires SRT ≥ 8 days at 20°C. If you're chasing ammonia violations, check your SRT before anything else.
🔑 Wet weather: stop WAS first. Diluted influent drops MLSS fast on its own. Wasting during a rain event is how you lose your bugs and blow your effluent limits for the next 2 weeks.
🔑 MLSS rising + SVI rising = trouble ahead. High MLSS combined with poor settling = clarifier overload. Don't wait — increase WAS and check SVI today.
🔑 Check this card daily. 5 minutes every morning with today's lab values shows you the trend before it becomes a problem.
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Process TypeYour design basis or permit. Check O&M manual or as-built drawings.
Current MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab test: mixed liquor grab sample, filter/dry at 103°C. Log sheet or LIMS.
Basin Volume (MG)As-built drawings. Also in your facility O&M manual or TCEQ permit application.
WAS Flow (GPD)WAS pump runtime × pump capacity (GPD). Some plants use a flow meter on WAS line.
WAS Conc (mg/L)Same as MLSS if wasting directly from aeration. Use RAS MLSS if wasting from RAS line.
Influent Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter daily average. SCADA or paper log.
Influent TSS (mg/L)Lab result from 24-hr composite influent sample. Required for DMR on most permits.
Influent BOD (mg/L)Lab result from 24-hr composite influent sample. BOD incubation result (5-day).
Effluent BOD (mg/L)Lab result from 24-hr composite effluent sample. Same incubation.
Target MLSS (mg/L)Your process target from O&M manual, or calculated from target SRT × F:M.
Days to Reach TargetYour operational decision — how urgently you need to adjust.
CAS: MLSS 1,500–3,000 mg/L | SRT 5–12 days | F/M 0.15–0.5
🌧 RAIN EVENT MODE ACTIVE — WAS recommendation suppressed. Dilution will naturally drop MLSS. Monitor closely; resume wasting when influent BOD recovers above normal baseline.
A — CURRENT INVENTORY
Today's lab result
Aeration basin(s)
Settleometer 30-min read
Or auto = 0.78 × MLSS

—
lbs solids

days (needs WAS)

Enter MLSS to see status

lb VSS/lb BOD

lbs/day

🤖 AI Plant Advisor
C — TARGET RECOVERY — find the WAS rate to reach your goal
Where you want to be
Recovery timeline
Required WAS rate to reach — in — days:
— GPD
Net Δ lbs = (Inf TSS + Y×BOD_removed) × Q × 8.34 − WAS × WAS_C × 8.34

11 Organic Loading Rate DIAGRAM

BOD loading per unit volume. Used for trickling filters, RBCs, and aeration basins. Select your process type for calibrated thresholds.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA daily average.
BOD (mg/L)24-hr composite influent BOD₅ result. Lab bench sheet.
Volume (1,000 ft³ or MG)As-built drawings. Convert ft³ ÷ 1,000. Or use MG toggle.
Effluent BOD (optional)24-hr composite effluent sample. Enables removal efficiency display.
Design OLR (optional)O&M manual or permit. Shows % of design capacity currently in use.
CAS: Optimal 20–40 | Max 60 lbs BOD/day/1,000 ft³
Volume unit: tap to switch to MG
Influent flow
Influent BOD₅
Basin/media volume
Enables removal %
From O&M manual
For F/M + nitrif outlook
0.0 lbs/day/1000ft³
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ Volume in 1000 ft³
ORGANIC LOADING RATE
What This Calculates
OLR (lbs BOD/day/1000 ft³ or lbs BOD/day/MG) is the BOD mass applied per unit reactor volume. It determines how hard the biomass is working and whether the reactor is under- or over-loaded relative to its design capacity.
OLR Design Ranges
Conventional CAS aeration basin: 20–40 lbs BOD/day/1000 ft³
Extended aeration: 5–15 lbs BOD/day/1000 ft³
High-purity O₂ systems: 50–150 lbs BOD/day/1000 ft³
Anaerobic digester: 0.04–0.10 lbs VS/day/ft³ (different units)
Trickling filter (NRC): 5–25 lbs BOD/day/1000 ft³
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• OLR = (Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ (Volume in 1000 ft³ or MG)
• OLR and F:M are related: F:M = OLR ÷ (MLVSS × 1000 ft³ or per unit vol) — proportional when MLSS is stable
• OLR exceeding design = overloading — expect high effluent BOD and oxygen demand
• Convert MGD × mg/L × 8.34 = lbs/day first, then divide by volume

12 Surface Overflow Rate DIAGRAM

Hydraulic loading rate for clarifiers. Flow per unit surface area.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPD)Influent flow meter. Convert MGD × 1,000,000.
Surface Area (ft²)As-built drawings. Circular: π/4 × D². Rectangular: L × W.
Gallons per day
Clarifier surface area
Circular: Area = 0.785 × D² | Rectangular: Area = L × W
0 GPD/ft²
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
Flow (GPD) ÷ Surface Area (ft²)
SURFACE OVERFLOW RATE — SOR
What This Calculates
SOR (GPD/ft²) is the hydraulic loading rate on a clarifier — flow per unit of surface area. It determines how fast the water rises relative to how fast particles settle. When SOR exceeds the settling velocity of the smallest particles you want to capture, those particles overflow with the effluent.
TCEQ Design Standards
Clarifier TypeAverage SOR (GPD/ft²)Peak SOR
Primary600–1,0001,500–2,000
Secondary (CAS)400–8001,000–1,200
Secondary (Extended)200–400600–800
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SOR = Flow (GPD) ÷ Surface Area (ft²) — never use depth, never use volume
• Multiple clarifiers: use total surface area = n × individual area
• Use Calc #59 (Peak Flow Impact) to check SOR at peak flow — the most common exam scenario
• SOR exceeding 1,200 GPD/ft² = effluent TSS will spike regardless of sludge condition
⚡ Clarifier Loading Suite — All Three Parameters
Enter values in Calcs #11, #12, #13 first — or use Daily Log → Populate All

13 Weir Overflow Rate DIAGRAM

Flow per linear foot of weir. Important for clarifier performance.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPD)Influent flow meter. Convert MGD × 1,000,000.
Weir Length (ft)As-built drawings. Measure field perimeter of effluent weir. For circular: π × D.
Gallons per day
Total weir length
Circular weir: Length = π × Diameter (3.14 × D)
0 GPD/ft
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
Flow (GPD) ÷ Weir Length (ft)
WEIR OVERFLOW RATE — WOR
What This Calculates
WOR (GPD/ft of weir) measures the hydraulic stress on the clarifier effluent weir. Too high a WOR creates turbulence at the weir that lifts settled solids back into the effluent stream, causing TSS carryover independent of clarifier loading.
Design Standards
Acceptable WOR: < 20,000 GPD/ft (TCEQ guidance for secondary clarifiers)
Good practice: < 15,000 GPD/ft
High WOR (> 25,000): Turbulence creates rising currents that carry floc over the weir

Increasing weir length: Installing v-notch or launder weirs doubles or triples effective weir length, reducing WOR without changing clarifier area.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• WOR = Flow (GPD) ÷ Total Weir Length (linear feet)
• For circular clarifier: weir length = π × diameter (perimeter weir) or 2 × π × r (launder)
• WOR often appears alongside SOR and SLR in design criteria exam problems — run all three with Calc #11 "Run All Three" button
• WOR > 30,000 GPD/ft = immediate action needed — consider launder weir extension

14 Solids Loading Rate

Solids applied per unit clarifier area. Key for secondary clarifier design.
InputWhere to Get It
Influent + RAS Flow (MGD)Influent meter + RAS pump meter (or RAS pump capacity × runtime). Sum both.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily aeration basin lab sample.
Surface Area (ft²)As-built drawings. Clarifier surface area.
Total flow to clarifier
Mixed liquor solids
Clarifier area
0.0 lbs/day/ft²
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
(Flow × MLSS × 8.34) ÷ Surface Area
SOLIDS LOADING RATE — SLR
What This Calculates
SLR (lbs TSS/day/ft²) is the mass of solids applied to the clarifier per unit surface area. It determines whether the clarifier has enough area to thicken and return sludge without losing the sludge blanket. SLR is the limiting design criterion for secondary clarifiers at high MLSS — particularly for extended aeration and MBR plants.
Design Standards
ProcessDesign SLR (lbs/day/ft²)
Conventional CAS20–30 max
Extended aeration15–25 max
At peak flowNever exceed design × 1.5×
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SLR = (Q + Q_RAS) × MLSS × 8.34 ÷ (1,000,000 × Area) — includes both influent AND RAS flow
• Or: SLR = (Flow + RAS) in GPD × MLSS mg/L × 8.34 ÷ area ft²
• SLR > 30 lbs/day/ft² → blanket rises → clarifier loss → permit violation
• At peak flow: SOR goes up AND MLSS goes up (diluted by peak flow) but total lbs increase → SLR spikes disproportionately
• Use "⚡ Run All Three" button in Calc #11 to compute SOR + WOR + SLR simultaneously

15 Chemical Dosing

Calculate chemical feed rate. Works for chlorine, alum, polymer, lime, etc.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent or effluent flow meter. SCADA.
Dose (mg/L)Jar test results or permit requirement. Alum: 10–100 mg/L. Chlorine: 5–15 mg/L.
Chemical % PurityProduct label or Safety Data Sheet (SDS). Liquid bleach: 12.5%. Alum: 48.8%.
Treatment flow
Target dosage
65% for HTH, 12% for bleach
Common: Gas Cl₂ = 100% | HTH = 65% | Bleach = 12% | Alum = 48%
0.00 lbs/day
Enter data...
Measured or calculated feed rate
Treatment flow
Product purity (same as forward)
— mg/L
Enter feed rate + flow...
Dose (mg/L) = lbs/day × (Purity÷100) ÷ (Flow × 8.34)
(Flow × Dose × 8.34) ÷ (Purity ÷ 100)
CHEMICAL DOSING — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Converts between dose (mg/L), feed rate (lbs/day), and pump output (GPD) for any chemical — chlorine, alum, polymer, lime, caustic, ferric. Two modes: Forward (dose → lbs/day) and Reverse (lbs/day → dose).
The Core Formula
lbs/day = Flow (MGD) × Dose (mg/L) × 8.34 × Purity%/100
8.34 lbs/gallon is the weight of water — memorize this. Purity divides because you need more product than pure chemical.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• 8.34 lbs/gal is the universal conversion constant — appears on almost every exam
• Reverse calc: Dose = (lbs/day × Purity%) ÷ (Flow × 8.34)
• 100% purity = pure chemical; 12.5% bleach needs 8× more volume than equivalent pure Cl₂
• Units trap: Flow must be in MGD, not GPD or GPM
🔬 MBR – Membrane Bioreactor Calculators

16 Membrane Flux Rate DIAGRAM

Calculate permeate flux through the membrane. Primary MBR performance indicator — check daily to detect fouling trends.
InputWhere to Get It
Permeate Flow (GPD)Permeate flow meter. SCADA. Total filtrate leaving membrane tank.
Membrane Area (ft²)Equipment nameplate or manufacturer submittal. Total active membrane area installed.
Gallons per day through membranes
Total active membrane area
Post-CIP flux for % decline tracking
10–15 GFD (avg) | 20–25 GFD (peak) | <8 GFD = likely fouled | >25 GFD = risk of damage
0.0 GFD
Enter data...
Flux (GFD) = Permeate Flow (GPD) ÷ Membrane Area (ft²)
MEMBRANE FLUX — MBR OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Flux (GFD or LMH) is the throughput rate per unit of membrane area. It determines how hard the membranes are working. High flux = faster fouling. Low flux = underutilized membrane area.
Operating Ranges
Design flux: 8–12 GFD (hollow fiber), 5–8 GFD (flat sheet)
Peak flux: never exceed 1.5× design flux — irreversible fouling risk
Units: GFD = gallons/ft²/day | LMH = liters/m²/hour
Declining flux at constant TMP = fouling developing → schedule cleaning
Rising TMP at constant flux = same diagnosis — fouling
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Flux = Permeate Flow (GPD) ÷ Membrane Area (ft²)
• MBR exam questions often give flow in m³/hr and area in m² — convert to consistent units first
• Permeability Lp = Flux ÷ TMP — declining Lp is the key fouling indicator

17 Transmembrane Pressure

Calculate pressure driving permeate through the membrane. TMP trending is the #1 fouling indicator and triggers CIP decisions.
InputWhere to Get It
Feed Pressure (psi)Feed side pressure gauge or transmitter. SCADA.
Permeate Pressure (psi)Permeate side pressure gauge or transmitter. Often near 0 psi for gravity permeate.
Upstream of membrane
Downstream (often ~0 or vacuum)
Post-CIP TMP for fouling tracking
Typical MBR TMP: 1–7 psi (normal) | 7–12 psi (fouling) | >12 psi = CIP required | Submerged membranes often read in kPa (1 psi = 6.895 kPa)
0.0 psi
Enter data...
TMP = Feed Pressure − Permeate Pressure
TRANSMEMBRANE PRESSURE (TMP) — MBR GUIDE
What This Calculates
TMP is the pressure difference across the membrane from feed side to permeate side. It is THE primary operational indicator for MBR membrane health. Rising TMP = fouling. Sudden TMP spike = blocked fibers or mechanical issue.
TMP Alarm Setpoints
Normal: 0.1–0.3 bar (1.5–4.4 psi) for submerged systems
Alert: TMP increasing 0.05 bar/day → schedule maintenance clean
Critical: TMP > 0.5 bar (7.3 psi) → reduce flux immediately
Emergency: TMP > 0.7 bar (10 psi) → shut down, recovery CIP required
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• TMP = Feed Pressure − Permeate Pressure (permeate is under suction, so often negative gauge)
• Units: psi or bar or kPa — convert carefully. 1 bar = 14.5 psi = 100 kPa
• Permeability = Flux ÷ TMP — use Calc #17 to trend membrane health over time

18 Membrane Permeability

Normalizes flux per unit of driving pressure. Detects fouling even when flux appears stable — declining permeability confirms membrane fouling.
InputWhere to Get It
Flux (GFD)Output from Flux calc (#15), or: permeate flow (GPD) ÷ membrane area (ft²).
TMP (psi)Output from TMP calc (#16), or read directly from SCADA differential pressure.
From Flux calculator or known
From TMP calculator or gauge
Post-CIP K for decline tracking
Typical Permeability: 3–6 GFD/psi (good) | 1.5–3 GFD/psi (fouling) | <1.5 GFD/psi = CIP needed | New membranes: 5–8+ GFD/psi
0.0 GFD/psi
Enter data...
Permeability = Flux (GFD) ÷ TMP (psi)
MEMBRANE PERMEABILITY — MBR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Permeability (Lp) = Flux ÷ TMP. It normalizes flux AND pressure into a single number that represents membrane condition independent of operating point. Declining Lp = fouling — regardless of whether you adjusted flux or TMP.
How to Use It Operationally
Record Lp on Day 1 (new membranes). This is your baseline.
Log Lp weekly at the same operating conditions.
20% decline from baseline → maintenance cleaning due
50% decline from baseline → recovery CIP required
Lp recovers after successful CIP → cleaning was effective
Lp does NOT recover after CIP → irreversible fouling or membrane damage
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Lp = Flux (GFD or LMH) ÷ TMP (psi or bar) — units must be consistent
• Temperature affects Lp — water viscosity decreases with temp, so Lp appears higher in summer
• Correct to 20°C: Lp_20 = Lp_actual × (viscosity_T ÷ viscosity_20)

19 Membrane Area Sizing

Calculate total membrane area needed for design flow. Essential for MBR retrofits, expansions, and new system evaluations.
InputWhere to Get It
Design Flow (MGD)TPDES permit design flow or engineering design report.
Peak Hour FactorEngineering report or TCEQ 30 TAC §217. Typical 2.5–4.0 for small systems.
Target Avg Flux (GFD)Manufacturer recommendation. Submerged hollow fiber: 8–15 GFD typical.
Peak Flux (GFD)Manufacturer peak rating. Usually 1.5–2× average flux.
Average day permitted flow
Typical: 1.5–3.0×
Design: 10–14 GFD avg
Peak: 18–25 GFD
Rule of thumb: Design to avg flux for daily operation. Verify peak flux ≤ 25 GFD. Larger area = lower flux = longer membrane life.
0 ft²
Enter data...
Area = Design Flow (GPD) ÷ Target Flux (GFD) — checked against peak
MEMBRANE AREA SIZING — MBR DESIGN GUIDE
What This Calculates
Required membrane area to handle a given flow at a design flux, checked against peak flow. Used for new MBR design and capacity expansion planning.
Design Sequence
1. Set design flux (typically 8–10 GFD for hollow fiber)
2. Calculate area for average daily flow
3. Check peak flow: Area × Peak Flux ≥ Peak Flow
4. If peak flux > 1.5× design flux → add more area (cassettes)
5. Add 20% safety factor for one train out of service
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Area (ft²) = Design Flow (GPD) ÷ Design Flux (GFD)
• Always check peak flow: Peak Flux = Peak Flow ÷ Area — must be ≤ 1.5× design flux
• TCEQ requires MBR design to accommodate peak hourly flow without exceeding maximum flux

20 Specific Aeration Demand

Calculate membrane air scouring efficiency. SADm = air per membrane area. SADp = air per permeate volume. Membrane aeration is 30–50% of MBR energy cost.
InputWhere to Get It
Air Flow (Nm³/hr)Blower panel or air flow meter on scour air header. SCADA.
Membrane Area (m²)Equipment nameplate or manufacturer submittal.
Permeate Flow (m³/hr)Permeate flow meter. Convert GPM × 0.2271 for m³/hr.
Coarse bubble to membranes
Total active area
Permeate production rate
Targets: SADm = 0.2–0.5 Nm³/m²/hr (flat sheet: 0.5–1.0) | SADp = 10–25 m³air/m³permeate | Lower = more efficient
—
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
SADm = Air Flow ÷ Membrane Area | SADp = Air Flow ÷ Permeate Flow
SPECIFIC AERATION DEMAND (SAD) — MBR GUIDE
What This Calculates
SADm (air per membrane area) and SADp (air per permeate volume) measure the efficiency of membrane air scouring. They allow comparison between different systems and help optimize blower energy — the largest energy cost in an MBR.
Target Ranges
SADm: 0.2–0.5 Nm³/hr/m² membrane area (hollow fiber typical)
SADp: 10–30 Nm³ air per m³ permeate — lower is more efficient
High SADp = lots of air per unit of water produced = energy waste
Low SADm = insufficient scouring = faster TMP rise
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SADm = Air Flow (m³/hr) ÷ Membrane Area (m²)
• SADp = Air Flow (m³/hr) ÷ Permeate Flow (m³/hr)
• MBR aeration is 30–50% of total plant energy — optimizing SAD directly reduces operating cost

21 MBR Operating Range Check

Verify MLSS, SRT, F:M, and DO are within MBR-appropriate ranges. MBR operates at drastically different setpoints than conventional activated sludge.
InputWhere to Get It
MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab sample from MBR tank. MBR typically operates 8,000–12,000 mg/L.
SRT (days)Output from SRT calc (#5), or from O&M log tracking WAS history.
F:M RatioOutput from F:M calc (#4).
DO (mg/L)DO probe in MBR tank. SCADA. MBR typically needs 1.5–3.0 mg/L.
MBR typical: 8,000–12,000
MBR typical: 15–40 days
MBR typical: 0.05–0.15
MBR typical: 1.0–3.0
CAS vs MBR: CAS MLSS 1,500–4,000 → MBR 8,000–12,000 | CAS SRT 5–15 d → MBR 15–40 d | CAS F:M 0.2–0.5 → MBR 0.05–0.15
—
Enter data...
Compares inputs against MBR-specific operating ranges
MBR OPERATING RANGE CHECK — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Compares your current MLSS, SRT, F/M, and DO against MBR-specific operating targets — not conventional AS targets. MBR operates at fundamentally different setpoints than conventional activated sludge.
MBR vs Conventional AS — Key Differences
ParameterConventional ASMBR
MLSS1,500–3,5006,000–12,000 mg/L
SRT5–15 days15–30+ days
F:M0.1–0.40.05–0.15
DO1–3 mg/L> 2.0 mg/L required
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• MBR replaces the secondary clarifier with membranes — allows much higher MLSS
• High MLSS = higher viscosity = more membrane fouling potential if DO drops
• MBR effluent turbidity typically <1 NTU vs 5–20 NTU for conventional secondary

22 CIP Chemical Dosing

Calculate sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl) and citric acid volumes for membrane maintenance and recovery cleans. Wrong dosing can damage membranes.
InputWhere to Get It
CIP Tank Volume (gal)Equipment specs. CIP tank capacity. O&M manual or tank label.
Target NaOCl (mg/L)Manufacturer CIP protocol. Typically 200–500 mg/L for membrane cleaning.
Bleach Strength (%)Product label or SDS. Liquid bleach: 12.5%. Diluted solutions: check label.
Target Citric Acid (mg/L)Manufacturer protocol. Typically 2,000–5,000 mg/L for inorganic scaling.
Citric Acid Strength (%)Product label. Dry citric acid = 100%; solutions vary.
Membrane train clean volume
Maint: 200–500 | Recovery: 1000–2000
Typical: 12.5% (trade) or 10%
Maint: 1000–2000 | Recovery: 2000–5000
Powder dissolved: 50% typical
CIP Schedule: Maintenance clean (MC): weekly–biweekly | Recovery clean (RC): monthly–quarterly | Always NaOCl first, then citric acid. Never mix!
—
Enter data...
Volume = (Tank Vol × Target Conc) ÷ (Solution Strength × 10,000)
CIP CHEMICAL DOSING — MBR MAINTENANCE GUIDE
What This Calculates
Chemical volumes needed for MBR maintenance cleaning (MC) and recovery CIP. Wrong CIP concentrations either fail to restore permeability (too weak) or damage membranes (too strong). This calc prevents both.
Cleaning Protocols
Maintenance clean (weekly/biweekly): NaOCl 200–500 mg/L, soak 20–60 min
Recovery CIP (as needed): NaOCl 1,000–5,000 mg/L for organics + citric acid 1,000–5,000 mg/L for inorganic scaling
Never exceed manufacturer max concentration — membrane damage is irreversible
pH neutralize between NaOCl and citric acid — never mix them directly!
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Volume of chemical = (Tank Vol × Target Conc mg/L) ÷ (Solution Strength × 10,000)
• 12.5% NaOCl = 125,000 mg/L available Cl₂ — the denominator is the full concentration
• CIP frequency: maintenance clean monthly minimum, recovery CIP when Lp drops 50%
🧬 MBBR – Moving Bed Biofilm Reactor Calculators

23 Surface Area Loading Rate

The fundamental MBBR design parameter — equivalent of F:M for suspended growth. Calculates organic or nitrogen load applied per unit of biofilm surface area.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA daily average.
Concentration (mg/L)BOD or NH₃ composite sample result. Lab bench sheet.
Biofilm Area (m²)Media specs from manufacturer. Total active media surface area.
Influent flow to MBBR
BOD₅ or NH₃-N
Total protected surface area
SALR Targets: BOD removal: 5–20 g/m²/d | Nitrification: 0.5–1.5 g NH₃-N/m²/d | Pre-denitrification: 1–3 g NO₃-N/m²/d
0.0 g/m²/d
Enter data...
SALR = (Flow × Conc × 3.785) ÷ Biofilm Area (g/m²/day)
SURFACE AREA LOADING RATE (SALR) — MBBR GUIDE
What This Calculates
SALR (g BOD/m²/day) is the organic loading per unit of biofilm surface area — the primary design parameter for MBBR systems. Unlike suspended growth, MBBR performance depends on biofilm area, not volume.
Design Targets
BOD removal: 3–10 g BOD/m²/day (conservative 5)
Nitrification: 0.5–2.0 g NH₃-N/m²/day
SALR too high → biofilm can't process fast enough → poor effluent quality
SALR too low → over-designed, excess media cost
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SALR = (Flow m³/day × Conc g/m³) ÷ Biofilm Area m² — units critical
• Biofilm Area = Reactor Vol × Fill% × SSA (specific surface area of media)
• SSA varies by media type: 500–800 m²/m³ for AnoxKaldnes/Veolia media

24 Effective Biofilm Area

Calculate total protected biofilm surface area from reactor volume, media fill fraction, and carrier specific surface area. Needed to verify actual loading rates.
InputWhere to Get It
Reactor Volume (ft³)As-built drawings. L × W × D for rectangular. Convert to ft³.
Fill Fraction (%)Design basis from O&M manual or manufacturer. Typical 40–60% fill.
Specific Surface Area (m²/m³)Media manufacturer datasheet (SSA or specific surface). 500–800 m²/m³ typical.
Liquid volume of MBBR zone
Typical: 40–67%
From media spec sheet
Common Media SSA: K1 Kaldnes = 500 m²/m³ | K3 = 500 | K5 = 800 | AnoxKaldnes™ BiofilmChip™ = 900+ | Typical fill: 50–60%
0 m²
Enter data...
Area = Reactor Vol (m³) × Fill% × Specific Surface Area (m²/m³)
EFFECTIVE BIOFILM AREA — MBBR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Total active biofilm surface area in an MBBR reactor. This is the denominator of SALR and determines treatment capacity. More media fill % and higher SSA = more biofilm area = more treatment capacity.
Media Fill Percentage Limits
Maximum fill: 67–70% (media must have room to move freely for scouring)
Typical fill: 40–60% for aerated reactors, 30–50% for anoxic
Below 25% fill = under-designed, insufficient biofilm surface area
Above 67% = media clumps, poor mixing, hot spots, reduced effectiveness
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Biofilm Area (m²) = Vol (m³) × Fill fraction × SSA (m²/m³)
• If Vol in gallons: convert first (1 m³ = 264.2 gal)
• SSA from manufacturer data sheet — never assume; different media have 5× SSA variation

25 Media Fill Volume

Calculate required carrier media volume from target SALR, influent load, and media specific surface area. Critical for sizing new MBBR systems or expansions.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA.
Concentration (mg/L)BOD or NH₃ composite influent sample.
Target SALR (g/m²/d)Design target from O&M manual. BOD: 5–15 g/m²/d; Nitrification: 0.5–1.5 g/m²/d.
Media SSA (m²/m³)Media manufacturer datasheet.
Influent flow
BOD₅ or NH₃-N
BOD: 5–20 | NH₃: 0.5–1.5
From spec sheet
Sizing Logic: Required area = Load ÷ SALR → Media volume = Area ÷ SSA → Check fill fraction stays 40–67%
0 m³
Enter data...
Media Vol = (Flow × Conc × 3.785) ÷ (Target SALR × SSA)
MEDIA FILL VOLUME — MBBR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Volume of media (m³ or ft³) needed to achieve a target SALR at your flow and loading. Use this to size the media order for a new reactor or determine how much media to add for an upgrade.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Media Vol = (Flow × Conc × 3.785) ÷ (Target SALR × SSA)
• 3.785 converts MGD × mg/L to g/day (MGD × 3785 × mg/L = g/day)
• After finding media volume, check reactor size: Reactor Vol = Media Vol ÷ Fill%
• Result feeds directly into Calc #25 (MBBR Reactor Sizing)

26 MBBR Reactor Sizing

Calculate required reactor volume from media volume and fill fraction. Verifies HRT and ensures adequate empty-bed volume for proper media circulation.
InputWhere to Get It
Media Volume (m³)Output from Media Volume calc (#24) or design report.
Fill Fraction (%)O&M manual or design basis.
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter.
From Media Fill calc or known
Target: 40–67% max
For HRT calculation
Design Notes: Max fill 67% — above this media cannot circulate. Typical HRT: 1–3 hrs BOD removal | 3–6 hrs nitrification. Provide adequate mixing energy.
0 m³
Enter data...
Reactor Vol = Media Vol ÷ (Fill% ÷ 100)
MBBR REACTOR SIZING — DESIGN GUIDE
What This Calculates
Total reactor volume required to contain the calculated media volume at a given fill percentage. The fill% limit (67%) determines minimum reactor size.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Reactor Vol = Media Vol ÷ (Fill% ÷ 100)
• If Media Vol = 100 m³ at 50% fill → Reactor Vol = 200 m³
• MBBR HRT is typically 1–4 hours; verify: HRT = Reactor Vol ÷ Flow
• Add 10–20% freeboard to final reactor dimensions for aeration and mixing headspace

27 MBBR Oxygen Demand

Calculate O₂ transfer needed for BOD removal and nitrification in MBBR. Biofilm systems need higher DO (4–6 mg/L) than CAS (~2 mg/L) due to boundary layer diffusion.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter.
BOD Removed (mg/L)Influent BOD minus effluent BOD. Lab results.
NH₃-N Removed (mg/L)Influent NH₃ minus effluent NH₃. Lab results.
Influent flow
Influent – Effluent BOD
0 if no nitrification
O₂ Factors: ~1.5 lb O₂/lb BOD removed | ~4.6 lb O₂/lb NH₃-N oxidized | MBBR target DO: 4–6 mg/L (vs CAS 1.5–2.5 mg/L)
0 lbs O₂/day
Enter data...
O₂ = (Flow × ΔBOD × 8.34 × 1.5) + (Flow × ΔNH₃ × 8.34 × 4.6)
MBBR OXYGEN DEMAND — AERATION GUIDE
What This Calculates
Total O₂ demand for an MBBR reactor combining carbonaceous BOD removal (1.5 lbs O₂/lb BOD) and nitrification (4.6 lbs O₂/lb NH₃-N). Feeds directly into blower sizing.
Oxygen Demand Factors
BOD removal: 1.5 lbs O₂ per lb BOD removed (includes endogenous respiration)
Nitrification: 4.57 lbs O₂ per lb NH₃-N oxidized (theoretical)
Combined: both run simultaneously in aerobic MBBR
Apply a 1.5–2.0× safety factor and SOTR→AOTR correction for final blower sizing
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• O₂ demand (lbs/day) = (Flow × BOD × 8.34 × 1.5) + (Flow × NH₃ × 8.34 × 4.6)
• 4.6 vs 4.57: exam typically uses 4.6 for nitrification oxygen demand
• Result feeds into Calc #62 (SOTR→AOTR) → then Calc #68 (Blower Sizing)

28 MBBR vs CAS Comparison

Side-by-side comparison of MBBR vs conventional activated sludge for the same flow and load. Useful for technology selection and consulting evaluations.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter.
Influent BOD (mg/L)24-hr composite influent sample. Lab bench sheet.
Design flow
Raw or primary effluent BOD
Comparison basis: CAS: F:M=0.3, MLSS=3000, SRT=10d | MBBR: SALR=10 g/m²/d, K3 media 500 m²/m³, 55% fill
—
Enter data...
CAS Vol = (Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ (F:M × MLSS) | MBBR Vol = Media ÷ Fill%
MBBR vs CAS COMPARISON — DECISION GUIDE
What This Calculates
Side-by-side comparison of reactor volume needed for conventional activated sludge vs MBBR to treat the same flow at the same BOD. Used for technology selection and plant upgrade analysis.
Typical Results
MBBR typically requires 30–60% less reactor volume than conventional CAS for same BOD load.
Trade-off: MBBR has higher media cost, but no clarifier/RAS/WAS system needed for some configurations.
MBBR is ideal for retrofitting existing basins — same footprint, higher capacity.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• CAS volume: V = (Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ (F:M × MLSS × 8.34) → 8.34 cancels
• MBBR volume: find media volume from SALR, then divide by fill%
• Key advantage: MBBR can nitrify in 1–2 hr HRT vs 6–8 hr for CAS

NEW MBBR Surface Area Loading (All-in-One)

Comprehensive MBBR/IFAS calculator. Enter reactor parameters, select media type, and get both BOD and ammonia SAR with status indicators.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA.
Influent BOD (mg/L)24-hr composite influent BOD₅. Lab bench sheet.
Influent NH₃-N (mg/L)24-hr composite influent sample. Lab result.
Reactor Volume (gal)As-built drawings. Convert ft³ × 7.48 for gallons.
Media Fill (%)Design basis. O&M manual or startup report.
Media SSA (m²/m³)Manufacturer datasheet for the specific media type installed.
Optional - for nitrification
Typical: 40-70%
Specific Surface Area from manufacturer
Typical SAR Ranges:
• BOD Removal: 5-25 g BOD/m²/d
• Nitrification: 0.5-1.5 g NH₃-N/m²/d
• Combined BOD+Nitrification: 3-8 g BOD/m²/d
Media Volume: —
Total Surface Area: —
BOD Load: —
NH₃ Load: —
BOD SAR
—
g BOD/m²/d
NH₃ SAR
—
g NH₃-N/m²/d
Enter reactor parameters...
📊 MBBR Design Tips
• DO Requirement: Maintain 3-5 mg/L bulk liquid DO (higher than AS)
• Mixing: Air provides both O₂ and keeps media in suspension
• Media Inspection: Healthy biofilm = thin tan/brown coating
• HRT: Typically 2-6 hours (shorter than suspended growth)
• Temperature: Nitrification SAR decreases ~50% from 20°C to 10°C
SAR = Mass Load (g/d) ÷ Surface Area (m²)
Surface Area = Reactor Vol × Fill% × SSA
Mass Load = Flow × Conc × 3.785 (MGD to kg/d)
⏱️ SBR – Sequencing Batch Reactor Calculators

30 SBR Cycle Time DIAGRAM

Calculate total cycle time, cycles per day, and effective treatment capacity. Every SBR process decision flows from cycle timing.
InputWhere to Get It
Fill (min)SBR controller/timer setpoint. PLC program or timer panel. O&M manual cycle table.
React (min)SBR controller setpoint. Aeration timer duration.
Settle (min)SBR controller setpoint. Typically 30–60 min minimum.
Decant (min)SBR controller setpoint. Decanter arm cycle duration.
Idle (min)SBR controller setpoint. Often 0 if plant is flow-limited.
Design Flow (MGD)TPDES permit design flow. Equipment nameplates and O&M manual.
SBR CYCLE TIME — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
An SBR operates in batch cycles: Fill → React (Aerate) → Settle → Decant → Idle. This calculator adds up all phase times to get your total cycle, then tells you how many cycles per day and whether your basin can handle the design flow.
Inputs Explained
Fill Time — How long to fill the basin. Static fill (no aeration) = stronger batch effect. Most plants: 1–2 hours.
React/Aerate Time — Active aeration period. This is where BOD removal and nitrification happen. Typically 2–4 hours. Longer = better treatment but fewer cycles.
Settle Time — Quiescent settling (no mixing or aeration). Typically 0.5–1.5 hours. Poor settling sludge needs more time.
Decant Time — Time to withdraw clear supernatant. Depends on decanter capacity. Typically 0.5–1 hour.
Idle Time — Buffer between cycles. Used for WAS, equalization, or waiting for the other basin to finish. Can be 0 if cycles are tight.
How to Read Results
Cycles/day = 24 hrs ÷ total cycle time. More cycles = more daily capacity. Typical: 4–6 cycles/day.
Aeration duty cycle = % of time blowers run. Used for energy cost estimates and blower sizing.
If cycles/day × volume/cycle < your design flow, you need to shorten cycles or add a basin.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• Total cycle = Fill + React + Settle + Decant + Idle
• Cycles/day = 24 ÷ Total Cycle (hrs)
• If they ask "how many gallons can the SBR treat per day" → cycles/day × volume per cycle
• SBR = activated sludge without a separate clarifier
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Fill (min)SBR controller/timer setpoint. PLC program or timer panel. O&M manual cycle table.
React (min)SBR controller setpoint. Aeration timer duration.
Settle (min)SBR controller setpoint. Required by design — typically 30–60 min minimum.
Decant (min)SBR controller setpoint. Decanter arm cycle duration.
Idle (min)SBR controller setpoint. Often 0 if plant is flow-limited.
Design Flow (MGD)Your TPDES permit design flow. Also on equipment nameplates and O&M manual.
Static or aerated fill
Aeration + mixing
Quiescent settling
Effluent withdrawal
Buffer / WAS (can be 0)
For capacity check
Typical Cycles: 4–6 hrs total | 4–6 cycles/day | Fill 25–30% of cycle | React 30–40% | Settle 20–25% | Decant 15–20%
—
Enter data...
Cycle = Fill + React + Settle + Decant + Idle | Cycles/day = 1440 ÷ Cycle
SBR CYCLE TIME — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Total time for one complete SBR cycle (Fill + React + Settle + Decant + Idle) and the resulting number of cycles per day. Directly determines treatment capacity and hydraulic loading.
Typical Phase Durations
Fill: 60–90 min (simultaneous react often runs during fill)
React: 120–240 min (main biological treatment)
Settle: 30–60 min (must be adequate for blanket to drop below decant arm)
Decant: 30–60 min (effluent withdrawal)
Idle: 0–30 min (equalization between cycles, optional)
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Cycles/day = 1440 min ÷ total cycle time (min)
• SBR exam problems often ask: given X cycles/day and Y basin volume, what daily flow can be treated?
• Minimum settle time check: blanket must drop at least 12–18 inches below decant arm

31 SBR Volume per Cycle

Calculate fill volume per cycle and verify reactor can handle the hydraulic load. Determines decant volume and checks against reactor capacity.
InputWhere to Get It
Daily Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter daily average. SCADA.
Cycles per DaySBR controller timer settings. PLC program or O&M manual.
Reactor Volume (gal)As-built drawings. Convert ft³ × 7.48.
# of BasinsCount from site plan. Only count basins in active service.
Average daily flow
From Cycle Time calc
Total basin volume
Parallel SBR basins
Design Rule: Decant volume (fill vol) should be 15–35% of total reactor volume. Freeboard must remain above max water level at all times.
—
Enter data...
Vol/Cycle = Daily Flow ÷ (Cycles × Basins) | Fill Ratio = Vol/Cycle ÷ Reactor Vol
SBR VOLUME PER CYCLE — DESIGN GUIDE
What This Calculates
Fill volume per cycle (how much influent enters each cycle) and the fill ratio (fill volume ÷ reactor volume). Fill ratio controls dilution of existing MLSS and determines F/M per cycle.
Fill Ratio Targets
Fill ratio 0.3–0.5 (30–50%): typical operating range
Below 0.25: too conservative — underutilizes reactor, high capital cost
Above 0.6: too aggressive — high F/M per cycle, poor effluent, sludge loss risk during decant
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Fill Vol = Daily Flow ÷ (Cycles/day × Basins)
• Fill Ratio = Fill Vol ÷ Reactor Vol — express as decimal or percent
• Fill ratio × MLSS × 8.34 × Reactor Vol = lbs MLSS remaining after fill (dilution calc)

32 SBR Decant Rate

Calculate decant withdrawal rate. Too fast = turbulence pulls solids into effluent. Too slow = steals time from react phase.
InputWhere to Get It
Decant Volume (gal)SBR design: daily flow ÷ cycles/day. Or from SBR Volume calc (#29).
Decant Time (min)SBR controller timer setpoint. O&M manual cycle table.
Weir Length (ft)As-built drawings or decanter equipment specs.
Volume removed per cycle
Available decant period
Decanter weir length
Decant Limits: Typical rate: 1,000–4,000 GPM total | Weir loading: <10 GPM/ft preferred | Floating decanters adjust with water level
0 GPM
Enter data...
Rate (GPM) = Decant Vol ÷ Decant Time | Weir Rate = GPM ÷ Weir Length
SBR DECANT RATE — HYDRAULIC CHECK
What This Calculates
Decant flow rate (GPM) and weir overflow rate. The decant arm or floating weir must withdraw effluent fast enough to finish in the allotted decant phase without disturbing the settled sludge blanket.
Key Limits
Decant rate: must finish fill volume withdrawal in decant time
Weir rate: < 15,000–20,000 GPD/ft to avoid turbulence disturbing settled sludge
Decant velocity: must be slow enough to prevent rising currents carrying sludge to effluent
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Decant Rate (GPM) = Decant Vol (gal) ÷ Decant Time (min)
• Weir Rate (GPD/ft) = Decant Rate (GPD) ÷ Weir Length (ft)
• Same weir rate formula as Calc #12 (WOR) — the concept applies to SBR decant weirs too

33 SBR Aeration Demand

Calculate O₂ required during react phase. In SBR, aeration only runs during fill+react — blowers must deliver the full daily O₂ demand in less time than continuous-flow plants.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter.
BOD Removed (mg/L)Influent BOD minus target effluent BOD. Lab results and permit.
NH₃-N Removed (mg/L)Influent NH₃ minus target effluent NH₃. Lab and permit.
