Aeration Duty (%)SBR cycle: aeration time ÷ total cycle time × 100. From controller settings.
Daily flow
Influent – Effluent BOD
0 if no nitrification
(Fill + React) ÷ Total Cycle × 100
SBR Aeration: If cycle = 4 hrs and aerated phases = 2 hrs → 50% duty. Blower must deliver 2× the continuous rate. Size blowers for peak duty, not daily average.
—
Enter data...
Daily O₂ = (BOD × 1.5 + NH₃ × 4.6) × Flow × 8.34 | Peak = Daily ÷ Duty%
SBR AERATION DEMAND — BLOWER SIZING
What This Calculates
Daily O₂ demand for an SBR and the peak aeration rate during the react phase. SBR aeration is cyclic — blowers must supply the full daily O₂ demand in only the aeration fraction of the cycle.
Peak vs Average Demand
Daily O₂ demand is the same as a continuous system.
But SBRs only aerate during Fill + React phases (typically 40–60% of cycle).
Peak aeration rate = Daily O₂ ÷ (Duty% × 24 hr) — must size blowers for this higher rate.
A 50% duty cycle means blowers deliver twice the continuous rate during aeration.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Daily O₂ (lbs) = (BOD × 1.5 + NH₃ × 4.6) × Flow × 8.34
• Peak Rate = Daily O₂ ÷ (Duty fraction × 24)
• Duty% = (Fill + React) ÷ Total Cycle × 100 — typical 40–65%

34 SBR F:M per Cycle

SBR F:M differs from CAS — the food slug arrives all at once during fill, creating a high instantaneous F:M that drops to near-zero by end of react.
InputWhere to Get It
Fill Volume (gal)SBR Volume calc (#29) or SCADA fill volume setpoint.
Influent BOD (mg/L)24-hr composite influent lab result.
Reactor Volume (gal)As-built drawings.
MLVSS (mg/L)Lab result. MLVSS ≈ MLSS × 0.75–0.85 if no direct VSS test.
Cycles per DaySBR controller setpoints. PLC or O&M manual.
Volume added per cycle
Incoming BOD concentration
Total basin volume
Volatile suspended solids
For daily F:M
SBR F:M: Daily avg: 0.05–0.15 typical | Instantaneous at start of fill is much higher | CAS equivalent would be 0.2–0.5
—
Enter data...
Daily F:M = (Fill × BOD × 8.34 × Cycles) ÷ (Reactor × MLVSS × 8.34)
SBR F:M PER CYCLE — PROCESS CONTROL
What This Calculates
Food-to-Microorganism ratio for an SBR on a per-cycle basis. SBR F/M is fundamentally different from continuous flow because food is added in batches — the instantaneous F/M at fill is much higher than the daily average.
SBR F/M Interpretation
SBR F/M is typically expressed as daily value (same formula as continuous AS).
Target: 0.05–0.20 lbs BOD/lb MLVSS/day (similar to extended aeration)
High fill ratio + high influent BOD = high instantaneous F/M = stress on sludge
Use this calc alongside Calc #29 (Fill Ratio) to ensure fill volume doesn't create a BOD spike.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Daily F:M = (Fill Vol × BOD × 8.34 × Cycles/day) ÷ (Reactor Vol × MLVSS × 8.34)
• 8.34 cancels if same units — simplifies to (Fill × BOD × Cycles) ÷ (Reactor × MLVSS)
• SBR responds faster to WAS adjustments than continuous flow — smaller system volume

35 SBR Settle & Decant Check

Verify adequate separation before decant begins. Flags risk of solids carryover if sludge blanket is too close to decant withdrawal level.
InputWhere to Get It
Total Water Depth (ft)As-built drawings or staff gauge reading at end of react phase.
Settled Sludge (%)Visual observation after settle phase, or calculated from MLSS and SVI.
Decant Depth (ft)Design: decant volume ÷ (basin area). Or decanter arm setting.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab sample.
Max water level at end of fill
30-min settleometer (% of total)
Water removed during decant
Mixed liquor concentration
Safety Rule: Sludge blanket must be ≥2 ft below decant weir at start of decant. Blanket depth = Water depth × Settled%. Good settling = <30% in 30 min.
—
Enter data...
Blanket = Depth × Settle% | Clearwater = Depth − Blanket | Buffer = Clearwater − Decant

36 SBR Timing Optimizer

Optimize SBR cycle timing: given daily flow, basin volume, number of basins, and minimum settle/decant time — find the maximum cycles/day and minimum fill volume that processes all flow. Outputs a complete 24-hour cycle schedule. Critical for permit compliance — settle+decant must be long enough to prevent TSS carryover.
SBR TIMING OPTIMIZER — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Given your daily flow and basin volume, this optimizer finds the minimum cycle time that processes all flow across all basins, with the constraint that settle + decant must meet your minimum time. It then builds an optimized 24-hour schedule showing each cycle phase.
SBR Cycle Phase Sequence
Fill — Influent enters. Aeration may be on or off depending on selector zone design.
React — Aeration on. BOD removal, nitrification, possibly denitrification.
Settle — All mixing/aeration off. Solids settle. Minimum 30–60 min required.
Decant — Effluent withdrawn from top. Surface skimmer or floating decanter. Too fast = turbulence and TSS carryover.
Idle/Waste — Optional WAS pump-down between cycles.
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#28 SBR Cycle TimeOriginal SBR cycle calc — input-specified phases. This calc is the optimizer version: finds the optimal phases given your design constraints.
#29 SBR VolumeVerifies the fill volume per cycle doesn't overflow the basin. Cross-check this calc's fill volume against #29.
#32 SBR F:MUse fill volume from this calc in #32 to verify F:M ratio per cycle is in the design range (0.04–0.12 for nutrient removal).
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SBR treats flow sequentially in time, not space — one basin = one "train" of treatment
• Minimum settle time = 30 min (TCEQ guidance); 45–60 min preferred
• Fill volume = daily flow ÷ (basins × cycles/day)
• React time drives BOD removal and nitrification — too short = effluent violations
• SBR F:M calculated per fill event: F:M = BOD_fill_lbs ÷ MLVSS_basin_lbs
InputWhere to Get It
Average Daily Flow (MGD)SCADA daily average or flow recorder. Use the highest flow day for design — not average. Include peaking factor if designing for new conditions.
Basin Volume (gallons each)From as-built drawings. Working volume = total volume minus freeboard. Typically 80–85% of total tank volume.
Number of BasinsOperating basins count. Offline or standby basins should not be included unless they are actively in the rotation.
Min Settle + Decant Time (min)Minimum time for particles to settle and decant to complete without turbulence. TCEQ guidance: 30 min minimum settle + 20–30 min decant = 50–60 min total. Your permit or O&M manual may specify longer.
React Time per Cycle (min)Design or current react (aeration) time. Nitrification typically needs 90–150 min. BOD removal alone: 60–90 min. Adjust as needed to meet permit.
Combined settle + decant time
Aeration + mixing time
Cycle time = Fill + React + Settle + Decant | Cycles/day = 1440 ÷ cycle_min | Fill vol = daily flow ÷ (basins × cycles/day)
—
Enter flow, basin volume, and timing...
Cycles/day = 1440 ÷ (fill + react + settle + decant) | Fill vol (MG) = flow ÷ (basins × cycles/day) | Fill time = fill_vol ÷ avg_inflow_rate
🧪 Disinfection Calculators

37 Chlorine CT Value

Calculate CT value for disinfection compliance. CT = chlorine residual × contact time. TCEQ requires specific CT for pathogen inactivation.
InputWhere to Get It
Cl₂ Residual (mg/L)Colorimetric test (DPD method) at end of contact chamber. Log sheet or SCADA.
Contact Time (min)Contact chamber volume ÷ flow rate. Or T10 baffling factor from tracer study.
Required CT (mg·min/L)TCEQ permit or 30 TAC §217. Typical: 450 for Giardia at pH <8.
CHLORINE CT VALUE — OPERATOR GUIDE
What is CT?
CT is the product of disinfectant concentration (C, in mg/L) multiplied by contact time (T, in minutes). It measures total disinfection "dose" — a higher CT means more pathogen kill. Your permit requires a minimum CT to ensure adequate disinfection before discharge.
Inputs Explained
Cl₂ Residual (mg/L) — Measured at the end of the contact chamber, not at the dosing point. Use your grab sample or continuous analyzer reading.
Contact Time (min) — Use T10, NOT theoretical detention time. T10 = volume ÷ flow × baffling factor (BF). Serpentine channels: BF = 0.7. Plug flow pipe: BF = 1.0. Open basin: BF = 0.1–0.3.
Required CT — From your permit or TCEQ guidance. Depends on target pathogen, temperature, and pH. Cold water + high pH = need higher CT.
Critical Operator Notes
• T10 matters: A 10,000-gal tank at 100 GPM has 100 min theoretical detention — but with BF=0.3, T10 is only 30 min
• Temperature effect: CT requirements roughly double for every 10°C drop in water temperature
• pH effect: Free chlorine is much more effective at pH < 7.5. Hypochlorous acid (HOCl) dominates below pH 7.5
• Don't over-chlorinate: High residual = high CT but also high dechlorination cost and aquatic toxicity risk
• TCEQ exam: CT = C × T. They often give you volume and flow — you must calculate T first (T = Vol ÷ Flow, convert to minutes)
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Cl₂ Residual (mg/L)Colorimetric test (DPD method) at end of contact chamber. Log sheet or SCADA.
Contact Time (min)Contact chamber volume ÷ flow rate. Or use T10 baffling factor from tracer study. O&M manual.
Required CT (mg·min/L)TCEQ permit or 30 TAC §217. Typical: 450 for Giardia at pH <8. Table in O&M manual.
At end of contact chamber
T₁₀ actual contact time
From permit or tables
Vol ÷ flow — for BF calc
0.1=poor, 0.5=baffled, 0.7=plug
BF quick-set:
Common CT Requirements: 2-log virus: 6 mg·min/L (free Cl₂) | 3-log Giardia: 104–150+ depending on temp/pH | TCEQ Surface Water: 450+ typical
0.0 mg·min/L
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
CT = Residual (mg/L) × Contact Time (min)
CHLORINE CT VALUE — DISINFECTION GUIDE
What This Calculates
CT = Concentration (mg/L) × Contact Time (min). The fundamental metric for disinfection effectiveness. TCEQ and EPA require specific CT values to achieve log reductions of target pathogens (Giardia, Cryptosporidium, viruses).
Required CT Values (Cl₂, pH 7, 15°C)
TargetLog ReductionCT Required
Giardia3-log (99.9%)~63 mg·min/L
Viruses4-log (99.99%)~6 mg·min/L
Crypto2-logUV preferred (Cl₂ ineffective)
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• CT = Cl₂ Residual (mg/L) × T10 (min), NOT total HRT
• T10 = Theoretical DT × Baffling Factor (BF). BF: poor=0.3, average=0.5, superior=0.7
• Lower pH, higher temp, higher Cl₂ residual → less contact time needed for same CT
• Wastewater disinfection permit limit: typically 200 fecal coliform/100 mL — not CT-based

38 Dechlorination Dosing

Calculate sodium bisulfite (NaHSO₃) or sulfur dioxide needed to neutralize chlorine residual before discharge. Required at most Texas plants.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Effluent flow meter. SCADA.
Cl₂ Residual (mg/L)Online chlorine analyzer or DPD test kit at dechlorination point.
Safety FactorYour operational choice. Typically 1.5–2.0 to account for residual variation.
Effluent flow
Before dechlorination
Typical: 1.1–1.5×
Stoichiometry: 1.0 mg SO₂ per 1.0 mg Cl₂ | 1.46 mg NaHSO₃ per 1.0 mg Cl₂ | 0.9 mg Na₂SO₃ per 1.0 mg Cl₂
—
Enter data...
SO₂ lbs/day = Flow × Cl₂ × 8.34 × 1.0 × SF | NaHSO₃ = × 1.46
DECHLORINATION DOSING — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Chemical dose required to neutralize chlorine residual before discharge to receiving stream. Over-dosing is as harmful as under-dosing — excess dechlorination chemical consumes dissolved oxygen in the receiving stream.
Dechlorination Ratios
ChemicalRatio to Cl₂Notes
SO₂ gas0.9:1Most common at large plants
Sodium bisulfite (NaHSO₃)1.46:1Liquid, easier to handle
Sodium thiosulfateLab use onlyNot for field dechlorination
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• SO₂ dose (mg/L) = Cl₂ residual (mg/L) × 1.0 (use 0.9 for pure stoichiometry, 1.0 practice)
• NaHSO₃ dose (mg/L) = Cl₂ residual × 1.46 — memorize 1.46
• Over-dechlorination depletes DO in receiving stream — a permit violation even if Cl₂ = 0
• Safety factor >1.0 provides margin but increases overdose risk — balance carefully

39 UV Dose

Calculate UV dose from intensity and exposure time. Verify adequate disinfection for permit compliance. UV increasingly replacing chlorine in Texas.
InputWhere to Get It
UV Intensity (mW/cm²)UV system control panel or SCADA. Measured by UV sensors in the reactor.
Exposure Time (sec)Contact chamber volume ÷ flow rate. Or from UV system engineer report.
UV Transmittance (%)Online UVT analyzer or bench-top spectrophotometer at 254 nm. Daily reading.
Measured by UV sensor
Chamber volume ÷ flow rate
Lab UVT result
Required Doses: E. coli/fecal: 30–40 mJ/cm² | TCEQ typical: 30+ mJ/cm² | Reuse water: 80–100+ mJ/cm² | Good UVT: >65%
0.0 mJ/cm²
Enter data...
Dose (mJ/cm²) = Intensity (mW/cm²) × Time (sec)

40 Hypochlorite Dosing (Cal-Hypo & Bleach) DIAGRAM

Calculate calcium hypochlorite or liquid bleach dosing for disinfection or RAS chlorination.
InputWhere to Get It
ApplicationVisual inspection or O&M manual — disinfection, RAS chlorination, etc.
Chemical TypeProduct label. Liquid NaOCl (12.5%) or calcium hypochlorite (65%).
% Available ChlorineProduct SDS or label. Verify with bulk delivery cert of analysis.
Flow (MGD)Flow meter SCADA reading at point of application.
Dose Target (mg/L)Permit CT requirement or operational target. Start with jar test.
Select application type
Cal-hypo or liquid bleach
Liquid: 10-12.5% | Cal-hypo: 65-70%
Treatment flow rate
Effluent: 2-10 mg/L typical
12.5% bleach ≈ 1.16 SG
Days since delivery — NaOCl degrades ~2%/month at 70°F
Higher temp accelerates degradation — 95°F doubles loss rate
🦠 RAS Chlorination for Filament Control
Mixed liquor concentration
Total aeration basin volume
Typical: 2-10 lb Cl₂/1000 lb MLSS/day
Typical % Available Chlorine:
• Sodium Hypochlorite (liquid bleach): 10-12.5% (trade), 5.25% (household)
• Calcium Hypochlorite (granular): 65-70%
RAS Chlorination: 2-10 lb Cl₂ per 1000 lb MLSS per day to control filaments
—
Enter flow, dose, and chemical strength...
lbs Cl₂/day = Flow (MGD) × Dose (mg/L) × 8.34 | Chemical lbs = Cl₂ lbs ÷ (% avail/100) | Gallons = lbs ÷ (8.34 × SG)
HYPOCHLORITE DOSING — OPERATOR GUIDE
Effluent Disinfection
1. Determine required CT value from permit
2. Set target residual (typically 0.5-2.0 mg/L)
3. Account for chlorine demand (dose > residual)
4. Typical dose: 5-15 mg/L depending on demand
RAS Chlorination (Filament Control)
• Dose: 2-10 lb Cl₂ per 1000 lb MLSS per day
• Start low (2-3 lb), increase if SVI doesn't improve
• Apply to RAS line before return to aeration
• Monitor SVI weekly — should drop within 2-4 weeks
TCEQ Exam Tips
• Chlorinate RAS 2-10 lb Cl₂ per 1000 lb MLSS/day to kill filaments
• 12.5% bleach has ~1.25 lb Cl₂ per gallon
• 65% cal-hypo: need 1.54 lb product per lb Cl₂
• Always calculate as Cl₂ first, then convert to product
📋 Where to Get Each Input
InputWhere to Find It
ApplicationVisual inspection or O&M manual — disinfection, dechlorination, RAS chlorination.
Chemical TypeProduct label. Liquid sodium hypochlorite (12.5%) or calcium hypochlorite (65%).
% Available ChlorineProduct SDS or label. Verify with bulk delivery certification of analysis.
Flow (MGD)Flow meter SCADA reading at point of application.
Dose Target (mg/L)Permit CT requirement or operational target. Start with jar test or pilot dosing.

41 Chlorine Residual Decay Model

First-order chlorine decay model. Calculates residual at any contact time, minimum CT for compliance, and time to reach permit discharge limit. Use for contact chamber sizing and reclaimed water distribution.
InputWhere to Get It
Initial Cl₂ Dose (mg/L)Chemical feed pump output calc (#14) or flow-paced controller setpoint.
Decay Constant k (hr⁻¹)Measure by taking timed residual samples, or from bench decay test. Typically 0.1–1.0 hr⁻¹.
Contact Time (min)Chamber volume ÷ flow rate. Or CT value calc (#34).
Min Permit Residual (mg/L)TPDES permit effluent limit for chlorine residual.
C₀, C₁, C₂ (decay fit)Lab: take 3 grab samples at timed intervals, measure residual each time with DPD.
Applied chlorine concentration
Wastewater: 0.3–1.5 | Clean water: 0.05–0.2
HRT of contact chamber
Typically 0.1–0.5 mg/L
Enter two known data points (time + residual) to back-calculate k for your water.
—
Enter C₀ and at least one data point...
Given dose and permit residual requirement, calculates minimum contact time needed to maintain compliance.
Dechlorination target if too high
—
Enter dose, k, and required residual...
First-Order Decay: C(t) = C₀ × e^(−k×t/60) | k in hr⁻¹, t in minutes
—
Enter dose, k, and contact time...
C(t) = C₀ × e^(−k×t/60) | Half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/k × 60 min | CT = C_avg × t

42 Chlorine Demand Calculator

Calculate chlorine demand (what the water consumes before residual forms), required dose to achieve a target residual, and track demand trends over time. Chlorine demand = dose − residual. Increasing demand signals increasing organic load, ammonia, or industrial contributors.
CHLORINE DEMAND CALCULATOR — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Three related chlorine calculations in one card: (1) demand from measured dose and residual, (2) required dose to achieve a target residual given known demand, and (3) feed rate in lbs/day for a given dose and flow. A rising demand trend is an early warning of process or loading changes.
The Three Components of Chlorine Demand
Immediate demand: Reacts with reducing compounds (H₂S, Fe²⁺, NO₂⁻) — occurs in seconds. Cannot be overcome by dosing faster.

Nitrogenous demand: Cl₂ + NH₃ → chloramines. Rate: 7.6 mg Cl₂ per mg NH₃-N. If effluent NH₃ > 1 mg/L, most of your dose goes here before free residual forms.

Carbonaceous demand: Reaction with organic compounds. Proportional to effluent BOD/COD. High-BOD effluent has high chlorine demand.
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#34 CT ValueTakes the residual output from this calc → computes CT for permit compliance. Residual × T10 = CT.
#35 DechlorinationTakes the residual from this calc to size dechlorination dose. Always run #35 after adjusting chlorine dose.
#98 Cl₂ DecayModels residual decay over time/distance using the rate constant k. Use when contact chamber is long or when dosing upstream of a long force main.
#14 Chemical DosingConverts mg/L dose from this calc to lbs/day product using purity. Cross-check feed rate calculation.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Demand = dose − residual. Residual = dose − demand. Dose = demand + target residual
• Nitrogenous demand = 7.6 mg Cl₂ per mg NH₃-N — if effluent has 2 mg/L NH₃, demand is 15.2 mg/L before free Cl₂ forms
• Breakpoint chlorination: all demand satisfied + chloramines destroyed = free Cl₂ residual forms
• Free vs combined residual: free (HOCl, OCl⁻) is stronger disinfectant than combined (chloramines)
InputWhere to Get It
Cl₂ Dose Applied (mg/L)Chemical feed pump output converted to mg/L using Calc #14 (Chemical Dosing). Or read from chlorine analyzer at the injection point if available. Must be measured at the injection point, not downstream.
Cl₂ Residual Measured (mg/L)DPD colorimetric test at the end of the contact chamber, or chlorine analyzer reading. Timing matters: measure at the design contact time, not immediately after injection.
Flow (MGD)Influent or effluent flow meter daily average. SCADA or daily operator log.
Target Residual (mg/L) — optionalYour desired residual at end of contact time. Typical: 0.5–1.0 mg/L. From permit or O&M manual.
NH₃-N in Effluent (mg/L) — optionalLab result from effluent sample before disinfection. Used to estimate nitrogenous chlorine demand separately. From composite sample or grab.
Applied at injection point
At end of contact chamber
Treatment flow
For required dose calc
To split nitrogenous demand
Demand = Dose − Residual | Required Dose = Demand + Target Residual | Feed Rate = Flow × Dose × 8.34
—
Enter dose and residual...
Cl₂ Demand (mg/L) = Dose − Residual | NH₃ demand = NH₃-N × 7.6 | Feed rate (lbs/day) = Flow(MGD) × Dose(mg/L) × 8.34
💩 Sludge & Biosolids Calculators

43 Sludge Volume & % Solids

Convert between wet volume, dry weight, and percent solids. Used daily for hauling, land application, and biosolids reporting.
InputWhere to Get It
Sludge Volume (gal/day)WAS pump log: runtime × GPD rating. Or WAS flow meter.
% SolidsLab total solids test on WAS sample. TS result ÷ 10,000 for %.
Wet sludge pumped
Lab result or estimate
Typical: WAS 0.5–1.5% | Thickened WAS 3–6% | Belt press cake 15–25% | Centrifuge cake 20–30% | 1 gal sludge ≈ 8.34 lbs
—
Enter data...
Dry lbs = Volume × 8.34 × (%Solids ÷ 100) | Dry tons = Dry lbs ÷ 2000

44 Volatile Solids Reduction

Calculate VS destruction in digesters using Van Kleeck equation. 38% minimum required for Class B biosolids (vector attraction reduction).
InputWhere to Get It
Influent VS (%)Lab: volatile solids test on raw sludge feed. (TS - FS) ÷ TS × 100.
Effluent VS (%)Lab: volatile solids test on digested sludge.
Raw sludge volatile fraction
Digested sludge volatile fraction
Van Kleeck: VSR = (VSin − VSout) ÷ (VSin − (VSin × VSout)) × 100 | Class B minimum: 38% | Healthy digester: 50–60%
0.0 %
Enter data...
VSR = (VSin − VSout) ÷ [VSin − (VSin × VSout)] × 100

45 Digester Loading Rate

Calculate volatile solids loading to anaerobic digester. Overloading causes pH drop, VFA buildup, and digester upset — common and costly operator mistake.
InputWhere to Get It
Sludge Flow (GPD)WAS pump log or WAS flow meter. Daily sludge feed to digester.
% SolidsLab total solids on digester feed sludge.
% VolatileLab volatile solids test on digester feed. (TS-FS)/TS × 100.
Digester Volume (ft³)As-built drawings. π/4 × D² × depth for cylindrical digesters.
Volume fed to digester
Feed sludge solids
VS as % of TS
Active digester volume
Loading Limits: Low-rate: 0.03–0.05 lbs VS/ft³/d | High-rate: 0.10–0.20 lbs VS/ft³/d | Overloaded: >0.20
0.00 lbs VS/ft³/d
Enter data...
Loading = (Flow × 8.34 × %Solids × %VS) ÷ Digester Volume

46 Digester Gas Production DIAGRAM

Estimate biogas production from anaerobic digester based on VS destroyed. Used for sizing gas handling, energy recovery, and flare capacity.
InputWhere to Get It
Sludge Flow (GPD)WAS pump log or flow meter. Daily feed to digester.
% SolidsLab total solids on digester feed.
% Volatile SolidsLab volatile solids on digester feed.
VS Destruction (%)Lab comparison: influent VS% vs. effluent VS%. Typical 50–60% for anaerobic.
Gas Yield (ft³/lb VS)Literature: 10–16 ft³/lb VS destroyed. O&M manual or design report.
Methane Content (%)Gas analyzer or design assumption. Typical 60–65% CH₄.
Feed to digester
Feed sludge concentration
VS as % of TS
Typical 50-60%
Typical 12-18 ft³/lb
Typical 60-70%
Biogas Composition: 60-70% CH₄ (methane), 30-40% CO₂ | ~600 BTU/ft³ | 1 ft³ CH₄ ≈ 1,000 BTU | Flare or energy recovery
—
Enter data...
Gas = VS destroyed (lbs/day) × Gas Yield (ft³/lb)
⚡ ENERGY RECOVERY ANALYSIS
Enter your plant's annual electricity consumption to see biogas offset potential.
From utility bill
Annual consumption
CHP: 28–35% electric
—
Enter $/kWh and engine efficiency (run main calc first)...
CH₄ kWh potential = CH₄ ft³/day × 0.3412 kWh/ft³ × engine eff% | Annual $ = kWh/yr × $/kWh | Heat recovery ≈ 2× electric output for CHP
See also: #39 VS Reduction · #40 Digester Loading

47 Polymer Dosing

Calculate active polymer per dry ton of solids for belt press or centrifuge. Polymer is one of the biggest chemical costs at any plant.
InputWhere to Get It
Sludge Flow (GPM)Feed pump capacity × runtime ÷ 60. Or sludge flow meter.
% SolidsLab total solids on dewatering feed sludge.
Polymer Solution (GPH)Polymer pump setting. Pump rate × dilution water ratio.
Polymer Conc (% active)Polymer SDS or product label. Emulsion polymers: 30–50% active. Dry: 90–100%.
Feed to dewatering unit
Feed sludge concentration
Neat or diluted solution fed
Active polymer in solution
Targets: Belt press: 3–8 lbs/DT | Centrifuge: 5–12 lbs/DT | Gravity thickener: 1–4 lbs/DT | Lower = more cost effective
—
Enter data...
lbs/DT = (Polymer GPH × 8.34 × %Active × 24) ÷ (Sludge GPM × 8.34 × %Solids × 60 × 24 ÷ 2000)
🧪 POLYMER OPTIMIZATION
Emulsion Makedown — Dilution Water Flow Required
From product label
Typical: 0.2–0.5%
Metering pump output
—
Enter bulk %, target %, and pump rate...
Polymer Cost per Dry Ton
From main calc above
—
Enter cost/gal, dose, SG, and % active...
Dilution water = (Bulk% − Target%) ÷ Target% × polymer GPH | Cost/DT = (dose lbs active/DT) ÷ (%active/100) ÷ (SG×8.34) × $/gal

48 % Solids / Specific Gravity / Cake Converter

Convert between % solids, mg/L, specific gravity, lbs/day, and gallons/day. Works for sludge, cake, scum, and biosolids. The exam frequently requires converting "22% cake at X GPM" to lbs/day.
% SOLIDS / SPECIFIC GRAVITY / CAKE CONVERTER — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Three conversion modes covering every form the sludge mass problem takes — whether you're calculating how many lbs/day your belt press is producing, verifying a MLSS mass balance, or converting a lab-measured specific gravity into a percent solids for your dewatering design. All three modes use the same physical relationships; only the unknown changes.
Mode 1 — % Solids → lbs/day (the dewatering production calc)
When to use: Calculating dry solids production from your BFP (#84), centrifuge (#85), or gravity thickener. Also used to size drying beds (#86) and estimate hauling loads (#87).

Core formula: lbs dry solids/day = Flow (GPD) × 8.34 lb/gal × SG × (% Solids ÷ 100)

SG guidance: If you don't have a pycnometer reading, use the Mode 3 estimate (SG from % solids) and enter it here. Or accept the default SG=1.0 — it introduces only a small error at typical cake solids. At 22% cake, the difference between SG=1.0 and SG=1.07 is about 7% in the lbs/day result.

Output cross-check: Take the dry lbs/day result and compare it to what your polymer dosing calc (#42) produces for active polymer per dry ton. If they disagree significantly, your pump totalizer or lab % solids measurement may be off.
Mode 2 — mg/L → lbs/day (the mass loading workhorse)
When to use: This is the standard pounds formula (#1) restricted to concentration-based inputs. Use it whenever you have a lab result in mg/L and need to know the mass per day — MLSS inventory, WAS mass leaving the system, effluent TSS lbs/day for permit tracking, chemical doses in mg/L converted to lbs/day.

Formula: lbs/day = Flow (MGD) × Concentration (mg/L) × 8.34

Cross-check with Calc #1 (Pounds Formula): Calc #1 does the same thing but also has a reverse-solve mode (given lbs/day + flow, find mg/L) and a permit-comparison feature. Use Calc #103 Mode 2 for speed when you just need lbs/day. Use Calc #1 when you need to compare to a permit limit or back-calculate a dose.
Mode 3 — SG ↔ % Solids (the density inter-conversion)
When to use: When you've measured SG with a pycnometer and need % solids for a report or calc input — or vice versa. Also useful when the only data available is specific gravity from a manufacturer's spec sheet.

Two-component model: Sludge = water (SG=1.0) + dry solids (SG typically 1.3–1.6). The formula assumes these two phases are the only components. For primary sludge with significant grit or grease, accuracy decreases.

SG of dry solids: Activated sludge biomass ≈ 1.35–1.45. Digested sludge (more mineral) ≈ 1.45–1.6. Primary sludge (mixed organic/inorganic) ≈ 1.3–1.5. When uncertain, 1.4 is a safe default.

Output feeds into Mode 1: Enter the calculated SG into Mode 1's optional SG field for a more accurate lbs/day result.
Typical % Solids Reference — Know These for the Exam
StreamTypical % SolidsTypical SG
Raw primary sludge3–8%1.02–1.06
WAS / RAS (liquid)0.5–2%1.004–1.02
Gravity thickened4–10%1.03–1.08
Digested sludge2–6%1.02–1.05
BFP cake (#84)18–25%1.05–1.10
Centrifuge cake (#85)20–28%1.06–1.12
Heat-dried biosolids90%+~1.3
🔗 RELATED CALCULATORS
CALCNAMEHOW IT CONNECTS
#1Pounds FormulaSame core formula in Mode 2 but with reverse-solve and permit comparison. Use #1 when you need to back-calculate mg/L from a known lbs/day load.
#8WAS Pumping RateWAS flow (GPD) and WAS concentration (mg/L) from #8 feed directly into Mode 2 to get lbs/day solids being wasted — your daily sludge inventory draw.
#38Sludge Volume & % SolidsCompanion calc: converts wet sludge volume to dry weight using % solids. Use #38 when you have gallons of sludge rather than a flow rate.
#42Polymer DosingTakes dry tons/day from Mode 1 as input. If BFP is producing X dry tons/day, Calc #42 tells you how much active polymer to apply per ton.
#84Belt Filter PressBFP cake % solids (18–25%) feeds directly into Mode 1 as the % solids input. BFP also outputs hydraulic loading and solids capture rate to cross-check your lbs/day.
#85Centrifuge PerformanceCentrifuge cake % (20–28%) similarly feeds Mode 1. Use #85 first to verify solids capture, then Mode 1 to get dry lbs/day.
#86Sludge Drying BedsDrying bed sizing (#86) takes lbs dry solids/day as input — that's your Mode 1 output. Sets the required bed area based on solids loading rate.
#87Sludge Hauling CostTakes dry cake lbs/day and % solids to calculate hauling costs. Feed Mode 1 result directly into #87 for total disposal cost.
#69Plant Available NitrogenLand application calcs (#69, #70) work in dry tons/acre. Mode 1 provides the dry tons/day that then gets divided by application rate to find required acreage.
#106Biosolids 503 RuleSection 3 of Calc #106 (annual loading) takes application rate in dry T/ac/yr — which comes from Mode 1 lbs/day ÷ 2000 ÷ acres. The whole 503 compliance chain starts here.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• The 8.34 lb/gal factor is non-negotiable — it appears on every sludge mass exam problem
• "22% cake" = 22 lbs dry solids per 100 lbs wet cake — not per 100 gallons
• mg/L → % solids shortcut: % = mg/L ÷ (SG × 10,000) — e.g. 40,000 mg/L at SG 1.04 = 3.85%
• Exam often gives cake % + pump flow in GPM → use Mode 1 with GPM unit selected
• When exam says "specific gravity of the sludge is 1.03", they're giving you SG for the lbs formula
• VSS is volatile fraction of TSS — for activated sludge expect VSS/TSS ≈ 0.75–0.85
InputWhere to Get It
Mode selectorChoose the calculation direction: % Solids→All (dewatering output), mg/L→lbs/day (mass loading), or SG↔% Solids (density conversion).
% Solids (Mode 1)Lab gravimetric test: (dried wt − filter wt) ÷ wet sample wt × 100. BFP cake: 18–25%. Centrifuge cake: 20–28%. Digested sludge: 2–6%. From Calc #71 (TSS/VSS) if available.
Flow Rate (Mode 1)Belt press: measure GPM from sludge pump totalizer or timed bucket. Centrifuge: feed pump GPM from nameplate × runtime. Gravity thickener: overflow + underflow meter. Select GPM, GPD, or MGD to match your meter.
Specific Gravity — optional (Mode 1)Pycnometer measurement in your lab (ASTM D792 or similar). Or estimate using Mode 3: enter % solids to calculate SG. If blank, defaults to 1.0 — introduces ~3–7% error at typical cake solids.
Concentration mg/L (Mode 2)Lab TSS/VSS result (Calc #71), MLSS reading from aeration basin, effluent TSS from composite sample, or WAS concentration from RAS line grab. Units must be mg/L.
Flow MGD (Mode 2)Influent or effluent meter daily average ÷ 1,000,000. For WAS: pump GPD ÷ 1,000,000. Daily operator log or SCADA.
% Solids (Mode 3 → SG)Same lab gravimetric result as Mode 1. Enter here to calculate the corresponding specific gravity using the two-component density model.
Specific Gravity (Mode 3 → % Solids)Enter a known SG reading to back-calculate % solids. Pycnometer result or manufacturer spec sheet value.
SG of Dry Solids (Mode 3)Activated sludge biomass: 1.35–1.45. Digested sludge (more mineral): 1.45–1.60. Primary sludge: 1.30–1.50. Use 1.4 as default when unknown — ASTM or literature value for your sludge type.
Percent solids by weight
Enter value below
Default: 1.0 if blank
MLSS, TSS, etc.
Treatment flow
Enter to get SG
Enter to get % Solids
Typical: 1.3–1.6
Quick refs: Liquid sludge 0.5–3% · Thickened 3–8% · Digested 2–6% · BFP cake 18–25% · Centrifuge cake 20–28% · Dried biosolids 90%+
—
Select mode and enter data...
lbs/day = Flow(GPD) × 8.34 × SG × (% Solids ÷ 100)

49 Biosolids 503 Rule Compliance Worksheet

40 CFR Part 503 biosolids compliance: Class A/B pathogen reduction determination, the nine regulated heavy metals (ceiling vs pollutant concentration limits), and cumulative pollutant loading tracking. TCEQ Class A exam — sludge disposal questions heavily test 503 Rule metal limits and Class A/B requirements.
BIOSOLIDS 503 RULE COMPLIANCE — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
A complete 40 CFR Part 503 compliance worksheet covering all three things TCEQ and EPA require before land-applying biosolids: pathogen class determination (Section 1), the nine regulated heavy metal concentration checks against ceiling and EQ limits (Section 2), and annual plus life-of-site cumulative pollutant loading tracking (Section 3). This is not a theoretical exercise — 503 violations carry significant penalties and can result in permit suspension.
Section 1 — Pathogen Classification: Class A vs Class B
Class A (PFRP): No detectable Salmonella (<3 MPN/4g TS) AND fecal coliforms <1,000 MPN/g TS. Class A biosolids can be sold or given away to the public in bags, applied to home gardens, parks, and golf courses without site restriction. Requires one of the 7 listed PFRP processes.

Class B (PSRP): Fecal coliforms reduced to <2,000,000 MPN/g TS. Class B carries mandatory site use restrictions: no crops harvested within 14 months (unless processed before consumption), no grazing within 30 days, public access restricted for 1 year. Anaerobic digestion and aerobic digestion qualify for Class B — these are the processes most municipal plants use.

Cross-check with Calc #88 (Class A/B Pathogen Reduction): Calc #88 is a simpler tool focused on just the time-temperature determination for heat treatment and composting. Use #88 if you just need to check whether your process meets the time-temperature criteria. Use this calc (#106) when you need the full compliance worksheet including metals and loading.

VAR is required for both classes. Vector Attraction Reduction is not optional — it must be demonstrated regardless of whether you're Class A or Class B. Options 1–3 require lab testing (VS reduction, SOUR). Options 4–8 are treatment-based. Options 9–10 are site management practices (injection, incorporation).
Section 2 — Nine Regulated Metals: Two Tiers of Limits
Ceiling Concentration Limits (503.13(b)(1)): Hard stops — no biosolids exceeding these may be land-applied under any permit condition. Red cells in this calc. If any metal exceeds its ceiling, land application of that biosolids is prohibited until the concentration is reduced.

Pollutant Concentration Limits / EQ Limits (503.13(b)(3)): Lower limits that qualify biosolids for "Exceptional Quality" (EQ) status. EQ biosolids have no annual loading limits, no cumulative tracking requirements, and minimal site restrictions. Yellow cells in this calc — above EQ limit but below ceiling. Meeting all EQ limits is the gold standard; it dramatically simplifies compliance record-keeping.

Where metal concentrations come from: Annual biosolids characterization analysis — required at a frequency set by your permit. Lab results in mg/kg on a dry weight basis. Compare against Calc #39 (VSR) results: the VS reduction in your digester affects organic content but not heavy metal concentrations (metals concentrate as organics are destroyed — digested biosolids actually have higher metal concentrations per kg than raw sludge). This is why running Calc #39 alongside #106 is important: higher VS reduction = higher metal concentrations per kg even if total mass is lower.
Section 3 — Annual Loading & Cumulative Tracking
Annual loading formula: lbs/ac/yr = metal conc (mg/kg) × application rate (dry T/ac) × 2,000 lb/T ÷ 1,000,000 mg/kg

The application rate comes from: Calc #69 (Plant Available Nitrogen) + Calc #70 (Land Application Rate). Those calcs determine the agronomic rate — the maximum rate at which PAN matches crop nitrogen uptake. Enter that dry T/ac/yr here.

Why cumulative loading matters: For non-EQ biosolids, the life-of-site cumulative loading limits in 503.13(b)(2) Table 2 represent the total mass of each metal that can ever be applied at a single site. Once reached, no further application is permitted — period. Lead (267 lbs/ac cumulative), Cadmium (35 lbs/ac), and Zinc (2503 lbs/ac) are typically the limiting metals for long-operating sites.

Site records: 503.17(a)(4) requires the preparer to maintain records of cumulative loading for each site. This calc tracks it — enter years applied and current concentrations each year to monitor trajectory toward limits.
🔗 RELATED CALCULATORS
CALCNAMEHOW IT CONNECTS
#38Sludge Volume & % SolidsConverts wet sludge to dry weight. Required upstream calc — you need dry tons/year applied to land before running Section 3 of this calc.
#39Volatile Solids ReductionVS reduction from digestion concentrates heavy metals per unit dry weight — digested sludge has higher mg/kg than raw at the same total mass. Run #39 to understand your VS reduction, then use digested biosolids metal concentrations (not raw) in Section 2.
#40Digester Loading RateDigester volatile solids loading connects to VS reduction in #39. Understanding your digester operation helps explain why metal concentrations change across the treatment train.
#42Polymer DosingBelt press or centrifuge polymer dosing affects cake % solids and therefore the volume of biosolids requiring land application. Higher cake % = less volume to haul and apply per ton of dry solids.
#69Plant Available NitrogenPAN calc determines nitrogen availability from biosolids — the primary driver of agronomic application rate. The dry T/ac/yr rate from Calc #70 is the input to Section 3 annual loading.
#70Land Application RateDirectly outputs dry tons/acre/year (agronomic rate). That value goes into the Application Rate field in Section 3 of this calc. These two calcs should always be run together.
#87Sludge Hauling CostOnce you know how many dry tons/year you can land-apply (from #70), subtract from total dry tons produced (from Calc #103) to find residual requiring hauling. Use #87 for that cost.
#88Class A/B Pathogen ReductionSimpler pathogen check for just the time-temperature PFRP/PSRP determination. Use #88 for a quick check; use this calc when you need the full 503 compliance picture.
#91DMR CalculatorDMR tracking for permit compliance — parallels what #106 does for biosolids. Both track regulatory limits, flag exceedances, and support TCEQ reporting.
#103% Solids / SG / Cake ConverterConverts dewatering output to dry lbs/day and tons/year — the upstream calculation that feeds both Section 3 of this calc and the land application rate calcs #69 and #70.
📝 TCEQ Exam — 503 Rule Must-Knows
• Class A = no detectable Salmonella AND fecal coliforms <1,000 MPN/g TS — both criteria required simultaneously
• Class B site restriction: no crops harvested within 14 months of application unless commercially processed
• VAR is required for both Class A and Class B — not just one class
• The 9 regulated metals: As, Cd, Cu, Pb, Hg, Mo, Ni, Se, Zn — know all nine
• Ceiling limits = absolute prohibition above them | EQ limits = simpler compliance path below them
• Annual loading (lbs/ac) = mg/kg × T/ac × 2,000 ÷ 1,000,000 — this exact formula has appeared on Class A exams
• Digestion concentrates metals per kg: if VS reduction = 50%, metal concentration per kg roughly doubles vs raw feed
• EQ status = all 9 metals below EQ limits + Class A pathogen + VAR met → no cumulative tracking required
InputWhere to Get It
Treatment Process (Section 1)40 CFR Part 503, Appendix B (PFRP) and Subpart D (PSRP). Select the process that describes your actual treatment. For most municipal plants with anaerobic digestion: Class B PSRP. For plants with heat drying or composting: Class A PFRP. Confirm with your TCEQ permit or operations manual.
Vector Attraction Reduction (Section 1)40 CFR 503.33. Must be demonstrated for all land application. For digested sludge: Options 1 (≥38% VS reduction) or 3 (SOUR ≤1.5 mg O₂/g TS/hr) are most common. Option 3 requires Calc #78 (SOUR) result. For dewatered cake: Options 7 (≥75% solids) or 9 (soil injection). Source: lab data, permit, or operations manual.
Metal Concentrations — 9 metals (Section 2)Annual biosolids characterization analysis from a TCEQ-certified laboratory. Results in mg/kg on a dry weight basis. Required analysis frequency set by your TCEQ permit (typically annual or per permit schedule). Metals: Arsenic, Cadmium, Copper, Lead, Mercury, Molybdenum, Nickel, Selenium, Zinc. Source: lab report, biosolids characterization summary.
Application Rate (dry T/ac/yr, Section 3)Agronomic rate from Calc #70 (Land Application Rate) — the rate at which Plant Available Nitrogen (PAN, from Calc #69) matches crop nitrogen uptake. Permitted rate from your TCEQ land application permit or site-specific land application plan. Typical range: 1–10 dry T/ac/yr.
Years Applied (Section 3)Total number of years biosolids have been applied at this specific land application site. From your site management log — 40 CFR 503.17(a)(4) requires this to be maintained for the life of the site. Enter current year's application as year 1 if starting a new site.
Biosolids % Solids (for rate conversion)From Calc #103 (% Solids / Cake Converter) Mode 1 output — or from dewatering equipment lab sheet. Needed to convert dry tons/year applied to wet tons (for hauling volume) via Calc #87.
🧫 Section 1 — Pathogen Classification
40 CFR 503 Subpart D / Appendix B
⚗️ Section 2 — Nine Regulated Metals (mg/kg dry wt)
Enter measured concentrations. Red = exceeds ceiling limit. Yellow = exceeds pollutant concentration limit (EQ limit for bulk land application).
METAL CEILING
(mg/kg)		 POLLUTANT CONC
LIMIT (EQ)		 MEASURED
(mg/kg)		 STATUS
📊 Section 3 — Annual Loading & Cumulative Tracking
From PAN calc (agronomic rate)
Total years at this site
—
Select treatment process to begin...
Annual metal loading (lbs/ac) = conc(mg/kg) × rate(T/ac) × 2000 lb/T ÷ 1,000,000 mg/kg

50 Digester Stability (VA/Alk)

The VA/Alkalinity ratio is the single most reliable indicator of digester health. Volatile acids accumulating faster than methanogens can consume them = rising ratio = impending upset. Catch it early — a ratio above 0.4 can spiral to full crash within days.
DIGESTER STABILITY CHECK — GUIDE
What This Calculates
The VA/Alkalinity ratio and its interpretation, the alkalinity buffer remaining after accounting for volatile acids, and the amount of NaHCO₃ or lime required to restore a safe buffer if the ratio is elevated.
The VA/Alk Ratio — Thresholds
RatioStatusAction
< 0.10ExcellentNo action needed
0.10–0.25StableNormal operation
0.25–0.35WatchIncrease sampling, review loading
0.35–0.50StressedReduce feed, add NaHCO₃
> 0.50UpsetStop feed, emergency buffer addition
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#40 Digester LRIf VA/Alk is elevated, check VS loading rate — overloading is the most common cause of VA accumulation.
#39 VSRVS reduction % should correlate with gas production and stability. Declining VSR alongside rising VA/Alk = double warning.
#41 BiogasGas production drop is often the first visible symptom after VA/Alk ratio climbs. Track both together.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• VA/Alk ratio = Volatile Acids (mg/L as acetic) ÷ Total Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃)
• Healthy digester: pH 6.8–7.4, VA/Alk < 0.25, gas 60–70% methane
• NaHCO₃ = gentlest alkalinity source (won't pH overshoot), preferred over lime
• 1 lb NaHCO₃ raises alkalinity by ~714 mg/L per 1000 gallons
• Class A exam: know all 5 PSRP treatment processes for Class B biosolids
InputWhere to Get It
Volatile Acids (mg/L as acetic)Lab titration test on digester supernatant or mixed liquor sample. Report as mg/L acetic acid equivalent. Typically run by outside lab or on-site if titration equipment available. Healthy range: 50–300 mg/L.
Total Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃)Lab titration (Standard Methods 2320B) on digester sample. Healthy range: 1,500–5,000 mg/L as CaCO₃. From daily/weekly lab report.
Digester Volume (gallons) — optionalFrom as-built drawings or design documents. Used to calculate total NaHCO₃ addition needed to raise alkalinity to target.
Target Alkalinity (mg/L) — optionalDesired alkalinity after correction. Typical target: 2,000–3,000 mg/L. Enter to get lbs NaHCO₃ required.
From lab titration
From lab titration
For chemical addition calc
For NaHCO₃ dose calc
VA/Alk = Volatile Acids ÷ Total Alkalinity | <0.25 stable | >0.40 action required | >0.50 upset
—
Enter volatile acids and total alkalinity...
VA/Alk = Volatile Acids (mg/L as acetic) ÷ Total Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃) | NaHCO₃ lbs = (target − current) × vol_gal × 8.34 ÷ 1,000,000 × (84/61)
🌿 Nutrient Removal Calculators

51 Phosphorus Removal

Calculate alum or ferric chloride dose for chemical phosphorus precipitation. TCEQ phosphorus limits are tightening across Texas watersheds.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA.
Influent P (mg/L)24-hr composite influent sample. Total P or ortho-P lab result.
Target Effluent P (mg/L)TPDES permit limit (Page 2a) or facility effluent target.
Molar Ratio (Me:P)Design basis. Alum: 1.5–2.0:1. Ferric chloride: 1.5–2.0:1. O&M manual.
Treatment flow
Total phosphorus in
Permit limit
Alum: 1.5–2.5 | Ferric: 1.5–3.0 | Higher = more removal, more sludge
Liquid alum delivered cost
Liquid ferric chloride cost
Chemical MW: Alum (Al₂(SO₄)₃·14H₂O) = 594 | Ferric Chloride (FeCl₃) = 162.2 | P = 31 | Al = 27 | Fe = 55.85
—
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
Metal dose = ΔP × Molar Ratio × (Metal MW ÷ P MW) | lbs/day = Flow × Dose × 8.34

52 Alkalinity & Nitrification DIAGRAM

Calculate alkalinity destroyed by nitrification (7.14 mg alk per mg NH₃-N). Low alkalinity causes nitrification crash and pH drop.
InputWhere to Get It
Influent Alkalinity (mg/L)Influent composite sample. Alkalinity titration test. Lab bench sheet.
NH₃-N Oxidized (mg/L)Influent NH₃ minus effluent NH₃. Lab results (both composite samples).
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA daily average.
Lab alkalinity result
Influent NH₃ − Effluent NH₃
For lime dosing calc
If anoxic zone present
Rule: 7.14 mg alk consumed per mg NH₃-N | 3.57 mg alk recovered per mg NO₃-N denitrified | Maintain >80 mg/L remaining
⚠️ ALKALINITY CRASH RISK — Supplemental Dosing Required
—
Enter data...
Alk consumed = NH₃-N × 7.14 | Remaining = Influent − Consumed

53 Nitrogen Balance

Track where nitrogen goes in the treatment process. Essential for TN permit compliance and process optimization.
InputWhere to Get It
Influent TKN (mg/L)Influent composite sample. TKN = total Kjeldahl nitrogen. Lab result.
Effluent NH₃-N (mg/L)Effluent composite sample lab result.
Effluent NO₃-N (mg/L)Effluent composite sample lab result. Nitrate analysis.
Biomass N Uptake (mg/L)Estimate: 12.4% of net VSS production. Use O&M design value or 2–3 mg/L typical.
Total Kjeldahl nitrogen in
Ammonia in effluent
Nitrate in effluent
Typically 1–3 mg/L
N Pathways: Nitrification (NH₃→NO₃) | Denitrification (NO₃→N₂ gas) | Biomass uptake | Effluent TN = NH₃ + NO₃ + Org-N
—
Enter data...
Denitrified = TKN − Eff NH₃ − Eff NO₃ − Biomass N

54 DO Saturation vs Temperature

Calculate DO saturation at any water temperature. Includes altitude and salinity corrections. Calculate DO deficit for aeration system design and troubleshooting. Critical for summer low-DO troubleshooting.
InputWhere to Get It
Water Temperature (°C)Thermometer or temp probe in aeration basin. SCADA or field measurement.
Altitude (ft)USGS topo map, Google Earth, or GPS at plant location. Fixed — enter once.
Salinity (g/L TDS)Lab TDS analysis or conductivity meter. Usually 0–2 for municipal WW.
Measured DO (mg/L)DO meter probe in aeration basin. Calibrate daily against Winkler titration.
DO SATURATION vs TEMPERATURE GUIDE
Why DO Saturation Changes with Temperature
Oxygen dissolves less in warm water than in cold water — it's basic physics. At 20°C, water can hold 9.09 mg/L of dissolved oxygen at saturation. At 30°C (a hot Texas summer), that drops to 7.56 mg/L — a 17% reduction. This is why summer low-DO problems are so common: your biology demands the same oxygen, but the water simply can't hold as much.
Step-by-Step Instructions
Step 1: Enter Water Temperature (required)
Use your aeration basin water temperature in °C, not air temperature. Get this from your temperature probe or grab sample. Range: 0–40°C.

Step 2: Enter Altitude (optional)
Atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, reducing how much oxygen dissolves. Houston is near sea level (enter 0). For inland Texas plants at elevation, enter your approximate altitude in feet. Reduces saturation by ~3% per 1,000 ft.

Step 3: Enter Salinity (optional)
Salt reduces DO saturation. Most municipal wastewater is 0–2 g/L TDS. Industrial wastewater or coastal plants may be higher. Each g/L reduces saturation by ~0.08 mg/L.

Step 4: Enter Measured DO (optional)
Enter your current measured DO reading. The calculator computes DO deficit (saturation minus measured), saturation percentage, and operational context.
DO Saturation Reference Table
Temp (°C)DO Sat (mg/L)Season (TX)Operational Note
10°C11.29WinterEasy to maintain DO targets
15°C10.07Spring/FallNormal operation
20°C9.09Mild summerStandard design reference
25°C8.26SummerMonitor aeration capacity
30°C7.56Hot summerAeration stress likely
35°C6.93Extreme heatNitrification failure risk
Using DO Deficit for Troubleshooting
DO Deficit = Saturation − Measured DO

• Deficit < 1 mg/L — Well saturated. Your aeration is more than adequate for current conditions.
• Deficit 1–3 mg/L — Normal operating range. Aeration is working but there's meaningful demand.
• Deficit > 3 mg/L — Large deficit. Aeration is struggling to keep up with oxygen demand. Check blower output, diffuser condition, and organic loading.
• Deficit at saturation (measured DO ≈ 0) — Anoxic conditions. Immediate action required unless this is an intentional anoxic zone.

Saturation % = Measured DO ÷ Saturated DO × 100
Healthy aeration basin: 30–80% saturation. Below 20% = oxygen limited. Above 90% = over-aerated (wasting energy).
SOTR → AOTR Connection
DO saturation feeds directly into blower and aerator sizing (use Calc #62 SOTR→AOTR and Calc #68 Blower Air Requirement). The correction factor:

AOTR = SOTR × (β × C_sat_T − C_L) ÷ (C_sat_20) × θ^(T−20)

Where C_sat_T is what this calculator gives you, C_L is your target operating DO (usually 2 mg/L), and C_sat_20 = 9.09 mg/L. Summer temperatures can reduce AOTR by 15–25% compared to standard conditions — a major factor in aeration system adequacy.
TCEQ Class A Exam Tips
• DO saturation at 20°C: Memorize 9.09 mg/L — this is the standard reference for all calculations
• TCEQ effluent DO permit limit: Minimum 4.0 mg/L (most TPDES permits) — minimum, not a target
• Aeration basin target: 1.5–3.0 mg/L for BOD removal; 2.0–4.0 mg/L for nitrification
• Temp effect rule: DO saturation decreases ~0.17 mg/L per 1°C increase
• Altitude effect: Significant for plants above ~2,000 ft — not relevant for coastal Texas but tested on exam
• Beta factor (β): Ratio of DO saturation in wastewater to clean water — typically 0.95–0.98 for municipal wastewater
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Water Temperature (°C)Thermometer or temperature probe in aeration basin. SCADA sensor or field measurement. Not air temp.
Altitude (ft)USGS topo map, Google Earth, or GPS at plant location. Fixed value — enter once and remember it.
Salinity (g/L TDS)Lab TDS analysis or conductivity meter × conversion factor. Usually 0–2 for municipal WW.
Measured DO (mg/L)DO meter probe in aeration basin. Calibrate daily against Winkler titration. SCADA or field reading.
Aeration basin temp
0 = sea level
Wastewater ≈ 0–2 g/L
For deficit calculation
DO Saturation: 9.09 mg/L @ 20°C | 8.26 @ 25°C | 7.56 @ 30°C | Drops ~0.17 mg/L per °C rise | Altitude reduces by ~3% per 1000 ft
—
Enter temperature...
DO_sat = 14.62 − 0.3898T + 0.006969T² − 0.00005896T³ (fresh, sea level) | Altitude: × e^(−0.000118×alt_m) | Salinity: − 0.0841×S
💧 Hydraulics & Pumping Calculators

55 Total Dynamic Head

Calculate total dynamic head from static lift, friction loss, and velocity head. Determines pump operating point and energy requirements.
InputWhere to Get It
Static Head (ft)Elevation difference between pump inlet and discharge. As-built drawings or survey.
Friction Loss (ft)Hazen-Williams formula or pump system curve. Hydraulic profile in O&M manual.
Velocity Head (ft)Calculated: V²/2g. Usually small (<1 ft) and sometimes ignored.
TOTAL DYNAMIC HEAD — OPERATOR GUIDE
What is TDH?
TDH is the total pressure a pump must overcome to move water from point A to point B. It's the sum of three components: how high you're pushing it (static head), how much friction the pipe creates (friction loss), and the energy in the moving water (velocity head). TDH determines your pump selection, motor size, and energy cost.
Inputs Explained
Static Head (ft) — The vertical distance between the water surface at the pump suction and the discharge point. Measure with a tape or from as-built drawings. If pumping uphill from a wet well at 100 ft elevation to a tank at 130 ft, static head = 30 ft.
Friction Loss (ft) — Energy lost to pipe wall drag. Depends on pipe diameter, length, material, and flow rate. Use Hazen-Williams tables, Calc #56 (Force Main), or a rule of thumb: 1–5 ft per 100 ft of pipe for typical WW flows.
Velocity Head (ft) — Kinetic energy in the moving water. Formula: V²/(2g). At typical WW velocities (3–8 fps), this is only 0.1–1.0 ft — often negligible but always included on TCEQ exams.
Why This Matters
A pump curve plots flow (GPM) vs head (ft). Your pump operates where the system curve (TDH at various flows) intersects the pump curve. If TDH increases (clogged pipe, higher discharge), flow decreases. If TDH is wrong during design, you get an oversized or undersized pump.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• TDH = Static Head + Friction Loss + Velocity Head
• Velocity head: V²/2g where g = 32.2 ft/s². At 5 fps: 5²/(2×32.2) = 0.39 ft
• To convert TDH (ft) to PSI: multiply by 0.433
• Water HP = (Flow GPM × TDH ft) ÷ 3960
• Brake HP = Water HP ÷ pump efficiency (typically 0.60–0.80)
• Common trick: they give pressure in PSI — convert to ft by dividing by 0.433 (or × 2.31)
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Static Head (ft)Measure elevation difference between pump inlet and discharge point. As-built drawings or field survey.
Friction Loss (ft)Hazen-Williams formula or pump system curve. Hydraulic profile in O&M manual or engineer's report.
Velocity Head (ft)Calculated: V²/2g. Velocity from flow ÷ pipe area. Usually small (<1 ft) and sometimes ignored.
Elevation difference
Pipe friction + fittings
v²/2g (often small)
Typical TDH: Influent lift station: 20–40 ft | RAS/WAS: 5–15 ft | Transfer pumps: 10–30 ft | 1 psi = 2.31 ft head
0.0 ft
Enter data...
TDH = Static Head + Friction Loss + Velocity Head

56 Pump HP & Energy Cost

Calculate water horsepower, brake horsepower, motor HP, and daily energy cost from flow, TDH, and efficiency.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPM)Pump flow meter, ultrasonic clamp-on, or pump curve at measured head.
TDH (ft)Output from TDH calc (#46). Or read from pump discharge/suction gauges.
Pump Efficiency (%)Pump curve at operating point. Equipment nameplate or manufacturer data.
Motor Efficiency (%)Motor nameplate. Premium efficiency: 93–96%. Standard: 88–92%.
Hours/Day RunningRuntime meter on pump panel or SCADA run-hours counter.
Electric Rate ($/kWh)Electric utility bill. Accounts/finance. Typically $0.08–$0.14/kWh.
Pump flow rate
Total dynamic head
Typical 60-80%
Typical 85-95%
Runtime per day
Utility rate
TCEQ Formulas: WHP = (GPM × TDH) ÷ 3960 | BHP = WHP ÷ Pump Eff | Motor HP = BHP ÷ Motor Eff | kW = HP × 0.746
—
Enter flow and TDH...
WHP = (GPM × TDH) ÷ 3960 | BHP = WHP ÷ ηpump
See also: #46 TDH · #48 Pipe Velocity

57 Pipe Velocity

Calculate flow velocity from flow rate and pipe diameter. Flags settling (<2 fps) and scouring (>10 fps) conditions.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPM)Flow meter or pump capacity. SCADA.
Pipe Diameter (in)As-built drawings or stamped on pipe. Collection system maps.
Pump or pipe flow
Internal diameter
Velocity Targets: Gravity sewer: 2–10 fps | Force main: 3–8 fps | Suction pipe: 3–6 fps | <2 fps = settling | >10 fps = erosion
0.0 fps
Enter data...
V (fps) = Flow (GPM) × 0.4085 ÷ Diameter² (in²)

58 Horsepower (WHP & BHP)

Calculate water horsepower and brake horsepower from flow, head, and pump efficiency. Used for energy costs and pump selection.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPM)Flow meter or pump capacity at operating conditions.
TDH (ft)TDH calc (#46) output or field gauge measurements.
Pump Efficiency (%)Pump curve at operating point. Manufacturer nameplate.
Motor Efficiency (%)Motor nameplate label.
Pump flow rate
Total dynamic head
From pump curve: 60–85%
Typical: 85–95%
Your utility rate for cost calc
Formulas: WHP = (Flow × TDH) ÷ 3960 | BHP = WHP ÷ Pump Eff | MHP = BHP ÷ Motor Eff | 1 HP = 0.746 kW
—
Enter data...
WHP = (GPM × TDH) ÷ 3960 | BHP = WHP ÷ Efficiency

59 Peak Flow Impact Calculator

Check SOR, HRT, and SLR across all clarifiers at peak wet weather flow. Replaces manually chaining cards 11, 13, and 6 for the most common storm/wet weather question. Flags which hydraulic limit is exceeded first.
InputWhere to Get It
Design Avg Flow (MGD)TPDES permit design flow. Engineering design report.
Peak Flow MultiplierPeaking factor from engineering report or TCEQ 30 TAC §217 (2.5–4.0 typical).
Clarifier Surface Area (ft²)As-built drawings. π/4 × D² for circular.
Clarifier Depth (ft)As-built drawings — side water depth.
MLSS at Design FlowTarget operating MLSS from O&M manual.
Number of ClarifiersCount from site plan. Only in-service units.
Normal daily average flow
Total active clarifier area
Side water depth
Current or design MLSS
Active units during storm
DESIGN LIMITS (TCEQ / Metcalf & Eddy)
SOR avg: 400–800 gpd/ft²
SOR peak: 1,000–1,500 gpd/ft²
SLR max: 30–50 lb/ft²/day
HRT min: 1.5–2.5 hrs
Depth: 10–16 ft
Peak SOR: Peak Flow ÷ Clarifier Area | SLR: Flow × MLSS × 8.34 ÷ Area | HRT: Volume ÷ Peak Flow × 24
—
Enter flow, clarifier area, and depth...
Peak SOR = Peak Flow (gpd) ÷ Area (ft²) | SLR = Flow × MLSS × 8.34 ÷ Area | HRT = Vol ÷ Peak Flow × 24

60 Pump Curve Operating Point

Find the pump operating point where the pump curve intersects the system curve. Enter 2–5 pump curve data points (GPM, TDH) and your system curve parameters. Used for pump selection, verification after installation, and TCEQ exam pump curve problems.
PUMP CURVE OPERATING POINT — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
The actual flow and head at which your pump will operate when installed in a specific piping system. A pump curve shows what a pump can do — the operating point is what it will do when the system resistance is applied. These are almost never the same as the nameplate rating.
Where This Fits in the Pump Design Workflow
1
Build the system curve: Use Calc #46 (Total Dynamic Head) to find static head. Use Calc #56 (Force Main Friction Loss / Hazen-Williams) to find friction head loss at a reference flow. Divide friction loss by Q² to get your k coefficient.
2
Get pump data: Read 3–5 GPM / TDH data point pairs from the manufacturer's pump curve data sheet. Shutoff head (GPM=0), BEP (best efficiency point), and max flow are the minimum three.
3
Run this calc: Enter the data points and your system curve (static head + k). The calc fits a quadratic curve through your pump data and solves for intersection with the system curve.
4
Verify with energy: Take the operating point GPM and TDH → enter into Calc #47 (Pump HP & Energy Cost) with your pump efficiency. Verify BHP doesn't exceed motor nameplate HP and check annual energy cost.
5
Check wet well: The operating GPM feeds directly into Calc #54 (Wet Well Sizing) as the pump capacity. Confirm the wet well provides adequate volume for your minimum cycle time.
Finding k — The Friction Coefficient
From Hazen-Williams (Calc #56): Run Calc #56 with your pipe diameter, C factor, and length. At any flow Q, it returns friction head loss in feet. Then k = friction loss (ft) ÷ Q² (GPM²).

Example: Calc #56 gives 12 ft friction at 600 GPM on your 6" force main → k = 12 ÷ 600² = 0.0000333

Typical ranges by system type:
• Short 4" line (≤500 ft): k ≈ 0.00003–0.0001
• Medium 6" line (500–2000 ft): k ≈ 0.0001–0.001
• Long 8" line (1–3 miles): k ≈ 0.001–0.005
• Long 12" transmission main: k ≈ 0.0001–0.001

Velocity check (Calc #48): After finding the operating GPM, run Calc #48 (Pipe Velocity) to confirm your force main velocity is 2–8 fps. Below 2 fps = solids deposition. Above 8 fps = erosion.
Interpreting the Result
Operating flow lower than expected: Your system curve is steeper than designed — check for partially closed isolation valves, increased pipe roughness (aging pipe / C-factor degradation), or an undersized discharge header.

Operating head higher than expected: Verify static head measurement — water level in wet well fluctuates, and low wet well level increases static head significantly.

BHP exceeds motor HP: Pump is operating far right of its curve (low head, high flow). Throttle the discharge valve slightly to move the operating point left. Or replace with a higher-HP motor.

Only 2 data points available: The calc uses linear interpolation — still useful but less accurate. Try to get at least 3 points including the shutoff head (at 0 GPM) and max flow.
🔗 RELATED CALCULATORS
CALCNAMEHOW IT CONNECTS
#46Total Dynamic HeadProvides static head + velocity head directly. TDH at any flow = the system curve value at that flow. Run #46 at two flows to verify your k coefficient.
#56Force Main Friction LossHazen-Williams friction loss at a reference flow. Divide that loss by Q² to get k. The primary upstream input to this calc.
#47Pump HP & Energy CostTakes operating GPM and TDH from this calc as inputs. Converts to WHP, BHP, and annual $. Run immediately after finding operating point to confirm motor sizing.
#48Pipe VelocityEnter operating GPM and pipe diameter to confirm 2–8 fps in the force main. A velocity check is the first thing to do after finding operating flow.
#49Horsepower (WHP & BHP)Alternate HP calculator for when you want a quick check without energy cost. Same inputs: flow, TDH, efficiency.
#54Wet Well SizingOperating GPM from this calc = pump capacity for wet well design. Feed directly into #54 to size working volume and confirm starts/hr.
#73Lift Station CapacityLift station analysis uses pump capacity (GPM) and inflow — operating point gives you the actual pump capacity under real system conditions, not nameplate.
#75Pump Station DrawdownEmergency planning calc that takes pump GPM and wet well volume. Operating GPM is more accurate than nameplate for drawdown time calculations.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Operating point = intersection of pump curve and system curve — never just the nameplate flow
• Higher static head (deeper wet well, higher discharge elevation) → operating point shifts LEFT = less flow, more head
• Two identical pumps in parallel → flow increases but less than doubles; each pump works against higher system resistance
• Two pumps in series → head approximately doubles at the same flow; used for high-head transmission mains
• WHP = Q(GPM) × TDH(ft) ÷ 3960 | BHP = WHP ÷ pump efficiency | Motor HP = BHP ÷ motor efficiency
• Pump operating far right of BEP = cavitation risk (NPSH exceeded). Far left = possible shaft damage from recirculation.
InputWhere to Get It
GPM / TDH Points (rows 1–5)Manufacturer's pump performance curve data sheet — the table or graph shipped with the pump or available from manufacturer website. Read at least 3 pairs: shutoff head (GPM=0, max TDH), best efficiency point (BEP), and runout (max GPM, min TDH). More points = more accurate quadratic fit.
Shutoff Head (GPM=0)The TDH value at zero flow on the pump curve — highest point on the curve. Read from the left y-intercept of the manufacturer curve graph.
Best Efficiency Point (BEP)The GPM and TDH at peak pump efficiency, usually marked on the curve with an asterisk or circled. Your target operating region. Read from pump data sheet.
Max Flow PointGPM and TDH at maximum flow (rightmost usable point). Read from the right end of the manufacturer curve before it goes flat.
Static Head (ft)Elevation difference between pump centerline (or wet well water surface) and the discharge point. Field survey with a level or total station. Site drawings (as-builts). Does not vary with flow rate — it is a fixed system parameter. Use Calc #46 (TDH) static component.
Friction Coefficient kk = friction head loss (ft) ÷ flow² (GPM²). Get friction loss from Calc #56 (Hazen-Williams) at any known flow, then divide by that flow squared. Example: Calc #56 returns 8 ft loss at 400 GPM → k = 8 ÷ 160,000 = 0.00005. If unknown, start with k = 0.0001 for short runs, 0.001 for long runs.
Pump Efficiency % (optional)From the pump efficiency curve on the same data sheet — efficiency at your expected operating region. Typical centrifugal pumps: 65–85% at BEP. Used only to calculate BHP output. Leave blank if efficiency is unknown.
📈 Pump Curve Data Points (2–5 points)
FLOW (GPM)
HEAD (ft TDH)
🏗️ System Curve Parameters
Elevation difference only
TDH_friction = k × Q²
For BHP calculation
System curve: TDH = Static Head + k × Q² | k = friction loss at known flow ÷ Q² | Operating point = where pump TDH = system TDH
—
Enter at least 2 pump curve points + static head...
Operating Point: pump TDH(Q) = Static Head + k × Q² | BHP = Q(GPM) × TDH ÷ (3960 × efficiency)

61 Equalization Basin Sizing

Size an equalization basin using the mass diagram method. Enter hourly flow rates (or use presets) and the calc finds the required EQ volume, peak attenuation, and discharge pump sizing. TCEQ Class A design topic — the exam may give hourly flows and ask for EQ volume.
EQUALIZATION BASIN SIZING — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
The required volume of a flow equalization basin using the mass diagram method. An EQ basin absorbs peak hourly flows and discharges a steady average rate to the treatment plant. This protects clarifiers, biological processes, and disinfection systems from the twice-daily surge that residential collection systems produce — and the sharp shift-start peaks that industrial contributors create.
Where EQ Fits in the Treatment Train
Why EQ matters for downstream calcs: Every clarifier loading calc (#11 SOR, #12 WOR, #13 SLR) is only valid at a specific flow. EQ converts peak flow to average flow before it hits the treatment train. Without EQ, your secondary clarifier may be sized for average flow but receive 2× average during morning peaks — blowing out the sludge blanket.

Peak Flow Impact Calc #59: Before deciding whether EQ is needed, run Calc #59 (Peak Flow Impact). It shows whether your existing clarifiers can handle peak flow without exceeding SOR/SLR limits. If #59 shows violations at peak, EQ is the design solution — and this calc sizes it.

After EQ: The discharge pump output of this calc (constant MGD) is the flow that hits the treatment plant. Use that constant flow — not the peak — when sizing aeration (#68), clarifiers (#98), and disinfection (#83).
Step-by-Step: Sizing a Real EQ Basin
1
Collect hourly flow data: Pull 24-hour SCADA flow records for several representative weekdays. Pick the highest-flow day as your design basis. If SCADA is unavailable, use Calc #55 (I/I) to estimate wet weather flow and Calc #64 (Population Equivalent) to estimate dry weather average flow. Build a diurnal factor curve.
2
Enter flows into the 24-hour grid: Each cell = average flow rate during that clock hour in MGD. Hour 0 = midnight–1am. If you only have peak flow ratio data (e.g. "peak = 2.5× average"), use the residential preset as a starting shape, then scale to match your known average and peak.
3
Set the discharge rate: Leave blank to automatically use the daily average. Override if your treatment train has a fixed-capacity bottleneck — e.g. if your aeration basin SRT requires maximum 1.8 MGD despite 2.1 MGD average. Your discharge pump must be able to maintain that constant rate.
4
Apply safety factor: Standard is 1.25. Use 1.50 if: (a) flow data is from dry season only and I/I is significant, (b) future growth is anticipated within the permit cycle, or (c) plant serves industrial contributors with unpredictable batch discharge.
5
Use the surface area output: Feed into Calc #66 (Tank Volume) with your target dimensions to verify the geometry achieves the required volume. Shallow EQ basins (8–12 ft) need less structural depth but more footprint — compare land cost vs construction cost.
6
Size the discharge pump: Operating GPM from this calc → enter into Calc #104 (Pump Curve Operating Point) with your discharge piping system curve to find actual operating flow and head. Then verify with Calc #47 (Pump HP).
Using the Presets Effectively
🏘️ Residential (default for most municipal plants): Classic diurnal double-peak — morning peak around 7–9am as households prepare for work, evening peak 5–8pm as people return home. Overnight flow drops to 40–50% of average. Peak factor typically 1.8–2.5× average.

🏭 Industrial (food processing, manufacturing): Flat overnight, sharp step-up at shift start (6am), sustained peak during operating hours, sharp cutoff at end of shift. Some industrial facilities have batch dump events — if known, these should be added as 1-hour spikes on top of the shift baseline.

🔀 Combined (mixed municipal + industrial): Residential diurnal shape blended with elevated daytime industrial flow. Typical of a plant serving a small city with a food processor or manufacturing plant as a significant industrial user.

Override the presets with real data: The presets scale to ~1.5 MGD average for demonstration. For your actual plant, replace every cell with actual SCADA hourly averages. If your plant averages 0.75 MGD, a properly loaded residential preset would show each overnight cell at ~0.55 MGD and morning peak cells at ~1.1 MGD.
🔗 RELATED CALCULATORS
CALCNAMEHOW IT CONNECTS
#55Infiltration & Inflow (I/I)I/I calc provides wet weather peak flow estimate — the scenario that drives maximum EQ volume requirements. Run #55 first to quantify your I/I contribution, then use the wet weather peak as your peak hourly flow input.
#64Population EquivalentPE calc estimates average dry weather flow from service population. Useful as a starting point for the daily average when SCADA data is unavailable.
#59Peak Flow Impact CalculatorRun #59 before sizing EQ to determine whether EQ is actually needed and by how much. If #59 shows clarifiers fail SOR/SLR at peak, EQ is warranted — this calc tells you how much volume to build.
#6Detention TimeEQ basin HRT = volume ÷ average flow. Use Calc #6 with EQ volume (MG) and average discharge MGD to verify minimum 4–8 hour HRT for adequate mixing/equalization.
#66Tank VolumeTakes EQ surface area and depth to back-check that a physically buildable tank matches the required volume. Verify that your depth and footprint assumptions are constructible.
#11Surface Overflow RateSOR calc uses discharge flow from EQ (constant average). The constant discharge rate from this calc is the correct flow to use in clarifier loading checks — not peak.
#98Clarifier DesignClarifier sizing (primary or secondary) should use the post-EQ constant flow, not peak. EQ eliminates the peak-flow design driver. Adding MLSS on #98 also gives you SLR against secondary design ranges.
#104Pump Curve Operating PointThe EQ discharge pump GPM feeds directly into #104. Size the pump to deliver constant average flow against your force main system curve — the operating point tells you actual GPM delivered.
#68Blower Air RequirementAeration sizing should use average BOD load (post-EQ), not peak load. EQ also dampens BOD concentration peaks which would otherwise require oversized blowers.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Required EQ volume = max vertical distance on the mass diagram = max cumulative excess − min cumulative excess
• Safety factor is 1.25 unless the problem states otherwise
• Discharge pump = daily average flow (constant rate) — not peak, not max
• EQ does NOT improve effluent quality directly — it prevents hydraulic upsets from exceeding unit process limits
• Peak attenuation % = (peak flow − average flow) ÷ peak flow × 100
• A 2.5× peak factor means EQ must store (2.5−1.0)/2.5 × 100% = 60% of the daily volume during the peak period
• Exam problems often give hourly flows as multipliers of average (e.g. "hour 8 = 2.1× average") — convert to actual MGD first
InputWhere to Get It
Hourly Flows (24 values, MGD)SCADA historian: pull 1-hour average flow values for a representative high-flow weekday. Export to spreadsheet, paste into the 24-hour grid (hour 0 = midnight, hour 12 = noon). If SCADA unavailable: use flow recorder chart paper, totalizer readings taken hourly, or timed bucket measurements. Pick the highest typical flow day — not a rain event unless designing for wet weather.
Preset buttonsUse Residential, Industrial, or Combined presets to populate a typical diurnal pattern scaled to ~1.5 MGD average. Replace with actual SCADA data for any real design. Presets are useful for exam practice and preliminary sizing only.
Safety FactorEngineering design standard — typically 1.25 per TCEQ guidelines. Use 1.50 if: flow data is from dry season only and I/I is unknown, future growth within the permit cycle is expected, or the service area includes significant industrial batch dischargers. Source: TCEQ design manual or project engineer's report.
Discharge Rate (MGD) — optionalThe constant flow rate at which the EQ basin pumps to the treatment plant. Leave blank to automatically use the calculated daily average of your hourly flows. Override if the treatment train has a fixed hydraulic capacity (e.g., biological process requires ≤1.8 MGD) lower than the daily average flow.
Basin Depth (ft)Target water depth for the EQ basin. Used only to calculate surface area — does not affect volume calculation. Typical EQ basins: 10–20 ft SWD. From site constraints (water table depth, soil bearing capacity) or engineer's report. Shallower = more land needed; deeper = more structural cost.
LOAD PRESET:
24-Hour Hourly Flows (MGD) — Hour 0 = midnight
Typical: 1.25–1.50
Blank = use daily average
For surface area calc
Mass diagram method: Plot cumulative flow vs time. Draw a line at the average rate. Max vertical distance between the cumulative curve and the average line = required EQ volume.
—
Load a preset or enter 24-hour flows above...
V_EQ = max(cumulative excess volume) × safety factor | Discharge pump = daily avg flow (constant)

62 Pump Efficiency (Field)

Calculate actual pump efficiency from field-measured output (GPM from totalizer) and power draw (kW from ammeter). Compare to nameplate efficiency to detect impeller wear or bearing degradation. A 10%+ efficiency drop means the pump is working harder for the same output.
PUMP EFFICIENCY (FIELD) — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Actual pump efficiency from real field measurements — not the design curve value. Pumps degrade over time due to impeller wear, bearing friction, and seal drag. This calc finds the actual efficiency so you can compare to nameplate and decide whether the pump needs maintenance or replacement.
Formula Chain
WHP (Water Horsepower) = Flow (GPM) × TDH (ft) ÷ 3960
BHP (Brake Horsepower) = kW × 1.341 (motor input power)
Pump Efficiency = WHP ÷ BHP × 100%
Wire-to-Water Efficiency = WHP ÷ kW_input × 100% (includes motor efficiency)
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#46 TDHProvides the TDH value for this calc. Run #46 first with your static head and friction losses.
#47 Pump HPForward calc: given nameplate efficiency → BHP. This calc is the reverse: given measured power → actual efficiency.
#104 Pump CurveIf efficiency has dropped and flow is lower than expected, use #104 to see whether the operating point has shifted.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• WHP = Q(GPM) × TDH(ft) ÷ 3960 — memorize this form
• BHP = WHP ÷ pump efficiency — efficiency is always ≤ 1.0
• Motor HP = BHP ÷ motor efficiency — two separate efficiencies
• Wire-to-water efficiency = WHP ÷ motor input kW — overall system metric
• New pumps: 70–85% efficient. Below 60% = time to evaluate rebuild vs replace
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPM)Pump discharge flowmeter totalizer: count gallons over a timed period (e.g., 1 minute) or read GPM directly from SCADA flow sensor on pump discharge line.
TDH (ft)From Calc #46 (Total Dynamic Head) — static lift + friction losses. Or read directly from pump discharge pressure gauge: pressure (psi) × 2.31 = ft of head.
Power Draw (kW)Clamp-on ammeter on pump motor leads → measure amps. kW = Volts × Amps × Power Factor × 1.732 (3-phase) ÷ 1000. Or read kW directly from motor control panel power meter if available.
Nameplate Efficiency (%)Pump data sheet or nameplate on motor housing. Typically 70–85% for centrifugal pumps at BEP. Use the efficiency at your expected operating flow, not peak efficiency.
Measured from totalizer or flow sensor
From Calc #46 or pressure gauge
From ammeter or panel power meter
From pump data sheet
WHP = GPM × TDH ÷ 3960 | BHP = kW × 1.341 | Efficiency = WHP ÷ BHP × 100%
—
Enter flow, TDH, and power draw...
Efficiency (%) = WHP ÷ BHP × 100 | WHP = GPM × TDH ÷ 3960 | BHP = kW × 1.341
📋 Compliance & Reporting Calculators

63 Geometric Mean

Calculate geometric mean for E. coli or fecal coliform DMR reporting. TCEQ requires geometric mean — not arithmetic mean. Operators frequently get this wrong.
InputWhere to Get It
Sample 1–7Fecal coliform or E. coli lab results (MPN or CFU/100 mL). Each individual sample result from the reporting period. Lab bench sheet or LIMS report.
Optional
Optional
Optional
Optional
TCEQ Limits: E. coli: 126 CFU/100mL (geomean) / 399 single | Fecal: 200 CFU/100mL (geomean) / 400 single
—
Enter at least 2 samples...
Geomean = (S1 × S2 × ... × Sn) ^ (1/n)

64 Flow-Weighted Composite

Calculate flow-proportional average concentration from grab samples at different flows. Required for accurate DMR reporting at many plants.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow 1–4 (MGD)Individual stream flow meters. Each stream separately metered or calculated.
Conc 1–4 (mg/L)Lab grab or composite result for each corresponding stream.
Optional
Optional
Optional
Optional
—
Enter at least 2 flow-concentration pairs...
FW Avg = Σ(Flow × Conc) ÷ Σ(Flow)
🔄 Quick Conversions

65 Unit Converter

Enter a value in any field to convert. One-stop conversion for common wastewater units.
InputWhere to Get It
Any flow/volume unitEnter your known value in its native unit (from flow meter, permit, or lab) — the calculator converts to all other units simultaneously.
━━ Flow ━━
━━ Volume ━━
━━ Pressure ━━
Enter a value in any field to convert
🔧 Collection Systems Calculators

66 Manning's Equation

Calculate gravity sewer flow from pipe slope, diameter, and roughness. The most tested collection system formula on every TCEQ exam level.
InputWhere to Get It
Pipe Diameter (in)As-built drawings. Stamped on pipe or manhole ring. Collection system maps.
Slope (ft/ft)As-built drawings or survey. Divide drop (ft) by run (ft).
Manning's nMaterial table: PVC=0.009, Concrete=0.013, CMP=0.024. TCEQ design criteria.
MANNING'S EQUATION — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
Manning's equation calculates the velocity and flow rate in a gravity sewer pipe flowing full. It's the foundation of collection system design — how fast wastewater moves through your pipes and how much they can carry. This is the single most-tested collection system formula on TCEQ exams at every level.
Inputs Explained
Diameter (inches) — Inside pipe diameter. Common gravity sewer: 8" (residential lateral), 10–12" (collector), 15–24" (trunk), 30"+ (interceptor). Minimum per TCEQ: 8" for public sewers.
