Slope (ft/ft) — Pipe grade as a decimal. Example: 0.5% slope = 0.005 ft/ft. A pipe dropping 1 foot over 200 feet = 1/200 = 0.005. Get from as-builts or survey. TCEQ minimum slopes: 8" pipe = 0.0040, 10" = 0.0028, 12" = 0.0022.
Manning's n — Roughness coefficient. Lower n = smoother pipe = faster flow. PVC/HDPE: 0.010–0.013. New concrete: 0.013. Old concrete/clay: 0.013–0.015. Corrugated metal: 0.022–0.025. Use 0.013 on TCEQ exams unless told otherwise.
Understanding Velocity Flags
2–10 fps: Good range — Self-cleansing velocity without pipe damage.
<2 fps: Too slow — Solids settle out → blockages, H₂S generation, odors. Increase slope or decrease pipe size to raise velocity.
>10 fps: Too fast — Pipe erosion, joint damage, manhole erosion. Use drop manholes or energy dissipation. Some agencies allow up to 15 fps with reinforced pipe.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• V = (1.486/n) × R^(2/3) × S^(1/2) — memorize this
• For a full pipe: R (hydraulic radius) = D/4 (diameter in feet ÷ 4)
• Q = V × A — flow = velocity × cross-sectional area
• Area of circle: A = π × r² = π/4 × D² (D in feet!)
• Unit trap: Diameter is almost always given in inches — convert to feet first (÷ 12)
• Minimum velocity: 2.0 fps (self-cleansing). They love asking this.
• Default n: Use 0.013 unless the problem specifies otherwise
• To convert CFS to GPM: × 448.83 | To MGD: × 0.6463
Example
12" pipe at 0.5% slope (0.005 ft/ft), n=0.013:
R = (12/12)/4 = 0.25 ft
V = (1.486/0.013) × 0.25^0.667 × 0.005^0.5 = 114.3 × 0.397 × 0.0707 = 3.21 fps ✓
A = π/4 × 1² = 0.785 ft²
Q = 3.21 × 0.785 = 2.52 CFS = 1,131 GPM
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Pipe Diameter (in)As-built drawings. Stamped on pipe or manhole ring. Collection system maps.
Slope (ft/ft)As-built drawings or survey. GIS utility maps. Divide drop (ft) by run (ft) for field calc.
Manning's nMaterial lookup table: PVC=0.009, Concrete=0.013, CMP=0.024. O&M manual or TCEQ design criteria table.
Internal diameter
Rise ÷ Run (e.g. 0.005)
PVC: 0.010 | Concrete: 0.013
n values: PVC/HDPE: 0.010 | New concrete: 0.012 | Old concrete: 0.013–0.015 | Clay/VCP: 0.013 | Corrugated metal: 0.024
—
Enter data...
V = (1.486/n) × R^(2/3) × S^(1/2) | Q = V × A | Full pipe: R = D/4

67 Wet Well Sizing

Calculate required wet well volume from pump capacity and cycle time. Undersized wet wells cause short-cycling and premature pump failure.
InputWhere to Get It
Pump Capacity (GPM)Pump nameplate or pump curve. Design pump rate.
Inflow Rate (GPM)Influent flow meter or calculated from service area demand. SCADA average.
Min Cycle Time (min)TCEQ standard: 6 minutes minimum for motors ≤25 HP. O&M manual or permit.
Single pump flow rate
Average incoming flow
Typical: 10–15 min minimum
Lead-lag configuration
For starts/hr limit lookup Typical: 10–15 min minimum
Design Rules: Min cycle = 10 min (most manufacturers) | V = T × Q_pump ÷ 4 (for constant speed) | Short cycling wears seals, overheats motors
—
Enter data...
V = (T × Q_pump) ÷ 4 | Fill time = V ÷ Inflow | Pump-down = V ÷ (Pump − Inflow)

68 Infiltration & Inflow (I/I)

Calculate infiltration/inflow rate from dry vs wet weather flow. Expressed as GPD per inch-diameter per mile. TCEQ exam staple and major compliance issue in aging Texas systems.
InputWhere to Get It
Wet Weather Flow (GPD)Flow meter during rain event. SCADA peak or average during storm.
Dry Weather Flow (GPD)Flow meter during extended dry period (no rain for 5+ days). SCADA.
Pipe Diameter (in)Collection system as-built drawings or GIS maps.
Pipe Length (miles)GIS system map or as-built drawings. Total length of pipe segment being evaluated.
Peak wet weather daily flow
Average dry weather flow
Sewer pipe diameter
Collection system length
I/I Limits: TCEQ concern: >500 GPD/inch-mile | Excessive: >1,000 | Typical target: <500 GPD/inch-mile
—
Enter data...
I/I = (Wet − Dry) ÷ (Diameter × Miles) GPD/inch-mile

69 Force Main Friction Loss

Hazen-Williams headloss in pressure pipes. Feeds directly into TDH calc (#44). Every lift station design uses this.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (GPM)Design flow or pump capacity from drawings.
C-FactorPipe material: PVC/HDPE=150, Ductile Iron=140, Old Cast Iron=100, Concrete=120.
Pipe Diameter (in)As-built drawings or pipe stamp.
Pipe Length (ft)As-built drawings or field measurement.
Pump flow rate
PVC: 150 | DI: 130 | Old CI: 100
Internal diameter
Total force main length
C values: New PVC/HDPE: 150 | New DI: 130 | Old cast iron: 100 | Concrete: 120 | Add 10–15% for fittings
—
Enter data...
hf = (4.52 × Q^1.85) ÷ (C^1.85 × D^4.87) × L (ft per 100ft × length)

70 Lift Station Capacity DIAGRAM

Calculate lift station wet well sizing, pump runtime, and cycle frequency. Critical for collection system design and TCEQ exams.
InputWhere to Get It
Peak Inflow (GPM)Peak flow factor × average daily flow. Engineering report or TCEQ 30 TAC §217.
Pump Capacity (GPM)Pump nameplate or pump curve at design TDH.
Wet Well Volume (gal)As-built drawings. Tank Volume calc (#66) or wet well dimensions.
Avg Daily Flow (GPD)Flow meter average. SCADA or daily log.
Between on/off levels
Design: Pump ≥ 1.5× peak inflow | Wet well = 10-30 min detention | Target 4-10 cycles/hr
—
Enter lift station data...
Fill Time = V ÷ Q_in | Pump Time = V ÷ (Q_pump - Q_in) | Cycles/hr = 60 ÷ Cycle Time

71 Force Main Velocity DIAGRAM

Calculate velocity in force mains. Check for scouring (min 2 fps) and erosion limits (max 8 fps). Includes travel time for septicity risk.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow Rate (GPM)Pump capacity or flow meter on force main.
Pipe Diameter (in)As-built drawings. Pipe label or collection system maps.
Pipe Length (ft)As-built drawings or GIS measurement.
C FactorPipe material: PVC/HDPE=150, Ductile Iron New=140, Ductile Iron Old=120.
For travel time
PVC=150, DI=140
Velocity: Min 2 fps (scouring) | Optimal 3-5 fps | Max 8 fps | Septicity: Risk if travel > 4 hrs
— fps
Enter force main data...
V = 0.408 × Q ÷ D² | Travel Time = Length ÷ (V × 60)

72 Pump Station Drawdown

Calculate time to pump down wet well. Use for emergency planning and overflow prevention timing.
InputWhere to Get It
Wet Well Volume (gal)As-built drawings or Tank Volume calc (#66).
Pump Rate (GPM)Pump nameplate or pump curve at operating head.
Inflow Rate (GPM)Flow meter. SCADA average or peak reading.
Emergency Storage (gal)As-built drawings. Volume between alarm high and overflow elevation.
Optional
— min
Enter data...
Pump Down = V ÷ (Pump - Inflow) | Fill Time = V ÷ Inflow

73 Sewer Line Capacity

Quick capacity lookup for gravity sewer pipes at TCEQ minimum slopes.
InputWhere to Get It
Pipe DiameterAs-built drawings. Collection system maps or pipe label.
Slope (ft/ft)As-built drawings or field survey. Drop ÷ horizontal distance.
TCEQ Min Slopes: 8"=0.0040 | 10"=0.0028 | 12"=0.0022 | 15"=0.0015 | 18"=0.0012
—
Select size...

74 Sewer Pipe Sizing

Inverse Manning's: given peak design flow, slope, and n-value, find the required pipe diameter. Used for new sewer line design and capacity verification. Complements Calc #53 (Manning's forward calc). TCEQ Class A exam: sewer design problems often ask for required diameter given flow and slope.
SEWER PIPE SIZING — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Required pipe diameter to carry your peak design flow at a given slope with adequate velocity. Manning's equation is solved in reverse — instead of computing flow from a known diameter, this finds the minimum diameter for a target flow. Results are rounded up to the next standard pipe size.
Design Criteria (TCEQ / Ten States Standards)
Minimum velocity (self-cleaning): 2.0 fps at design flow
Maximum velocity: 10 fps (erosion limit for concrete), 15 fps (PVC)
Minimum pipe size: 8 inches for public sewers (TCEQ 30 TAC 217)
Minimum slope for 8": 0.0022 ft/ft (TCEQ minimum for 2 fps)
Typical Manning's n: PVC = 0.009, Concrete = 0.013, CMP = 0.024
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#53 Manning'sForward calc: given D, slope, n → finds flow and velocity. Use #53 to verify this calc's output or check capacity at d/D ≠ 1.0.
#55 I/II/I analysis gives peak wet weather flow input for this calc. Size pipes for peak wet weather, not just dry weather average.
#76 Sewer CapacityQuick capacity lookup for standard TCEQ minimum-slope pipes. Use for checking existing pipes; use this calc for designing new ones.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Manning's: Q = (1.486/n) × A × R^(2/3) × S^(1/2) — full pipe
• R = D/4 for full circular pipe (NOT D/2) — most common exam trap
• Design at full-pipe capacity, operate at 75–80% (d/D = 0.75–0.80 for max velocity)
• Minimum 8" public sewer per TCEQ 30 TAC 217.52
• Peaking factor for residential: 2.5–4.0× average daily flow
InputWhere to Get It
Design Flow (MGD or GPM)Peak design flow including peaking factor. Residential: avg daily flow × 2.5–4.0 peak factor. From Calc #55 for wet weather flow or population projection × 100 GPD/person × peaking factor.
Slope (ft/ft)From site survey (topographic map or field measurement). Minimum slope for self-cleaning per TCEQ: 0.0022 for 8", 0.0015 for 10", 0.0012 for 12". Steeper slopes can cause excessive velocity — check upper limit.
Manning's nPVC/plastic: 0.009–0.010. New concrete: 0.013. Old/rough concrete: 0.015. Corrugated metal pipe (CMP): 0.024. From pipe manufacturer specifications or TCEQ design manual.
Min Velocity (fps)TCEQ minimum: 2.0 fps for self-cleaning. Default auto-applied. Override only if local design standard differs.
MGD
e.g. 0.005 = 0.5%
PVC: 0.009 | Concrete: 0.013
Standard sizes: 8" · 10" · 12" · 15" · 18" · 21" · 24" · 27" · 30" · 36" · 42" · 48"
—
Enter design flow, slope, and n...
Q = (1.486/n) × A × R^(2/3) × S^(1/2) | R = D/4 (full pipe) | Solve for D iteratively | Round up to next standard size
📏 Flow Measurement Calculators

75 Weir Flow

Calculate flow over rectangular, V-notch (90°), or Cipolletti weirs. On every single TCEQ exam at every level.
InputWhere to Get It
Weir TypeVisual inspection and as-built drawings. Rectangular, V-notch, or Cipolletti.
Head over Weir (ft)Staff gauge upstream of weir. Float/pressure sensor. SCADA.
Weir Length (ft)As-built drawings or field measurement of crest length.
Measured upstream of weir
Not needed for V-notch
Formulas: Rect: Q = 3.33 × L × H^1.5 | V-notch 90°: Q = 2.5 × H^2.5 | Cipolletti: Q = 3.367 × L × H^1.5 | Q in CFS
—
Enter data...
Suppressed rect: Q = 3.33LH^1.5 | Contracted: Q = 3.33(L−0.2H)H^1.5

76 Area-Velocity Flow

Q = A × V for open channels and pipes. Used for field flow verification with portable meters. Tested constantly on TCEQ exams.
InputWhere to Get It
Channel TypeVisual inspection and as-built drawings.
Velocity (fps)Electromagnetic or Doppler velocity meter. Float timing for rough estimate.
Diameter / Width (ft)As-built drawings or field measurement.
Depth (ft)Staff gauge or ultrasonic level sensor. SCADA.
Measured or calculated
Pipe dia (in) or channel width (ft)
Water depth in channel
—
Enter data...
Q (CFS) = Area (ft²) × Velocity (fps) | GPM = CFS × 448.83
🪨 Trickling Filter Calculators

77 TF Efficiency (NRC) DIAGRAM

National Research Council equation for single-stage trickling filter BOD removal. Guaranteed TCEQ exam question at Class B and above.
InputWhere to Get It
BOD Load (lbs/day)Influent BOD (mg/L) × Flow (MGD) × 8.34. Composite sample + flow meter.
Filter Volume (1,000 ft³)As-built drawings. π/4 × D² × depth for circular filters. O&M manual.
Recirculation RatioRecirculation pump flow ÷ influent flow. Pump meter or pump curve × runtime.
NRC TRICKLING FILTER EFFICIENCY — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
The NRC (National Research Council) equation predicts BOD removal efficiency for a trickling filter based on organic loading and recirculation. It was developed from WWII-era military installations and is still the standard design equation used by TCEQ. This is a guaranteed exam question at Class B and above.
Inputs Explained
Flow (MGD) — Average daily influent flow to the trickling filter (not including recirculation).
Influent BOD (mg/L) — BOD concentration entering the TF. If there's a primary clarifier upstream, use the primary effluent BOD (typically 60% of raw = ~120 mg/L).
Filter Volume (1000 ft³) — Total media volume in thousands of cubic feet. A 60 ft diameter × 6 ft deep TF = π/4 × 60² × 6 = 16,965 ft³ = 16.965 (enter 16.965). Use Calc #66 for circular volume.
Recirculation Ratio (R) — Recirculated flow ÷ influent flow. R=0 means no recirculation. R=1 means recirculating equal to influent (2× total flow over the TF). Typical: 0.5–2.0.
How the NRC Equation Works
The equation has two parts:
1. W/(V×F) — BOD load applied vs filter capacity. W = lbs BOD/day, V = volume in 1000 ft³, F = recirculation factor.
2. Recirculation factor F = (1+R)² / (1+0.1R)² — Recirculation improves efficiency by giving organics multiple passes through the biofilm.
Higher recirculation → higher F → lower W/(VF) → higher efficiency.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• E = 1 / [1 + 0.4432 × √(W/(V×F))]
• W = Flow (MGD) × BOD (mg/L) × 8.34 = lbs BOD/day
• F = (1+R)² / (1+0.1R)² — recirculation factor
• Volume is in thousands of ft³ — don't forget to divide by 1000
• Typical efficiency: 65–85%. Below 65% means the filter is overloaded or media is clogged
• If they ask "what is the effluent BOD?" → Effluent = Influent × (1 − E)
• Common trick: giving you diameter and depth instead of volume — calculate V = π/4 × D² × depth first
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
BOD Load (lbs/day)Influent BOD (mg/L) × Flow (MGD) × 8.34. From composite sample and flow meter.
Filter Volume (1,000 ft³)As-built drawings. π/4 × D² × depth for circular filters. O&M manual specs.
Recirculation RatioRecirculation pump flow ÷ influent flow. Pump meter or pump curve × runtime.
Applied BOD to filter
In thousands of ft³
R = recirc flow ÷ influent flow
NRC: E = 1 ÷ [1 + 0.4432 × √(W/VF)] | F = (1+R) ÷ (1+0.1R)² | W = BOD lbs/day | V = 1000 ft³ | Typical: 65–85% removal
—
Enter data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
E = 1 ÷ [1 + 0.4432 × √(W ÷ (V × F))] × 100

78 TF Hydraulic Loading

GPD/ft² applied to filter surface. Determines filter classification: low-rate, standard, high-rate, super-high-rate.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter.
Filter Diameter (ft)As-built drawings.
Influent BOD (mg/L)Composite influent sample lab result.
Filter Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Measure from media surface to underdrain.
Including recirculation
Circular filter
For organic loading check
Media depth
Classifications: Low-rate: 25–100 GPD/ft² | Standard: 100–300 | High-rate: 300–1000 | Super-high: 1000–2000
—
Enter data...
Hyd Load = Flow (GPD) ÷ Filter Area (ft²) | Org Load = BOD lbs/day ÷ Volume (1000 ft³)
🌊 Lagoon & Pond Calculators

79 Pond BOD Loading

Calculate lbs BOD/acre/day for waste stabilization ponds. Primary design parameter. Overloading causes odor complaints and TCEQ violations.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter.
BOD (mg/L)Composite influent sample lab result.
Pond Area (acres)As-built drawings or GIS survey. O&M manual.
Pond Depth (ft)Staff gauge or design depth from as-built drawings.
Average daily flow
Influent BOD
Water surface area
Average operating depth
Texas Limits: Facultative: 35–50 lbs BOD/acre/day | Aerated: 50–200+ | HRT: 90–180 days typical | Depth: 3–8 ft
—
Enter data...
Loading = (Flow × BOD × 8.34) ÷ Acres | HRT = (Acres × 43,560 × Depth × 7.48) ÷ (Flow × 1,000,000)
💨 Aeration Calculators

80 SOTR → AOTR

Convert manufacturer's Standard Oxygen Transfer Rate to Actual conditions using alpha, beta, temperature, altitude, and operating DO. This is how you actually size blowers.
InputWhere to Get It
SOTR (lbs O₂/hr)Aeration equipment nameplate or manufacturer submittal. Engineering design report.
Alpha (α)Manufacturer data. Typical: 0.4–0.8 (fine bubble), 0.6–1.2 (surface). O&M manual.
Beta (β)Manufacturer data. Typical 0.95–0.98 for municipal wastewater. O&M manual.
Temperature (°C)Basin water temperature probe. Daily log or SCADA.
Operating DO (mg/L)Target DO setpoint. Usually 2.0 mg/L BOD removal, 2.0–4.0 mg/L nitrification.
Altitude (ft)USGS topographic map, Google Earth, or GPS at plant site.
SOTR TO AOTR — OPERATOR GUIDE
Why This Conversion Matters
Manufacturers rate their aeration equipment under standard conditions: clean water, 20°C, zero DO, sea level. Your plant operates in wastewater at a different temperature, with dissolved solids, at some DO setpoint, and possibly at altitude. The AOTR (Actual Oxygen Transfer Rate) is always lower than SOTR — often 40–60% of the manufacturer's number. If you size blowers using SOTR, you'll be undersized.
Inputs Explained
SOTR (lbs O₂/hr) — From the manufacturer's spec sheet or performance test. This is the clean-water rating.
Alpha (α) — Wastewater correction factor. Accounts for surfactants and contaminants that reduce O₂ transfer. Range: 0.4–0.8. Use 0.5 for fine bubble diffusers in typical municipal WW, 0.6–0.8 for coarse bubble. Lower α = dirtier wastewater.
Beta (β) — Salinity/dissolved solids correction. Reduces O₂ saturation. Range: 0.90–0.98. Use 0.95 for typical municipal. Lower for industrial/saline waste.
Temperature (°C) — Mixed liquor temperature. Higher temp = less dissolved O₂ capacity but faster biological rates. Houston summer: 28–32°C. Winter: 15–18°C.
Operating DO (mg/L) — Your basin DO setpoint. Typical CAS: 2.0 mg/L. MBR: 1.0–2.0. Nitrification: ≥2.0. Higher DO = less driving force = less transfer.
Altitude (ft) — Elevation above sea level. Higher altitude = lower atmospheric pressure = lower O₂ saturation. Houston: ~50 ft (negligible). Denver: 5,280 ft (significant correction).
Reading the Results
The AOTR/SOTR ratio tells you what fraction of the manufacturer's rating you actually get. Typical: 30–60%. If your ratio is below 30%, double-check your alpha and temperature — something may be wrong with your wastewater characteristics or you have an industrial slug.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• AOTR = SOTR × α × [(β × Cs_T − DO) / Cs_20] × θ^(T−20)
• θ (theta) = 1.024 — temperature correction constant. Memorize this.
• Cs_20 = 9.09 mg/L (O₂ saturation at 20°C, sea level)
• α always makes AOTR smaller (α < 1)
• Higher operating DO → smaller driving force → less transfer
• This calc feeds directly into Blower Air Requirement (Calc #65)
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
SOTR (lbs O₂/hr)Aeration equipment nameplate or manufacturer submittal. Engineering design report.
Alpha (α)Manufacturer data or site-specific testing. Typical: 0.4–0.8 (fine bubble), 0.6–1.2 (surface). O&M manual.
Beta (β)Manufacturer data. Typical 0.95–0.98 for municipal wastewater. O&M manual or engineering report.
Temperature (°C)Basin water temperature probe. Daily log or SCADA. Take in aeration basin, not influent.
Operating DO (mg/L)Target DO setpoint. Usually 2.0 mg/L for BOD removal, 2.0–4.0 mg/L for nitrification.
Altitude (ft)USGS topographic map, Google Earth elevation, or GPS reading at plant site.
From manufacturer specs
Wastewater factor: 0.4–0.8
Salinity factor: 0.90–0.98
Wastewater temperature
Target basin DO
Houston ≈ 50 ft
Corrections: α: fine bubble 0.5–0.7, coarse 0.6–0.9 | β: municipal 0.95–0.98, industrial 0.80–0.95 | CS at 20°C = 9.09 mg/L
—
Enter data...
AOTR = SOTR × α × [(β × CS_alt × θ^(T-20) − DO) ÷ CS_20] | θ = 1.024

81 Blower Air Requirement

Calculate required SCFM from oxygen demand, basin depth, and transfer efficiency. Connects your AOTR (Calc #59) to actual blower sizing. Aeration = 50–65% of plant energy cost.
InputWhere to Get It
Oxygen Demand (lbs O₂/hr)From SOTR→AOTR calc (#62), or BOD load × 1.5 lb O₂/lb BOD estimate.
Diffuser Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Distance from water surface to diffuser centerline.
O₂ Transfer Efficiency (%)Manufacturer's clean water test data. Fine bubble: 20–40%. Coarse: 6–10%.
Number of BasinsCount from site drawings. Only count basins in active service.
Safety FactorEngineering design standard — typically 1.25. Check design engineer's report.
Energy Cost ($/kWh)Electric utility bill. Most plants pay $0.08–$0.14/kWh.
BLOWER AIR REQUIREMENT — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
This converts your oxygen demand (lbs O₂/hr) into the actual volume of air your blowers need to deliver (SCFM). Air is only ~23% oxygen, and diffusers only transfer a fraction of that O₂ into the water, so you need much more air than you might think. This calculator also estimates motor horsepower and annual energy cost — since aeration is 50–65% of a typical plant's electric bill.
Inputs Explained
Oxygen Demand (lbs O₂/hr) — Your AOTR from Calc #59, or calculated as: (Flow MGD × BOD removed mg/L × 8.34) ÷ 24 hours. Include nitrification demand if applicable (4.6 lbs O₂ per lb NH₃-N oxidized).
Diffuser Depth (ft) — Submergence depth of the diffusers. Deeper = higher back-pressure but better OTE. Typical: 12–18 ft.
OTE (%) — Oxygen Transfer Efficiency. Fine bubble diffusers: 25–35% (at 15 ft depth, clean water). Coarse bubble: 6–12%. Mechanical surface: varies. Use actual field-tested OTE if available — clean-water OTE is higher than process conditions.
Number of Basins — SCFM is divided equally between parallel basins for per-basin blower sizing.
Safety Factor — Peak:average O₂ demand ratio. Accounts for diurnal load variation and peak events. Typical: 1.5 for municipal, 2.0 for industrial.
Energy Cost ($/kWh) — Your utility rate. Texas average: $0.08–0.12/kWh. Enables annual cost projection.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• Air is 23.2% O₂ by weight, 0.075 lbs/ft³ at standard conditions
• O₂ per ft³ of air = 0.075 × 0.232 = 0.0174 lbs
• SCFM = O₂ demand (lbs/min) ÷ (0.0174 × OTE fraction)
• BHP = relates to SCFM and discharge pressure
• Motor HP is typically next standard size above BHP ÷ motor efficiency
• Higher OTE = less air needed = smaller blower = lower energy cost
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Oxygen Demand (lbs O₂/hr)From SOTR→AOTR calc (#62), or: BOD load × 1.5 lb O₂/lb BOD as rough estimate. Engineering design report.
Diffuser Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Distance from water surface to diffuser centerline. O&M manual specs.
O₂ Transfer Efficiency (%)Manufacturer's clean water test data. Submittal documents or O&M manual. Fine bubble: 20–40%. Coarse: 6–10%.
Number of BasinsCount from site drawings or observation. Only count basins in active service.
Safety FactorEngineering design standard — typically 1.25. Check your design engineer's report.
Energy Cost ($/kWh)Electric utility bill. Most plants pay $0.08–$0.14/kWh. Accounts/finance can provide rate.
AOTR from Calc #62
Submergence depth
Fine bubble: 20-35% | Coarse: 6-12%
Parallel aeration basins
Peak:Avg (1.3–2.0 typical)
For annual cost estimate
Standard=68°F. Hot air reduces capacity.
Centrifugal: 65–75%
OTE by type: Fine bubble: 25–35% at 15 ft | Coarse bubble: 6–12% | Mechanical surface: 1.5–3.5 lbs O₂/hp-hr | Air is 23.2% O₂ by weight (0.075 lbs/ft³ × 0.232)
—
Enter data...
SCFM = (O₂ demand ÷ (0.075 × 0.232 × OTE/100)) ÷ 60 | BHP = SCFM × ΔP / (6356 × eff)
⚡ FIELD EFFICIENCY CHECK
From field measurements (ammeter + flow sensor): compare actual vs theoretical BHP to detect blower degradation. A >15% gap = impeller wear, bearing drag, or inlet filter clogging.
From panel ammeter or power meter
From inline air flow meter
From discharge pressure gauge
From blower data sheet
—
Enter measured kW, SCFM, and discharge pressure...
Actual BHP = kW × 1.341 | Theoretical BHP = SCFM × P₁ × [n/(n-1)] × [(P₂/P₁)^((n-1)/n)−1] ÷ (229) | Efficiency = Theoretical ÷ Actual × 100%
🌊 Receiving Water / Discharge

82 Dilution / Mixing Zone

Calculate downstream concentration from effluent and stream mixing. Used for TPDES permit compliance and in-stream standards verification.
InputWhere to Get It
Effluent Flow (MGD)Effluent flow meter. SCADA average.
Effluent Conc (mg/L)Effluent composite sample lab result for the parameter of interest.
Stream Flow (MGD)7Q10 flow from TCEQ permit or USGS stream gage. Permit specifies design flow condition.
Upstream Conc (mg/L)Upstream grab sample or TCEQ background concentration from permit.
Discharge flow
Effluent parameter value
Upstream flow (7Q2 for permit)
Background concentration
TPDES: Use 7Q2 (lowest 7-day avg with 2-yr return) for critical low flow | Dilution ratio = Stream flow ÷ Effluent flow
—
Enter data...
C_mix = (Q_eff × C_eff + Q_stream × C_stream) ÷ (Q_eff + Q_stream)
👥 Population & Design

83 Population Equivalent

Comprehensive PE analysis: flow, BOD, TSS, and TKN population equivalents with per-capita comparison, industrial load detection, EDU conversion, peaking factor design, capacity projections, and TCEQ-ready reporting summary.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter average. SCADA or daily log. Use recent 30-day average.
Influent BOD (mg/L)Composite sample lab result. Average recent 12 months for planning.
Influent TSS (mg/L)Composite sample lab result. Average recent 12 months.
Influent TKN (mg/L)Composite sample lab result. Less common — check permit.
Actual PopulationCity/county census data, utility billing records, or water system count.
Permitted Flow (MGD)TPDES permit Page 1 (design flow). Also in facility engineering report.
Annual Growth Rate (%)City planning department, utility master plan, or historical connection data.
Peaking FactorEngineering report or TCEQ 30 TAC §217. Typical: 2.5–4.0 for small systems.
📖 POPULATION EQUIVALENT — OPERATOR GUIDE
What is Population Equivalent (PE)?
PE converts plant loading into an equivalent number of people producing that wastewater. It tells you how big your plant's workload is in human terms — even if your actual population is different. A PE higher than your actual population means you have industrial or commercial contributors.
Step 1 — Enter Plant Loading (minimum: Flow)
Flow (MGD) — Your average daily flow from the plant flowmeter. Use the monthly average from your DMR, not a single day's reading.
BOD (mg/L) — Influent BOD₅ from your composite sampler. Use your monthly average.
TSS (mg/L) — Influent TSS from composite. Typical domestic: 200–250 mg/L.
TKN (mg/L) — Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (ammonia + organic N). Typical domestic: 30–40 mg/L. Leave blank if you don't monitor TKN.
💡 The more parameters you enter, the more complete your analysis. Flow alone gives a basic PE; all four gives a full characterization.
Step 2 — Enter Service Area Info (optional but powerful)
Actual Population — Number of people your system serves. Get this from your utility's billing records or census data. This unlocks per-capita comparison bars showing if your system matches expected loading.
Permitted Flow (MGD) — Your TPDES permit's maximum daily average flow. Found on page 1 of your permit. This unlocks the capacity analysis with TCEQ 75% trigger tracking.
Growth Rate (%) — Annual population growth for your service area. Check your city's comprehensive plan or TWDB data. Typical Texas suburban: 2–5%/yr. This projects when you'll hit capacity milestones.
Peaking Factor — Ratio of peak hourly flow to average daily flow. Small systems (<1,000 pop): 3.5–4.0. Medium (10,000): 2.0–2.5. Large (100,000+): 1.5–2.0. This sizes your wet weather capacity.
Understanding Your Results
▸ PE Comparison Gauges
Four horizontal bars showing PE from each parameter. The highest one is your Governing PE — this is the one TCEQ cares about. If BOD PE is much higher than Flow PE, you likely have industrial discharge adding organic load without proportional flow.
▸ EDU (Equivalent Dwelling Units)
Governing PE ÷ 3.5 people per household. This is the number developers and planners use for impact fees and capacity allocation. If a developer wants to connect 200 new homes, that's ~700 PE of new load.
▸ Industrial vs Domestic Split
A stacked bar estimating how much of your BOD load comes from industrial/commercial sources vs. residential. If your system is >15% industrial, you may need a pretreatment program (40 CFR 403).
▸ Wastewater Ratios
BOD:TSS — Normal domestic is 0.7–1.2. Above 1.2 = high organic strength (food processing?). Below 0.7 = high inert solids (construction I/I?).
TKN:BOD — Normal domestic is 0.12–0.25. Above 0.25 = nitrogen-rich (septic hauling, food waste). Below 0.12 = industrial dilution.
▸ Per Capita Loading Bars
Compares your actual per-person loading against national standards. The vertical line is the standard. Green = near standard. Yellow = elevated (industrial contributor?). Blue = below standard (I/I dilution?).
▸ Capacity Analysis
Shows current utilization against your TPDES permit. TCEQ requires a capacity planning report when you hit 75% of permitted flow (30 TAC §217.33). The bar marks 75% and 90% milestones. Red = you need to act now.
▸ Growth Timeline
Visual projection showing when your plant hits 75%, 90%, and 100% of permitted capacity based on growth rate. The projection table below shows flow, capacity %, and population at 5, 10, and 20 years. Use this for CIP budgeting and expansion planning.
▸ TCEQ Report Summary
A monospace text block formatted for direct copy-paste into capacity planning reports, engineering memos, or TCEQ submittals. Click "Copy TCEQ Report Summary" to put it on your clipboard.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• Memorize: 100 GPD/person, 0.17 lbs BOD/person/day, 8.34 lbs/gal conversion
• Formula: PE = (Flow MGD × BOD mg/L × 8.34) ÷ 0.17
• Shortcut: At 200 mg/L BOD and 100 GPD/cap, both methods give the same PE
• Watch for: Questions that give you flow in GPD vs MGD — don't double-convert
• Trick question: If PE from BOD >> PE from flow, the answer is "industrial contribution"
• 75% rule: TCEQ requires planning report at 75% of permitted capacity
Real-World Example
Scenario: Your plant treats 1.5 MGD with 200 mg/L BOD, 220 mg/L TSS, serving 15,000 people. Permit = 2.0 MGD. Growth = 3%/yr.

Enter: Flow: 1.5 | BOD: 200 | TSS: 220 | Pop: 15000 | Permit: 2.0 | Growth: 3.0

Result: Flow PE = 15,000 | BOD PE = 14,706 | TSS PE = 13,761 → Governing: Flow at 15,000 PE. Balanced domestic character. At 75% capacity. TCEQ planning report required. At 3% growth, you'll hit 100% in ~10 years.
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter average. SCADA report or daily log. Use recent 30-day or 12-month average.
Influent BOD (mg/L)Composite sample lab result. Average recent 12 months for planning purposes.
Influent TSS (mg/L)Composite sample lab result. Average recent 12 months.
Influent TKN (mg/L)Composite sample lab result (TKN = NH₃ + organic N). Less common — check permit.
Actual PopulationCity/county census data, utility billing records, or water system customer count.
Permitted Flow (MGD)TPDES permit Page 1 (design flow). Also in facility engineering report.
Annual Growth Rate (%)City planning department, utility master plan, or historical connection data.
Peaking FactorEngineering report or TCEQ design criteria (30 TAC §217). Typical: 2.5–4.0 for small systems.
━━ Plant Loading ━━
Average daily flow
Influent BOD5
Total suspended solids
Total Kjeldahl nitrogen
━━ Service Area ━━
Current served population
TCEQ permitted capacity
Population growth rate
Peak:Avg ratio (2.0-4.0 typical)
Per Capita Standards: Flow: 100 GPD | BOD: 0.17 lbs/day | TSS: 0.20 lbs/day | TKN: 0.03 lbs/day | EDU: 3.5 persons/unit | Peaking: 2.0-4.0 (smaller systems peak higher)
—
Enter data...
PE(flow) = GPD / 100 | PE(BOD) = lbs BOD / 0.17 | PE(TSS) = lbs TSS / 0.20 | PE(TKN) = lbs TKN / 0.03 | EDU = PE / 3.5

84 Parshall Flume DIAGRAM

Calculate flow through a Parshall flume from throat width and upstream head (Ha). Standard flow measurement device in wastewater — on every TCEQ exam. Supports free-flow and submerged correction.
InputWhere to Get It
Throat WidthStamped on the flume itself or as-built drawings.
Ha — Upstream Head (ft)Staff gauge or ultrasonic sensor in stilling well. SCADA or chart recorder.
Hb — Downstream Head (ft)Only needed if submerged (>70%). Downstream gauge or pressure transducer.
PARSHALL FLUME — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
A Parshall flume is a specially shaped open-channel flow measurement structure. Water accelerates through a narrowed throat section, and the upstream water level (Ha) directly correlates to flow rate. You read Ha from a staff gauge or ultrasonic sensor, and this calculator converts that reading to GPM/MGD.
Inputs Explained
Throat Width — The narrowest section of the flume. This is a fixed dimension — stamped on the flume nameplate or in your O&M manual. Common WW sizes: 6"–24" for small plants, 3'–8' for large plants.
Ha (Upstream Head) — Water level measured at the Ha stilling well, located 2/3 of the way back from the throat in the converging section. Read in feet from the flume floor (not from the channel floor). This is your primary measurement — accuracy here is everything.
Hb (Downstream Head) — Optional. Measured in the diverging section downstream of the throat. Only needed to check for submergence. If Hb/Ha exceeds the submergence limit, the standard equation is invalid and a correction factor must be applied.
Free-Flow vs Submerged
Free-flow — Water drops through the throat freely. Ha alone determines flow. This is normal operation.
Submerged — Downstream water backs up into the throat. Occurs when Hb/Ha exceeds the limit (0.60 for large flumes, 0.70 for >12"). Submergence means your flow reading is too high. Fix: clear downstream blockages, check for debris, or apply submergence correction tables.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• Q = C × Ha^n — C and n depend on throat width
• They'll give you Ha and throat width — plug into the equation
• Ha is measured in feet — watch for questions giving inches
• Free-flow only needs Ha. Submerged needs both Ha and Hb
• Most common exam flume sizes: 6", 9", 12"
• A Parshall flume has a CONVERGING section, THROAT, and DIVERGING section
• Unlike a weir, a Parshall flume is self-cleaning (no upstream sediment buildup)
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Throat WidthStamped on the flume itself or as-built drawings. Standard USBR sizes (3", 6", 9", 1', 2', etc.).
Ha — Upstream Head (ft)Staff gauge or ultrasonic/float sensor in the converging section stilling well. SCADA or chart recorder.
Hb — Downstream Head (ft)Only needed if submerged (>70% submergence). Downstream gauge or pressure transducer.
Standard Parshall throat sizes
Measured at 2/3 A from throat
Optional — for submergence check
Free-flow formula: Q = C × Ha^n (CFS) | C and n vary by throat width | Submerged when Hb/Ha > 0.70 (small) or > 0.60 (large) — correction required
—
Enter data...
Q (CFS) = C × Ha^n | GPM = CFS × 448.83 | MGD = GPM × 0.00144
📐 Tank Geometry

85 Tank Volume

Calculate volume for circular, rectangular, and cone-bottom tanks in gallons, ft³, and m³. The most-used field calculator — feeds every volume-based calc in the app.
InputWhere to Get It
Tank ShapeVisual inspection and as-built drawings.
Diameter / Length (ft)As-built drawings. Measure with tape for field verification.
Width (ft)As-built drawings. Rectangular basins only.
Sidewall Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Measure from liquid surface to floor at wall.
Cone Depth (ft)As-built drawings for cone-bottom tanks.
TANK VOLUME — OPERATOR GUIDE
Why You Need This
Tank volume is the starting input for HRT, SRT, F:M, chemical dosing, and pump-down time. If you don't know your tank volume, you can't calculate anything else. This calculator handles every common WW tank geometry and gives you conversions in every unit you'll need.
Choosing the Right Shape
Circular — Clarifiers, digesters, SBR basins, equalization tanks. Enter the inside diameter and water depth (SWD).
Rectangular — Aeration basins, chlorine contact chambers, grit channels. Enter length, width, and water depth.
Cone-Bottom — Gravity thickeners, clarifier sludge hoppers, some digesters. Enter diameter, straight sidewall depth, PLUS the cone depth. The calculator adds cylinder + cone volumes.
Tips for Accurate Measurements
Use sidewall depth (SWD) — the actual water depth, not the tank wall height. SWD is usually 1–3 feet less than wall height due to freeboard. For cone-bottom tanks, measure cone depth separately from the straight-wall portion. Get dimensions from as-built drawings when possible — field tape measurements are less accurate for large tanks.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• Memorize: 1 ft³ = 7.48 gallons. This shows up constantly.
• Circle area = π × r² = 0.785 × D² (they love 0.785)
• Rectangle volume = L × W × D (easy)
• Cone volume = (π/3) × r² × h (1/3 of a cylinder)
• To get from gallons to MG: ÷ 1,000,000
• Common trap: giving diameter in inches. Always convert to feet first.
• Common trap: asking for gallons but giving dimensions that naturally give ft³
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Tank ShapeVisual inspection and as-built drawings. Circular clarifiers, rectangular basins, cone-bottom digesters.
Diameter / Length (ft)As-built drawings. Measure with tape for field verification. Usually stamped in O&M manual specs.
Width (ft)As-built drawings. Rectangular basins only.
Sidewall Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Measure from liquid surface to floor at wall. Staff gauge helps.
Cone Depth (ft)As-built drawings for cone-bottom tanks. Measured separately from sidewall depth.
Select tank geometry
Diameter (circ/cone) or Length (rect)
Leave blank for circular
Water depth (SWD)
Depth of cone section
Conversions: 1 ft³ = 7.48 gal | 1 m³ = 264.17 gal | Circle area = π × r² | Cone vol = π/3 × r² × h
—
Enter data...
Circ: V = π × r² × d | Rect: V = L × W × D | Cone: V = (π/3) × r² × h | gal = ft³ × 7.48

86 Pond Detention Time

Calculate actual hydraulic retention time accounting for sludge accumulation reducing effective volume. Ponds lose 5–15% of volume per decade from sludge buildup — your actual HRT may be much shorter than design.
InputWhere to Get It
Pond Area (acres)As-built drawings or survey. GIS aerial measurement. O&M manual.
Design Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Permitted operating depth. Staff gauge at deepest point.
Sludge Depth (ft)Sludge judge measurement from pond bank. Record quarterly.
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA or daily log.
Number of CellsCount from site drawings or physical observation.
POND DETENTION TIME — OPERATOR GUIDE
Why Sludge Depth Matters
Every lagoon accumulates sludge on the bottom — dead algae, settled solids, and inorganic grit. A pond designed for 5 ft depth with 1.5 ft of sludge only has 3.5 ft of effective water. That's 30% less volume, 30% less HRT, and 30% less treatment. Many lagoon permit violations are caused by operators not knowing their actual sludge depth.
Inputs Explained
Pond Area (acres) — Water surface area. Get from as-builts, aerial imagery, or measure the dike-to-dike dimensions and subtract for slope.
Design Depth (ft) — Original operating depth from design documents. Typical: 3–5 ft (facultative), 8–15 ft (aerated).
Sludge Depth (ft) — Measure with a sludge judge or core sampler at multiple points and average. If unknown, assume 0.5–1.0 ft per decade of operation. Enter 0 for a new or recently dredged pond.
Flow (MGD) — Average daily influent flow. Use your monthly DMR average.
Number of Cells — Ponds in series. A 3-cell system has better plug-flow behavior than a single cell, meaning less short-circuiting and more effective HRT.
Short-Circuiting
The actual effective HRT is always less than the calculated HRT because water doesn't flow uniformly through the pond — it short-circuits from inlet to outlet. A single-cell pond may only use ~30% of its volume effectively. Adding baffles or cells in series dramatically improves performance.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• HRT (days) = Volume (gal) ÷ Flow (GPD)
• Volume = Acres × 43,560 ft²/acre × Depth (ft) × 7.48 gal/ft³
• Facultative pond design HRT: 90–180 days
• Aerated lagoon HRT: 3–20 days
• Polishing/maturation pond: 10–30 days
• 1 acre-foot = 43,560 ft³ = 325,851 gallons
• Questions may give volume in acre-feet — multiply by 325,851 for gallons
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Pond Area (acres)As-built drawings or survey. Can also calculate from GIS aerial. O&M manual.
Design Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Permitted operating depth. Staff gauge at deepest point.
Sludge Depth (ft)Sludge judge measurement from pond bank. Record quarterly. Subtract from design depth.
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA or daily log. Use average daily flow.
Number of CellsCount from site drawings or physical observation. Each berm-separated pond = 1 cell.
Water surface area
Original operating depth
Accumulated sludge (0 if new)
Average daily flow
Ponds in series
Design targets: Facultative: 90–180 days | Aerated: 3–20 days | Polishing: 10–30 days | Short-circuiting reduces effective HRT by 30–50% in single-cell systems
—
Enter data...
HRT = Volume (gal) ÷ Flow (GPD) | Vol = Acres × 43,560 × Effective Depth × 7.48
🌱 Biosolids & Land Application

87 Plant Available Nitrogen

Calculate Plant Available Nitrogen (PAN) from biosolids nitrogen fractions and mineralization rates. PAN determines the agronomic application rate — apply too much and you contaminate groundwater, too little and the farmer complains.
InputWhere to Get It
NH₃-N (mg/kg dry)Biosolids lab analysis report. Required by 40 CFR 503.
NO₃-N (mg/kg dry)Biosolids lab analysis report. Often near zero in anaerobic biosolids.
Organic N (mg/kg dry)Biosolids lab analysis. TKN − NH₃-N = Organic N.
Application MethodYour land application plan or 503 permit.
Mineralization Rate (%)503 guidance: 20% default for Class B. O&M manual or agronomist.
% SolidsBiosolids lab analysis (total solids test). Log or lab report.
PLANT AVAILABLE NITROGEN — OPERATOR GUIDE
What is PAN?
Not all nitrogen in biosolids is immediately available to plants. PAN (Plant Available Nitrogen) is the fraction that crops can actually use in the first growing season. It includes ammonia (with volatilization losses), nitrate (100% available), and a fraction of organic nitrogen that mineralizes. PAN is the legal basis for determining how much biosolids you can land-apply — apply more N than the crop can use and you risk groundwater contamination.
Inputs Explained
NH₃-N (mg/kg dry) — Ammonia nitrogen from your biosolids lab analysis. Immediately plant-available but some is lost to volatilization (evaporation to air) during surface application.
NO₃-N (mg/kg dry) — Nitrate nitrogen. 100% plant available. Usually a small fraction in biosolids but can be significant in aerobically digested material.
Organic N (mg/kg dry) — Calculated as TKN minus NH₃-N. This is nitrogen locked in organic matter that slowly becomes available as microbes break it down (mineralization). Only a percentage converts in Year 1.
Application Method — How you apply affects ammonia loss. Injected/incorporated within 24 hrs: 0% NH₃ loss (best). Surface applied: 50% of NH₃ is lost to volatilization.
Mineralization Rate (%) — What fraction of organic N converts to plant-available form in Year 1. Depends on how the biosolids were treated: Aerobically digested: 30%. Anaerobically digested: 20%. Composted: 10%. Raw/unstabilized: 40%.
% Solids — Total solids of your biosolids. Liquid: 2–6%. Dewatered cake: 15–30%. Dried: 80%+. Needed to convert between dry and wet tons.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• PAN = (Kvol × NH₃-N) + NO₃-N + (Kmin × Org-N)
• Kvol = 1.0 (injected) or 0.5 (surface applied)
• Convert mg/kg to lbs/dry ton: multiply by 0.002
• Org-N = TKN − NH₃-N
• PAN determines the legal application rate under 40 CFR 503
• If they ask "what is the agronomic rate?" → next step is Calc #67
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
NH₃-N (mg/kg dry)Biosolids lab analysis report. Required by 40 CFR 503. Your lab or contracted analytical lab result.
NO₃-N (mg/kg dry)Biosolids lab analysis report. Less common in biosolids — often near zero.
Organic N (mg/kg dry)Biosolids lab analysis report. Usually the largest fraction. TKN − NH₃-N = Organic N.
Application MethodYour land application plan or 503 permit. Surface vs injection determines volatilization loss.
Mineralization Rate (%)Biosolids quality (Class A/B) and local soil conditions. 503 guidance: 20% default for Class B.
% SolidsBiosolids lab analysis (total solids test). Dewatered cake: typically 15–25%. Log or lab report.
Ammonia nitrogen
Nitrate nitrogen
TKN minus NH₃-N
Affects ammonia volatilization
Year 1: 20-30% typical
Biosolids % total solids
Volatilization: Injected = 0% NH₃ loss | Surface = 50% NH₃ loss | Mineralization: Aerobic digested: 30% | Anaerobic: 20% | Composted: 10% | Raw: 40%
—
Enter data...
PAN = (Kvol × NH₃-N) + NO₃-N + (Kmin × Org-N) | lbs PAN/dry ton = PAN(mg/kg) × 0.002

88 Land Application Rate

Calculate agronomic application rate in dry tons/acre and wet gallons/acre from PAN and crop nitrogen uptake. Ensures compliance with 40 CFR 503 and TCEQ biosolids permit requirements.
InputWhere to Get It
PAN (lbs/dry ton)Output from PAN calculator (#69). Or from your 503 land application plan.
Crop N Uptake (lbs N/acre/yr)TCEQ guidance tables, Texas A&M AgriLife Extension, or agronomist.
% SolidsBiosolids lab analysis. Same value used in PAN calc.
Annual Production (dry tons/yr)Biosolids production log. Wet tons hauled × (% solids ÷ 100).
Available AcreageLand application site maps and 503 permit. Survey or GIS acreage.
LAND APPLICATION RATE — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
This determines how many dry tons of biosolids you can apply per acre per year based on the nitrogen balance: crop nitrogen uptake divided by PAN per dry ton. Apply at the agronomic rate — the amount of nitrogen the crop can use — and you stay in compliance. Exceed it and you risk nitrate groundwater contamination and regulatory action.
Inputs Explained
PAN (lbs/dry ton) — From Calc #69 or your lab report. This is the nitrogen the crop can access from each dry ton of biosolids applied.
Crop N Uptake (lbs N/acre/yr) — How much nitrogen the crop can absorb in one growing season. Get from NRCS guidance, your agronomist, or TCEQ design guidance. Common Texas crops: Coastal bermuda 300–400, corn silage 200–250, wheat 100–150, hay 150–250.
% Solids — Your biosolids total solids. Critical for converting dry tons to wet gallons for hauling logistics. Liquid biosolids (2–6% solids) require tanker trucks; dewatered cake (15–30%) uses dump trucks.
Annual Production (dry tons/yr) — How much biosolids your plant generates per year. Calculate from: (lbs dry solids/day × 365) ÷ 2000.
Available Acreage — Total permitted land application acreage. The calculator checks if your site is big enough for your annual production.
Site Capacity Check
If your production exceeds what the site can handle at the agronomic rate, you need more acreage, a different disposal method (composting, landfill, incineration), or you need to reduce solids production at the plant.
TCEQ Exam Tips
• DT/acre = Crop N uptake ÷ PAN per dry ton
• Wet gal/acre = (DT/acre × 2000 lbs) ÷ (8.34 × % solids as decimal)
• Required acres = Annual production (DT/yr) ÷ Rate (DT/acre/yr)
• 40 CFR 503: Federal regulation governing biosolids use and disposal
• Agronomic rate = match N application to crop uptake. This is the law.
• Exam may give you all PAN components and crop info in one problem — solve PAN first, then rate
• Remember: 1 dry ton = 2,000 lbs
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
PAN (lbs/dry ton)Output from PAN calculator (#69). Or from your 503 land application plan calculations.
Crop N Uptake (lbs N/acre/yr)TCEQ guidance tables, Texas A&M AgriLife Extension, or agronomist recommendation. Crop-specific.
% SolidsBiosolids lab analysis. Same value used in PAN calc. Total solids test result.
Annual Production (dry tons/yr)Biosolids production log. Wet tons hauled × (% solids ÷ 100). Hauler manifests.
Available AcreageLand application site maps and 503 permit. Survey or GIS acreage of permitted fields.
From Calc #69 or lab
Bermuda: 200-400 | Corn: 150-200
Liquid: 2-6% | Cake: 15-30%
Total plant biosolids output
Permitted land app site
Crop N Uptake (lbs/acre/yr): Coastal bermuda: 300–400 | Corn silage: 200–250 | Wheat: 100–150 | Hay: 150–250 | Fescue: 150–250
—
Enter data...
DT/acre = Crop N ÷ PAN | Wet gal/acre = (DT × 2000) ÷ (8.34 × %solids/100) | Required acres = Production ÷ Rate
💉 Chemical Feed & Pump Calculators

89 Chemical Dosing Pump Output

Complete chemical feed pump calculator: forward calculation (settings → output), reverse calculation (target dose → required settings), and dose verification with plant flow. Includes chemical presets, cost tracking, and calibration tools.
InputWhere to Get It
Chemical PresetProduct label or SDS. Select from dropdown to auto-fill SG and concentration.
Pump Capacity (GPD)Pump nameplate or manufacturer spec sheet. Rated capacity at 100% stroke.
Stroke / Speed (%)Pump control panel dial or VFD setpoint. Current operating setting.
Flow (MGD)Influent or effluent flow meter. Point of chemical application.
Target Dose (mg/L)Jar test result, permit requirement, or operational target.
Max output at full stroke/speed
0–100% of max stroke
Speed setting 0–100%
Auto-filled from preset
Active ingredient %
For cost estimates
🎯 Reverse Calc: Enter target lb/day → calculates required pump settings.
Required chemical feed rate
Your pump's max output
Chemical density
Fixed stroke, solve for rate
— % setting
Enter target lb/day and pump capacity...
💧 Dose Verification: Enter pump output + plant flow to verify actual dose in mg/L.
From forward calc or meter
Treatment flow
Active ingredient %
Chemical density
— mg/L
Enter pump output and plant flow...
🔧 Calibration: Compare calculated vs actual measured output (bucket test).
Nameplate rating
Setting during test
Speed during test
How long you ran test
Actual collected amount
— % accuracy
Enter calibration test data...
🔬 3-Point Calibration Check (TCEQ Inspection Standard ±5%)
Collect 3 separate timed samples. Average is compared to calculated output. TCEQ expects ±5% accuracy.
Common Pump Sizes: Small: 0.5–5 GPD | Medium: 5–25 GPD | Large: 25–100+ GPD
— mL/hr
Enter pump capacity and settings...
VOLUME OUTPUT
mL/hr
—
mL/day
—
L/day
—
GPD
—
GPH
—
lb/day
—
ACTIVE CHEMICAL & COST
Active lb/day
—
$/Day
—
$/Month
—
USAGE ESTIMATES
Gal/Month
—
Drums/Month
—
🔧 CALIBRATION CHECK (1 min)
Collect for 1 min → expect — mL
Output = Capacity × Stroke% × Rate% | lb/day = GPD × 8.34 × SG
CHEMICAL DOSING PUMP — OPERATOR GUIDE
Four Calculation Modes
• Forward — Pump settings → Output (mL/hr, GPD, lb/day)
• Reverse — Target lb/day → Required pump settings
• Dose — Verify mg/L dose at plant flow
• Calibrate — Compare actual vs calculated
Chemical Presets
• Bleach 12.5% — SG 1.16
• Ferric 40% — SG 1.42
• Caustic 50% — SG 1.53
TCEQ Exam Tips
• lb/day = GPD × 8.34 × SG
• Diaphragm pumps — Most common
• Always calibrate — Pump curves are theoretical
📋 Where to Get Each Input
InputWhere to Find It
Chemical PresetProduct label or SDS. Select from dropdown to auto-fill SG and concentration.
Pump Capacity (GPD)Pump nameplate or manufacturer spec sheet. Rated capacity at 100% stroke.
Stroke / Speed Setting (%)Pump control panel dial or VFD setpoint. Current operating setting.
Flow (MGD)Influent or effluent flow meter. Point of chemical application.
Target Dose (mg/L)Jar test result, permit requirement, or operational target. Adjust to actual residual.

90 Chemical Cost Comparison

Compare two chemicals that achieve the same treatment goal: alum vs ferric for phosphorus, bleach vs liquid SO₂ for disinfection, caustic vs lime for alkalinity. Computes dose required, lbs/day, gal/day, $/day, and $/year for each option at your plant flow. The cheapest label price is rarely the cheapest actual cost — this calc shows the full picture.
CHEMICAL COST COMPARISON — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Side-by-side cost comparison for two chemical alternatives achieving the same treatment goal. The comparison accounts for active ingredient concentration, specific gravity, required dose, and unit cost — giving you $/day and $/year for each option. Common comparisons include alum vs ferric for P removal, sodium hypochlorite strengths from different suppliers, and caustic vs lime for alkalinity.
Common Chemical Substitution Pairs
GoalOption AOption B
P removalAlum 48% (SG 1.33)Ferric chloride 37–40% (SG 1.42)
ChlorinationBleach 10% (SG 1.07)Bleach 12.5% (SG 1.17)
DechlorinationNaHSO₃ 38% (SG 1.38)SO₂ gas (100%)
AlkalinityCaustic NaOH 50% (SG 1.53)Lime Ca(OH)₂ 90% dry
P removalAlum liquidPAC (polyaluminum chloride)
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#14 Chemical DosingForward calc: given dose mg/L → lbs/day for one chemical. This calc does both simultaneously and adds cost comparison.
#43 Phosphorus RemovalComputes alum or ferric dose needed for a target P — use that dose as input here to compare costs between the two coagulants.
#72 Pump Dose OutputOnce you choose the better chemical, use #72 to set pump settings that deliver the required GPD.
#37 Hypochlorite DosingComputes bleach dose at different strengths — use those doses here to compare 10% vs 12.5% bleach from different suppliers.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Chemical cost analysis: $/lb active = $/gal ÷ (SG × 8.34 × %active/100)
• Always compare on $/lb ACTIVE ingredient — label price per gallon is misleading
• 12.5% bleach vs 10% bleach: if 12.5% costs <25% more per gallon, it is cheaper per lb Cl₂
• Alum vs ferric: ferric requires ~30% less product dose by weight for same P removal, but ferric is typically more expensive per gallon — compare on $/lb active Al or Fe
InputWhere to Get It
Plant Flow (MGD)SCADA daily average or operator log. Use average dry weather flow for annual cost estimate. Use peak flow for maximum daily cost.
Chemical A & B NamesProduct name from supplier invoice or SDS. Used for labeling only — does not affect calculation.
Dose (mg/L) — each chemicalFrom jar test (coagulants), existing calibration data, or permit requirement. For coagulants: use Calc #43 to determine dose needed for target P. For chlorine: use Calc #34 or #109 to determine dose for target residual.
% Active / PurityFrom SDS or certificate of analysis. Sodium hypochlorite: % available chlorine. Alum: % Al₂(SO₄)₃ or % Al₂O₃. Ferric: % FeCl₃. Caustic: % NaOH. If 100% (gas or dry), enter 100.
Specific GravityFrom SDS. Liquid chemicals: SG is critical to converting $/gal to $/lb. Dry chemicals: enter 1.0 — the calc uses lbs/lb instead of lbs/gal. Typical values: bleach 10% = 1.07, bleach 12.5% = 1.17, alum = 1.33, ferric = 1.42, caustic 50% = 1.53.
Unit Cost ($/gal or $/lb)From most recent supplier invoice. Compare on the same unit basis — if both are liquid use $/gal; if one is dry use $/lb. The calc normalizes both to $/lb active and $/day for valid comparison.
CHEMICAL A
Required dose in process
From SDS
From SDS (1.0 for dry)
$/gal (liquid) or $/lb (dry)
CHEMICAL B
Required dose in process
From SDS
From SDS (1.0 for dry)
$/gal or $/lb
Average daily flow
lbs/day = Flow(MGD) × Dose(mg/L) × 8.34 | $/day = (lbs/day ÷ %active) × unit cost per lb product
—
Enter both chemicals, doses, costs, and plant flow...
lbs active/day = Flow(MGD) × Dose(mg/L) × 8.34 | lbs product/day = lbs active ÷ (% active/100) | $/day = lbs product × (unit cost ÷ lbs per gal or lb)
🧫 Activated Sludge – Advanced Calculators

91 OUR – Oxygen Uptake Rate

Calculate Oxygen Uptake Rate from respirometry test. OUR indicates biological activity, toxicity, and process health.
InputWhere to Get It
Initial DO (mg/L)DO meter reading at start of test in aeration basin (with aeration off).
Final DO (mg/L)DO meter reading at end of timed interval (aeration still off).
Time Interval (min)Stopwatch. Typically 5–15 minutes depending on DO drop rate.
MLVSS (mg/L)Lab result. Filter at 550°C. Or MLSS × 0.75–0.85 estimate.
For SOUR calculation
Typical OUR: 5–20 mg/L/hr (healthy) | <5 = toxicity/washout risk | 20–35 = high load | >35 = overloaded | Sudden drop = toxicity event
— mg/L/hr
Enter respirometry data...
🤖 AI Plant Advisor
OUR = (DO₁ - DO₂) ÷ Time × 60 | SOUR = OUR ÷ MLVSS × 1000

92 SOUR – Specific OUR

Calculate Specific Oxygen Uptake Rate (per unit biomass). Better for comparing activity across different MLSS levels.
InputWhere to Get It
OUR (mg O₂/L/hr)Output from OUR calc (#77), or calculate from timed DO drop test.
MLVSS (mg/L)Lab result. MLSS × 0.75–0.85 if VSS test not available.
Typical SOUR: 8-20 mg O₂/g MLVSS/hr (CAS) | <3 = old/starved sludge | >25 = young sludge or toxicity recovery
— mg O₂/g/hr
Enter OUR and MLVSS...
SOUR = OUR ÷ MLVSS × 1000 (mg O₂/g MLVSS/hr)

93 Settleability Test

Interpret 30-minute settleability test results. Calculate SSV30, SVI, and settling velocity for sludge quality assessment.
InputWhere to Get It
30-min Settled (mL/L)Settleometer test: 1L graduated cylinder, 30-min reading. Daily log.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily lab grab sample from aeration basin.
5-min Settled Vol (mL/L)Same settleometer test, reading at 5-minute mark.
Initial Height (cm)Measure the settled interface height for zone settling velocity calculation.
Read from 1L cylinder
Optional - for rate
1L cylinder ≈ 36 cm
SVI targets: 50-150 good | <50 old/pin floc | >150 bulking | >200 severe bulking
—
Enter settleability data...
SVI = (SSV30 × 1000) ÷ MLSS | ISV = settling rate in m/hr

94 Nitrification Rate – Temperature Correction

Arrhenius temperature correction for nitrification rate. Critical for Texas seasonal swings — rate drops ~50% from summer (30°C) to winter (15°C). Nitrification requires SRT ≥ 8 days at any temperature.
InputWhere to Get It
Measured Nitrification RateOUR test at current temp, or respirometry. Lab measurement.
Reference Temperature (°C)Temperature at which your measured rate was taken. Basin thermometer.
Target Temperature (°C)Future or design condition. Seasonal projection or design temp.
θ CoefficientLiterature: 1.072 (standard). O&M manual or engineering report may specify.
Safety FactorYour engineering margin. Typically 1.5–2.0.
Yield (Y)Literature default 0.6 for activated sludge. O&M manual or design report.
Rate at reference temperature
Temperature when measured (default 20°C)
Current or design temperature
Process-specific Arrhenius coefficient
⏱️ Minimum SRT for Nitrification at Target Temp
g VSS / g NH₃-N
Arrhenius: Rate_T = Rate_20 × θ^(T−20) | θ=1.072 for nitrification | At 15°C: rate = 71% of 20°C rate
—
Enter rate and temperatures...
Rate_T = Rate_ref × θ^(T_target − T_ref) | Minimum SRT_T = SRT_20 / θ^(T−20)

95 Denitrification / Anoxic Zone Sizing

Size anoxic zones for biological denitrification. Calculates NO₃ removal rate, HRT required, volume needed, and methanol equivalent if external carbon is required. Critical for TN permit compliance.
InputWhere to Get It
Influent NO₃-N (mg/L)Lab grab or composite at anoxic zone inlet. For MLE: end-of-aeration NO₃.
Target Effluent NO₃-N (mg/L)TPDES TN permit limit minus expected NH₃-N and organic N. Permit Page 2a.
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter daily average. SCADA or daily log.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily mixed liquor lab result. Assume ~80% VSS for MLVSS.
Process ConfigYour plant design. As-built drawings or O&M manual process description.
Water Temp (°C)Basin water temperature probe. SCADA or daily log.
DENITRIFICATION / ANOXIC ZONE SIZING GUIDE
What is Denitrification?
Denitrification is the biological process where bacteria convert nitrate (NO₃-N) to nitrogen gas (N₂), removing it from the wastewater permanently. It requires an anoxic zone — no dissolved oxygen — and a carbon source for energy. As TN limits appear in more Texas TPDES permits, sizing anoxic zones correctly is becoming essential.
Step-by-Step Instructions
Step 1: Enter NO₃-N Values
• Influent NO₃-N — The nitrate concentration entering your anoxic zone. For pre-anoxic MLE, this is the internal recycle stream from the end of the aerobic zone. Typical: 15–30 mg/L.
• Target Effluent NO₃-N — Your permit TN target minus expected NH₃ and organic N in effluent. Common targets: 3–10 mg/L.

Step 2: Enter Flow and MLSS
Use your average daily flow in MGD. MLSS in the anoxic zone — typically same as your aeration basin MLSS (2,000–4,000 mg/L).

Step 3: Select Process Configuration
• Pre-anoxic (MLE) — Anoxic zone before aeration, uses influent BOD as carbon source. Most common and cost-effective. BOD:NO₃ ratio should be ≥5:1.
• Post-anoxic — Anoxic zone after aeration. Requires external carbon (methanol, acetate). Used when effluent TN targets are very low (<3 mg/L).
• Oxidation Ditch / Step-feed — Alternating aerobic/anoxic zones built into the basin configuration.

Step 4: Enter Water Temperature
Denitrification slows in cold water. Winter temps (10–15°C) require significantly larger anoxic zones than summer operation (20–25°C).
Understanding SDNR
SDNR = Specific Denitrification Rate (mg NO₃-N removed per mg VSS per day). It describes how fast your bacteria can denitrify per unit of biomass.

ConfigSDNR at 20°CCarbon Source
Pre-anoxic MLE0.05–0.10Influent BOD
Post-anoxic0.03–0.06External (methanol)
Oxidation ditch0.04–0.07Mixed

Temperature correction: SDNR_T = SDNR_20 × 1.09^(T−20). At 10°C, denitrification is roughly half the rate at 20°C.
External Carbon (Post-Anoxic)
When post-anoxic configuration is selected, the calculator shows methanol requirement:
• Rule of thumb: 2.47 lbs methanol per lb NO₃-N removed
• Methanol is flammable — requires chemical storage safety measures
• Acetate and glycerol are safer alternatives with similar dosing rates
• Some plants use plant effluent or primary clarifier overflow as low-cost internal carbon
TCEQ Class A Exam Tips
• Denitrification requires: Anoxic conditions (no DO), carbon source, and facultative bacteria
• Electron acceptor: NO₃ replaces O₂ as electron acceptor in anoxic respiration
• End product: N₂ gas — leaves the liquid phase entirely
• pH effect: Denitrification produces alkalinity (opposite of nitrification) — about 3.57 mg alk per mg NO₃-N removed
• MLE process = Modified Ludzack-Ettinger — pre-anoxic zone with internal recycle from aerobic to anoxic
• DO in anoxic zone: Must be below 0.1–0.2 mg/L or denitrification stops
• Typical TN permit targets in Texas: 10–15 mg/L emerging; 3 mg/L for sensitive watersheds
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Influent NO₃-N (mg/L)Lab result from grab or composite sample at anoxic zone inlet. For MLE: measure end-of-aeration NO₃.
Target Effluent NO₃-N (mg/L)Your TPDES TN permit limit minus expected NH₃-N and organic N in effluent. Permit Page 2a.
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter daily average. SCADA or daily log.
MLSS (mg/L)Daily mixed liquor lab result. Assume ~80% VSS for MLVSS calculation.
Process ConfigYour plant design. As-built drawings or O&M manual process description.
Water Temp (°C)Basin water temperature probe. SCADA or daily log. Use basin temperature, not air temp.
NO₃ entering anoxic zone
TN permit target
In anoxic zone
For rate correction
SDNR: 0.03–0.11 mg NO₃/mg VSS/day (pre-anoxic, 20°C) | Methanol: 2.47 lb CH₃OH per lb NO₃-N removed
—
Enter NO₃ values and flow...
SDNR_T = SDNR_20 × 1.09^(T−20) | Volume = NO₃ load ÷ (SDNR × MLVSS) | Methanol = 2.47 × NO₃ removed (lbs/day)

96 Temperature Correction Suite

Arrhenius temperature correction for all three TCEQ-testable biological rate adjustments: nitrification (θ=1.047), denitrification (θ=1.072), and BOD removal (θ=1.047 or 1.06). One calc, three processes. Critical for Class A exam — temperature correction problems appear in activated sludge AND design criteria sections.
TEMPERATURE CORRECTION SUITE — GUIDE
What This Calculates
Rate at a new temperature using the Arrhenius equation: k_T = k_ref × θ^(T − T_ref). All three TCEQ Class A biological process correction factors are built in with standard theta values, but you can also enter a custom theta.
Theta Values — Memorize These for the Exam
Processθ (theta)Ref TempNote
Nitrification1.04720°CMost sensitive to temp drop
Denitrification1.07220°CMore sensitive than nitrif.
BOD (CAS)1.04720°CSame as nitrification
BOD (Lagoon)1.0620°CHigher than CAS systems
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#5 SRTMinimum SRT for nitrification increases as temperature drops. This calc gives the corrected nitrification rate — use it to determine if your SRT provides adequate safety factor.
#97 Nitrif. TempCalc #97 covers nitrification rate correction specifically. This calc adds denitrification and BOD correction to complete the suite.
#101 Denitrif.Denitrification anoxic zone sizing uses the temperature-corrected denitrification rate from this calc.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Formula: k_T = k_20 × θ^(T−20) — θ raised to the DIFFERENCE, not the temperature itself
• Temperature BELOW 20°C → exponent is negative → rate DECREASES
• Temperature ABOVE 20°C → exponent is positive → rate INCREASES
• At 10°C with θ=1.047: k_10 = k_20 × 1.047^(10−20) = k_20 × 1.047^(−10) = k_20 × 0.63
• Nitrification rate at 10°C is only 63% of the 20°C rate — this is why winter SRT must be 60%+ longer
InputWhere to Get It
Process TypeSelect nitrification, denitrification, BOD (CAS), BOD (lagoon), or custom. This auto-fills the standard theta value. Use custom if your design documents specify a site-specific theta.
Rate at Reference Temp (k_ref)The known reaction rate at the reference temperature (usually 20°C). For nitrification: maximum specific nitrification rate from bench test or design document. For BOD: k_BOD from pilot test or literature. For denitrification: specific denitrification rate from ORP test or literature.
Reference Temperature (°C)Temperature at which k_ref was measured. Standard reference = 20°C for all three processes per Metcalf & Eddy and TCEQ exam convention.
Operating Temperature (°C)Current wastewater temperature from thermometer in aeration basin. Measure at the same time of day consistently. Seasonal low (winter minimum) is the design-controlling temperature.
Custom Theta — optionalOverride the standard theta if your design documents specify a different value. Only use if you have a site-specific theta from treatability testing.
Any rate unit (relative or absolute)
Usually 20°C (standard)
Current wastewater temp
Auto-filled by mode
Arrhenius: k_T = k_ref × θ^(T_op − T_ref) | θ=1.047 nitrif/BOD-CAS | θ=1.072 denitrif | θ=1.060 BOD-lagoon
—
Select process and enter temperatures...
k_T = k_ref × θ^(T_op − T_ref) | Temp factor = θ^ΔT | % change vs reference = (k_T/k_ref − 1) × 100

97 Sludge Production Model

Predict daily sludge production from first principles: yield coefficient, endogenous decay, SRT, and BOD loading. Shows net VSS production, total TSS wasting rate, and the WAS rate needed to maintain target MLSS. The design criteria exam question — "a plant receiving X lbs BOD/day at Y-day SRT produces how many lbs TSS/day?"
SLUDGE PRODUCTION MODEL — GUIDE
The Activated Sludge Production Equation
P_x,VSS = Y_obs × Q × (S₀ − S) × 8.34
Y_obs = Y ÷ (1 + kd × SRT)
P_x,TSS = P_x,VSS ÷ (VSS/TSS ratio) + inorganic TSS load
What Each Parameter Controls
Y (yield): How much biomass grows per unit of BOD removed. Higher Y = more sludge per lb BOD treated.

kd (decay): Rate at which cells self-oxidize (endogenous respiration). Higher kd at longer SRT = less net sludge.

Y_obs (observed yield): Y reduced by endogenous decay over the SRT. At SRT=20 days, Y_obs is much lower than at SRT=5 days — extended aeration produces less sludge per unit BOD.

SRT sensitivity: Doubling SRT roughly halves sludge production. This is why extended aeration plants handle biosolids less frequently.
Related Calculators
CALCHOW IT CONNECTS
#5 SRTSRT from Calc #5 is the primary input here. This model is the downstream calculation of what that SRT means for sludge production.
#8 WAS RateWAS rate from this model feeds into Calc #8. The model tells you how many lbs/day to waste; Calc #8 converts that to a pump GPD setting.
#103 % SolidsDry lbs/day from this model → enter into Calc #103 with cake % solids to get hauling volume and cost inputs for Calc #87.
#112 Temp Correctionkd increases with temperature. Run Calc #112 with θ=1.04 to correct kd at your operating temperature before entering here.
📝 TCEQ Exam Tips
• Y_obs = Y ÷ (1 + kd × SRT) — this is the observed yield after endogenous decay
• Net VSS production (lbs/day) = Y_obs × Q(MGD) × BOD_removed(mg/L) × 8.34
• At SRT=10 days, Y=0.5, kd=0.06: Y_obs = 0.5÷(1+0.06×10) = 0.5÷1.6 = 0.313
• TSS production = VSS production ÷ VSS/TSS ratio
• WAS rate needed = TSS production ÷ WAS concentration (mg/L) ÷ 8.34 (in MGD or GPD)
• Extended aeration (SRT=20+): Y_obs ≈ 0.15–0.25 | Conventional (SRT=5–10): Y_obs ≈ 0.3–0.45
InputWhere to Get It
Influent BOD (mg/L)From composite sample lab result or SCADA online analyzer. Use average of last 30 days for steady-state model. Daily variation is normal — this model outputs steady-state production.
Flow (MGD)SCADA daily average. Use same period as BOD data to get consistent loading.
Yield Coefficient Y (lb VSS/lb BOD)Design documents or O&M manual. Typical activated sludge: 0.4–0.6 lb VSS/lb BOD. Extended aeration: 0.3–0.4. Use 0.5 if unknown — conservative for most CAS systems.
Endogenous Decay kd (per day)Design documents or literature. Typical: 0.04–0.08 /day at 20°C. Use 0.06 if unknown. Increases with temperature (θ=1.04).
SRT (days)From Calc #5 — your current calculated SRT. Or from design documents. This is the most sensitive variable in the model.
VSS/TSS RatioFrom weekly lab: VSS ÷ TSS on mixed liquor samples. Typical activated sludge: 0.75–0.85. Lower ratios indicate more inorganic content (grit, chemical addition).
Inorganic TSS (mg/L)Non-volatile TSS from influent that passes through treatment. From primary clarifier effluent TSS minus VSS. Typical: 30–80 mg/L. If unknown, use 50 mg/L.
Average concentration
Secondary effluent
Typical CAS: 0.40–0.60
Typical: 0.04–0.08
From Calc #5
Typical AS: 0.75–0.85
Non-volatile influent solids
Y_obs = Y ÷ (1 + kd × SRT) | VSS prod = Y_obs × Flow × BOD_removed × 8.34 | TSS prod = VSS ÷ VSS/TSS ratio
—
Enter BOD, flow, Y, kd, and SRT...
Y_obs = Y ÷ (1 + kd × SRT) | P_x,VSS = Y_obs × Q(MGD) × (S₀−S)(mg/L) × 8.34 | P_x,TSS = P_x,VSS ÷ (VSS/TSS) + ITSS×Q×8.34
📐 Design Criteria Calculators

98 Clarifier Design DIAGRAM

Unified primary/secondary clarifier design. Enter flow + geometry to compute surface area, SOR, DT, and WLR. Add MLSS (and RAS if available) to trigger Solids Loading Rate and switch targets to secondary-clarifier design ranges automatically.
CLARIFIER DESIGN — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Does
One calculator for both primary and secondary clarifiers. Enter the four required fields (Flow, Diameter, Side Water Depth, Weir Length) and the tool computes Surface Area, Surface Overflow Rate, Detention Time, and Weir Loading Rate. Add MLSS (and optionally RAS Flow) and it automatically switches to secondary mode, adding Solids Loading Rate and changing all design targets to secondary-clarifier ranges.
Primary vs. Secondary Mode — How the Switch Works
🏭 Primary Mode (MLSS field empty) — Calculator assumes you're sizing or evaluating a primary clarifier. Target ranges: SOR 600–1,200 GPD/ft², DT 1.5–2.5 hr, WLR 10,000–20,000 GPD/ft.

🧪 Secondary Mode (MLSS field has a value) — Adding MLSS signals this is a secondary clarifier handling mixed liquor. Calculator adds an SLR tile and switches target ranges: SOR 400–800 GPD/ft² (peak ≤1,200), DT 2–4 hr, WLR ≤20,000, SLR 20–30 lb/day/ft² (peak ≤50).

⚠️ Gotcha: If you're running a primary but accidentally type a value in MLSS, the tool will evaluate your primary against secondary design targets and may show false warnings. Leave MLSS blank for primary-only sizing.
The Formulas
Area = π × (D/2)²   ft²
SOR = Flow (gpd) ÷ Area   GPD/ft²
Volume = Area × SWD × 7.48   gallons
DT = Volume ÷ Flow (gph)   hours
WLR = Flow (gpd) ÷ Weir Length   GPD/ft
SLR = (Q + Q_RAS) × MLSS × 8.34 ÷ Area   lb/day/ft²
Flow in MGD gets converted to GPD (× 1,000,000) or GPH (× 1,000,000 ÷ 24) internally. Don't convert before entering — just type your MGD value.
Reading the Output Tiles
Green — Value is within the design target range. ✅

Amber — Value is edge-of-range or mildly outside. Not immediately critical but worth noting.

Red — Value is clearly outside the design envelope. Likely cause of settling or hydraulic problems.
5 Daily Uses at Your Plant
① Troubleshoot a solids washout — Enter current flow + MLSS. If SOR or SLR tile goes red during high-flow events, that's your evidence. Document it and consider reducing MLSS or bringing a second clarifier online.

② Verify design capacity at peak flow — Enter peak hourly flow instead of average. If SOR > secondary peak limit (1,200 GPD/ft²) or SLR > peak limit (50 lb/day/ft²), the clarifier will blow out the blanket in wet weather. Use #59 Peak Flow Impact for a more complete peak analysis.

③ Size a new primary clarifier — Leave MLSS blank. Iterate diameter until SOR lands in 600–1,200 GPD/ft² at your design flow, then check DT is 1.5–2.5 hr. Adjust SWD if DT is off.

④ Evaluate a secondary clarifier under operating MLSS — Enter actual MLSS and RAS flow. SLR should land 20–30 lb/day/ft². If SLR > 30, MLSS is too high for this clarifier — the settling rate can't keep up with solids loading. Either drop MLSS or increase wasting.

⑤ Plan weir modifications — If WLR is flagged red (>20,000 GPD/ft), you have short-circuiting at the effluent weir. Consider adding inboard launders or a double-weir configuration to cut WLR in half.
Design Target Reference
Parameter Primary Secondary
SOR (avg)600–1,200 GPD/ft²400–800 GPD/ft²
SOR (peak)≤1,500 GPD/ft²≤1,200 GPD/ft²
Detention Time1.5–2.5 hr2–4 hr
Weir Loading Rate10,000–20,000 GPD/ft≤20,000 GPD/ft
SLR (avg)N/A20–30 lb/day/ft²
SLR (peak)N/A≤50 lb/day/ft²
Side Water Depth10–12 ft typical12–15 ft (min 10 ft TCEQ)
Expected Removal50–65% TSS, 25–40% BODTSS < 30 mg/L typical
Operator Notes
• Circular clarifiers only — this calculator assumes Area = π × (D/2)². For rectangular clarifiers, compute Area = L × W separately and use the formulas with your computed area.
• Weir Length for peripheral weir = π × Diameter. For inboard launders (double weir), multiply by 2.
• Use average flow for design verification, peak flow for stress-testing. The design point is whether your clarifier can handle both.
• RAS flow does not affect SOR — only influent flow passes the weir. RAS is added only in the SLR calculation because RAS solids contribute to total solids loading.
• TCEQ exam tip: Class A exams love SOR and SLR problems. Memorize 400–800 secondary SOR, 20–30 SLR, 600–1,200 primary SOR. If you see MLSS in the problem, it's a secondary clarifier question.
• Deeper is better for secondary clarifiers. Shallow tanks (<10 ft SWD) fail first during peak flow because the settling zone is compressed. TCEQ requires minimum 10 ft SWD.
• If SOR is fine but SLR is red, your clarifier is solids-limited, not hydraulically limited. Drop MLSS by increasing WAS.
• If SLR is fine but SOR is red, your clarifier is hydraulically limited. Bring another unit online or equalize peak flow upstream with #61 Equalization Basin Sizing.
InputWhere to Get It
Flow (MGD)Influent flow meter. SCADA. Use average daily for design check.
Diameter (ft)As-built drawings. Circular clarifiers only.
Side Water Depth (ft)As-built drawings. Water surface to floor. Typical 10–15 ft.
Weir Length (ft)As-built drawings. Peripheral weir length (π × D) or inboard (double weir) if launder.
MLSS (mg/L)Optional — Daily aeration basin lab sample. Adding this switches to secondary-clarifier targets and computes SLR.
RAS Flow (MGD)Optional — RAS pump runtime × capacity, or RAS meter. Adds Q_RAS to SLR calculation.
π × D for peripheral weir
🧪 Optional — Secondary Clarifier Details
Add these to compute Solids Loading Rate and auto-switch to secondary-clarifier design targets.
Design Targets — enter MLSS to switch context.
Primary: SOR 600–1,200 GPD/ft² | DT 1.5–2.5 hr | WLR 10,000–20,000 GPD/ft | 50–65% TSS removal
Secondary: SOR 400–800 GPD/ft² (peak ≤1,200) | SLR 20–30 lb/d/ft² (peak ≤50) | DT 2–4 hr | WLR ≤20,000 GPD/ft
—
Enter clarifier data...
Area = π × (D/2)² | SOR = Flow ÷ Area | DT = Vol ÷ Flow | WLR = Flow ÷ Weir | SLR = (Q + QRAS) × MLSS × 8.34 ÷ Area

100 Grit Chamber Design DIAGRAM

Calculate grit chamber sizing for horizontal flow or aerated grit removal. Targets 0.21 mm particles.
InputWhere to Get It
Peak Flow (MGD)Peak hour flow from SCADA or engineering design report. TCEQ 30 TAC §217.
Chamber TypeAs-built drawings — vortex, aerated, or channel type.
Detention Time (min)Design basis from O&M manual. Typically 2–5 min for aerated grit.
Velocity (fps)Design basis. Horizontal velocity: 0.8–1.3 fps to settle grit, not organics.
Horiz: 1 min | Aerated: 2-5 min
Horiz: 1.0 fps target
Design: Horizontal DT=1min, V=1fps | Aerated DT=2-5min | Remove ≥95% of 0.21mm+ particles
—
Enter grit chamber data...
Volume = Flow × DT | Area = Flow ÷ Overflow Rate

101 Chlorine Contact Chamber

Calculate contact chamber sizing to achieve required CT value for disinfection. TCEQ requires minimum 20-min contact at peak flow.
InputWhere to Get It
Peak Flow (MGD)SCADA peak flow or engineering design report.
Required Contact Time (min)TCEQ permit or 30 TAC §217. Calculated as T₁₀ = volume × baffling factor ÷ flow.
Chlorine Residual (mg/L)Online analyzer or DPD test at chamber exit.
Baffling FactorTracer study result, or use USEPA defaults: excellent=0.7, good=0.5, poor=0.3.
Poor=0.3 | Avg=0.5 | Good=0.7
TCEQ: 20 min @ peak | CT ≥ 25-30 mg·min/L typical | Baffling: Serpentine=0.7, Single=0.3
—
Enter chamber data...
Volume = Flow × Time ÷ Baffling | CT = Cl₂ × Time
💩 Sludge Disposal – Advanced Calculators

102 Belt Filter Press DIAGRAM

Calculate belt press performance: hydraulic and solids loading, polymer dose, and cake solids prediction.
InputWhere to Get It
Sludge Feed (GPM)Feed pump rate. Pump capacity × runtime ÷ 60.
Feed Solids (%)Lab total solids on BFP feed sludge.
Belt Width (m)Equipment nameplate or manufacturer specs.
Polymer Dose (lb/DT)Jar test result or startup data. Typically 10–25 lb/dry ton.
lb per dry ton
Targets: Hydraulic 50-100 GPM/m | Solids 500-1000 lb/hr/m | Cake 18-25% | Polymer 4-10 lb/DT
—
Enter BFP data...
Solids Load = GPM × 8.34 × %TS × 60 ÷ 2000 (lb DS/hr)

103 Centrifuge Performance

Calculate centrifuge solids capture rate and recovery. Essential for dewatering optimization.
InputWhere to Get It
Feed Solids (%)Lab total solids on centrifuge feed sludge.
Cake Solids (%)Lab total solids on cake sample. Target 18–25% for most plants.
Centrate TSS (mg/L)Lab TSS test on centrate. Return stream to head of plant.
Feed Rate (GPM)Feed pump rate or centrifuge feed flow meter.
Targets: Capture >95% | Cake 20-28% | Centrate <1000 mg/L TSS | Polymer 15-25 lb/DT
— % capture
Enter centrifuge data...
Capture = (Feed - Centrate) ÷ Feed × 100

104 Sludge Drying Beds

Calculate drying bed area requirements based on solids loading and climate conditions.
InputWhere to Get It
Sludge Production (lb DS/day)Daily sludge log: wet tons × % solids × 2000 lb/ton. Or solids mass balance.
Loading Rate (lb/ft²/yr)O&M manual or TCEQ design criteria. Typically 20–30 lb/ft²/yr for municipal.
Drying Cycle (days)Historical plant data. Varies by climate: 10–30 days in TX summer, longer in winter.
Application Depth (in)O&M manual. Typically 8–12 inches for sand beds.
15-25 typical
Loading: Open beds 15-25 lb/ft²/yr | Covered 25-35 | Dry to 25-40% solids | Texas: 20-30 day cycles
— ft²
Enter drying bed data...
Area = (lb DS/day × 365) ÷ Loading Rate

105 Sludge Hauling Cost

Calculate biosolids hauling costs based on cake solids, distance, and disposal fees.
InputWhere to Get It
Dry Solids (lb/day)Daily sludge log or solids mass balance.
Cake Solids (%)Lab total solids on dewatered cake sample.
Hauling Cost ($/wet ton)Hauler contract. Request invoice rate per wet ton transported.
Disposal Fee ($/wet ton)Landfill or land application contract. Request per wet ton tipping fee.
Cost impact: Every 1% increase in cake solids ≈ 4-5% reduction in hauling volume!
—
Enter hauling data...
Wet tons = Dry lb ÷ % Cake ÷ 2000 | Cost = Wet tons × (Haul + Disposal)

106 Class A/B Pathogen Reduction
