Check EPA 503 pathogen reduction requirements for Class A and Class B biosolids.
InputWhere to Get It
Target ClassTCEQ permit or 40 CFR 503. Class A = highest reduction, Class B = standard.
PFRP MethodYour treatment process. O&M manual or 503 land application plan.
Temperature (°F)Digester or thermal treatment temperature gauge. SCADA.
Time at Temp (hours)Measured retention time at required temperature. Log book.
Class A: <1000 MPN fecal coliform/g OR <3 MPN Salmonella/4g | Class B: <2M MPN fecal coliform/g
—
Select pathogen reduction method...
40 CFR 503 Subpart D requirements
🔬 Laboratory Calculators

107 BOD Dilution Calculator

Calculate proper dilutions for BOD₅ test. Target 2+ mg/L DO depletion and final DO ≥1 mg/L for valid test.
InputWhere to Get It
Expected BOD (mg/L)Historical data or process knowledge. Estimate to choose dilution factor.
BOD Bottle Size (mL)Standard BOD bottle is 300 mL. Check your lab equipment.
Initial DO (mg/L)DO meter reading immediately after sample preparation (Day 0).
Final DO (mg/L)DO meter reading after 5-day incubation at 20°C (Day 5).
Sample sizes: Effluent (5-20 BOD): 100-300 mL | Primary (100-300): 5-15 mL | Raw (200-400): 2-10 mL
—
Enter expected BOD or test results...
BOD = (DO₁ - DO₂) × Bottle Vol ÷ Sample Vol | Valid: DO depletion ≥2 mg/L, Final DO ≥1

108 Composite Sample Calculator

Calculate flow-proportional composite sample volumes for TCEQ compliance sampling.
InputWhere to Get It
Sample Interval (hr)Your sampling protocol or permit requirement. 1-hour intervals are standard.
Total Composite (mL)Lab requirement. Standard 24-hr composite: 2,000 mL total.
Sample Period (hr)Permit requirement. 24-hr composite is standard for most TPDES parameters.
Avg Flow (MGD)Flow meter daily average. SCADA.
Flow at Sample TimesInstantaneous flow at each sample collection time. SCADA historian or flow chart.
TCEQ: 24-hr composite typical | Min 6 aliquots | Flow-proportional preferred | Min 100 mL per aliquot
—
Enter sampling parameters...
Vol per sample = (Flow at time ÷ Total Flow) × Total Composite Vol

109 TSS / VSS Lab Calculator

Calculate Total Suspended Solids and Volatile Suspended Solids from gravimetric lab data.
InputWhere to Get It
Filter Weight (g)Tared glass fiber filter (GF/C). Weigh before use on analytical balance.
Weight After 105°C (g)Same filter + sample after oven drying at 103–105°C for 1 hour.
Weight After 550°C (g)Same filter after muffle furnace at 550°C for 15 min.
Sample sourceMLSS: grab from aeration basin midpoint. Effluent TSS: composite. RAS: RAS line.
Clean, dry filter before use
Filter + residue after drying
Optional — for VSS calculation
Volume filtered (required)
QC Duplicate (optional — for RPD calculation)
Tared GF/C filter, duplicate
After 105°C drying
Volume filtered, duplicate
Typical Sample Volumes: Effluent: 100–500 mL | MLSS: 10–25 mL | WAS: 5–10 mL | RAS: 10–25 mL | Primary sludge: 5–10 mL
—
Enter filter weights and sample volume...
TSS = (W_105°C − W_filter) × 1,000,000 ÷ mL | VSS = (W_105°C − W_550°C) × 1,000,000 ÷ mL | FSS = TSS − VSS
TSS / VSS LAB PROCEDURE — OPERATOR GUIDE
What This Calculates
TSS (Total Suspended Solids) and VSS (Volatile Suspended Solids) from filter weight measurements.
Lab Procedure
TSS (Standard Methods 2540D): Filter sample → Dry at 103–105°C → Weigh
VSS (Standard Methods 2540E): Take dried filter → Ignite at 550°C → Weigh
TCEQ Exam Tips
• TSS dried at 103–105°C — Weight difference = suspended solids
• VSS ignited at 550°C — Weight loss = volatile (organic) solids
• Typical MLVSS/MLSS ratio: 70–85%
📋 Where to Get Each Input
InputWhere to Find It
Filter Weight (g)Tared glass fiber filter (GF/C or equivalent). Weigh before use on analytical balance.
Weight After 105°C (g)Same filter + sample after oven drying at 103–105°C for 1 hour. Analytical balance.
Weight After 550°C (g)Same filter after muffle furnace at 550°C for 15 min. Measures ash/fixed solids.
Sample sourceFor MLSS: grab sample from aeration basin midpoint. For TSS: effluent composite. For RAS: RAS line grab.

110 SPC — Statistical Process Control

Calculate mean, standard deviation, UCL, LCL, and identify process drift. Detects violations before they happen. Enter process data (BOD, TSS, NH₃, MLSS, etc.) to build a control chart.
InputWhere to Get It
Parameter NameAny process parameter you track. Match your lab report or SCADA tag name.
Sigma Level (σ)Your choice. Use 3σ standard; 2σ for permit-critical parameters.
Data PointsConsecutive lab results or SCADA logs. Daily log sheets, LIMS export, or SCADA historian.
SPC — STATISTICAL PROCESS CONTROL GUIDE
What is SPC and Why Does It Matter?
Statistical Process Control uses your own historical data to define what "normal" looks like for your plant — then flags when something is drifting or out of control before it becomes a permit violation. Instead of reacting to a bad DMR result, you catch the trend weeks earlier.
Step-by-Step Instructions
Step 1: Name Your Parameter
Type a label like "Effluent BOD", "MLSS", or "Effluent NH₃". This appears on the summary report.

Step 2: Choose Sigma Level
• 3σ (Standard) — catches 99.7% of normal variation. One false alarm every ~370 readings. Use this for most parameters.
• 2σ (Tighter) — catches 95.4% of variation. More sensitive — good for parameters approaching permit limits or when you want early warning.

Step 3: Enter Your Data
Paste in daily, weekly, or any regular readings separated by commas or one per line. Minimum 5 points — 15–30 points gives reliable control limits. Use a consistent time period (e.g., last 30 days of effluent BOD).
Reading the Results
X̄ (Mean) — Your process center line. All values scatter around this.

UCL / LCL — Upper and Lower Control Limits. Values outside these limits are statistically "out of control" — not random variation, something changed.

CV (Coefficient of Variation) — Std Dev ÷ Mean × 100. Below 15% = very consistent process. Above 30% = high variability, investigate causes.

Drift Rule — 7 or more consecutive points all above or all below the mean indicates a systematic shift, even if no point crosses UCL/LCL. This is often the most valuable finding.
Practical Use Cases
Effluent BOD or TSS trending up — Enter last 20 days. If 7+ consecutive points are above the mean, investigate clarifier performance, sludge blanket, or organic loading.

MLSS control — Enter daily MLSS readings. UCL/LCL shows your natural operating band. Points outside = WAS was too much or too little.

Influent flow monitoring — Use SPC on daily flow to detect I/I patterns. Rising mean in wet months confirms infiltration problem.

Ammonia compliance — If summer NH₃ limits are tight, run SPC on effluent NH₃ weekly. Drift above mean = nitrification slowing — adjust SRT before DMR violation.
TCEQ Class A Exam Tips
• UCL/LCL formula: Mean ± (n × Std Dev) where n = sigma level
• Sample standard deviation uses (n−1) in denominator, not n — the exam sometimes asks which formula
• Western Electric Rules: 1 point beyond 3σ OR 7 points in a row on one side = out of control
• Common cause variation = random, expected (within control limits)
• Assignable cause variation = something specific changed (outside control limits) — requires investigation
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Parameter NameAny process parameter you track. Match your lab report or SCADA tag name exactly for traceability.
Sigma Level (σ)Your operational choice. Use 3σ for standard monitoring; 2σ for permit-critical parameters.
Data PointsConsecutive lab results or SCADA logs for one parameter. Daily log sheets, LIMS export, or SCADA historian. Use consistent time intervals — daily values for effluent params, hourly for process.
Minimum 5 data points recommended for reliable control limits
SPC Rules: UCL/LCL = Mean ± (σ × Std Dev) | 1 point beyond limits = out of control | 7 consecutive points above/below mean = drift
—
Enter data points...
UCL/LCL = X̄ ± (nσ × s) | s = √[Σ(xᵢ−X̄)² ÷ (n−1)]
🏛️ TCEQ Compliance Tools

111 DMR Calculator

Calculate monthly averages, maximums, and loading for Discharge Monitoring Reports. Automatically flags permit exceedances.
InputWhere to Get It
ParameterFrom TPDES permit effluent limitations table (Page 2a). Match name exactly.
Permit Limit (Avg)TPDES permit Page 2a — Monthly Average column.
Permit Limit (Max)TPDES permit Page 2a — Daily Maximum column.
Avg Flow (MGD)Sum all daily flows ÷ number of days. From flow totalizer.
Daily ValuesIndividual lab results from bench sheets or LIMS. One per sampling day.
DMR CALCULATOR — COMPLETE OPERATOR GUIDE
What is a DMR?
The Discharge Monitoring Report (DMR) is a required monthly report submitted to TCEQ documenting your effluent quality. It's your proof of permit compliance. Errors on DMRs are one of the top violations cited during inspections.
Step-by-Step Instructions
Step 1: Select Parameter
Choose the parameter you're calculating (BOD₅, TSS, NH₃-N, etc.). This helps track which values you're entering.

Step 2: Enter Permit Limits
• Permit Limit (Avg) — Your monthly average limit from your permit. Example: 30 mg/L for BOD₅.
• Permit Limit (Max) — Your daily maximum limit. Example: 45 mg/L for BOD₅.
Find these in your TPDES permit, Page 2a (Effluent Limitations).

Step 3: Enter Average Flow
Enter your monthly average flow in MGD. This is used to calculate mass loading (lbs/day). Get this from your flow totalizer or daily logs.

Step 4: Enter Daily Values
Enter all your sample results for the month. You can:
• Separate with commas: 22, 25, 28, 24, 26
• One value per line
• Mix both formats
The calculator will parse them automatically.
What the Results Mean
📊 Monthly Avg — The arithmetic mean of all values. This is what you report as "Monthly Average" on the DMR.

📈 Daily Max — The highest single value. Report this as "Daily Maximum."

📉 Daily Min — The lowest value (for reference, not usually reported).

📅 Sample Count — Number of values entered. Verify this matches your sampling frequency.

📦 Loading — Mass discharge in lbs/day. Calculated as: Concentration × Flow × 8.34. Many permits have mass limits in addition to concentration limits.
Exceedance Color Codes
✓ Green (OK) — Below 90% of limit. You're in good shape.
⚠️ Yellow (Approaching) — 90-100% of limit. Be careful, take action now.
🔴 Red (EXCEEDED) — Over the limit. This is a permit violation that must be reported.
TCEQ DMR Requirements
• Due Date: 20th of the month following the reporting period
• Late DMRs: Violation even if all values are in compliance
• No Data: Enter "NODI" code with explanation (e.g., "NODI-C" = conditional monitoring not triggered)
• Below Detection: Report as "<[detection limit]" or use 1/2 detection limit for averaging
• Significant Figures: Report to same precision as your permit limit
• Exceedances: Must be reported within 24 hours if >40% over limit, then written report within 5 days
Common Permit Limits (Texas)
ParameterMonthly AvgDaily MaxLoading
BOD₅20-30 mg/L45 mg/LOften limited
TSS20-30 mg/L45 mg/LOften limited
NH₃-N (summer)2-6 mg/L—Sometimes
NH₃-N (winter)4-15 mg/L——
E. coli126 geo mean399 single—
Cl₂ Residual—0.5 mg/L—
DO≥4.0 mg/L min——
pH6.0-9.0 SU——
Sampling Frequency Requirements
Your permit specifies minimum sampling frequency:
• 1/Week — Most common for small plants (<1 MGD)
• 2/Week — Common for 1-5 MGD plants
• 3/Week — Larger plants or sensitive receiving waters
• Daily — Large plants or parameters like Cl₂, pH, DO

⚠️ Missing samples = violation, even if results would have been good
Example Calculation
Given: Weekly BOD₅ samples: 22, 28, 25, 31 mg/L
Flow: 1.5 MGD | Permit: 30 avg / 45 max

Monthly Avg: (22+28+25+31) ÷ 4 = 26.5 mg/L
Daily Max: 31 mg/L
Loading: 26.5 × 1.5 × 8.34 = 331.5 lbs/day
% of Limit: 26.5 ÷ 30 × 100 = 88% ✓
TCEQ Class A Exam Tips
• 8.34 Factor: Memorize this — it converts mg/L × MGD to lbs/day
• Monthly Average: Always arithmetic mean (add and divide)
• E. coli: Uses GEOMETRIC mean, not arithmetic — use Calc #94
• pH: Cannot be averaged arithmetically — report range or median
• Loading formula: lbs/day = Conc (mg/L) × Flow (MGD) × 8.34
• Trick question: "Weekly average" still uses arithmetic mean for that week
• Below detection: Use ½ detection limit for averaging (unless permit specifies otherwise)
Common Mistakes to Avoid
❌ Forgetting to report exceedances within 24 hours
❌ Using geometric mean for BOD/TSS (only for bacteria)
❌ Averaging pH values arithmetically
❌ Missing the 20th deadline (even by 1 day = violation)
❌ Not keeping lab records to support DMR values
❌ Rounding incorrectly (26.5 → 27 vs 26.5 → 26)
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
ParameterFrom your TPDES permit effluent limitations table (Page 2a). Match parameter name exactly.
Permit Limit (Avg)TPDES permit Page 2a — Monthly Average column. If blank, no monthly average limit.
Permit Limit (Max)TPDES permit Page 2a — Daily Maximum column.
Avg Flow (MGD)Sum all daily flows ÷ number of days. From flow totalizer. Required field on DMR.
Daily ValuesIndividual lab results from bench sheets or LIMS. One result per sampling day. Match sampling frequency in permit.
Monthly average limit
Daily maximum limit
For loading calc
Enter all sample results for the month
📅 Monthly DMR Summary — Multi-Parameter
Enter monthly samples for each parameter. Calculates averages vs. permit limits and exports a TCEQ-ready CSV.
DMR Reporting: Monthly Avg = Σ values ÷ n | Daily Max = highest value | Loading = Conc × Flow × 8.34
—
Enter daily values...
Loading (lbs/day) = Concentration (mg/L) × Flow (MGD) × 8.34

112 Permit Limit Tracker

Track your permit limits and current performance. Shows % of limit used and warns when approaching exceedance.
InputWhere to Get It
ParameterFrom your TPDES permit Page 2a effluent limitations table.
Current ValueYour most recent lab result or calculated DMR value for that parameter.
Permit LimitTPDES permit Page 2a — monthly average or daily maximum column.
BOD₅ Avg
—
Limit: 30 mg/L
TSS Avg
—
Limit: 30 mg/L
NH₃-N Avg
—
Limit: 3 mg/L
E. coli Geo
—
Limit: 126/100mL
Status Colors: 🟢 <80% of limit | 🟡 80-95% | 🔴 >95% or exceeded

113 TCEQ Inspection Checklist

Comprehensive checklist of what TCEQ inspectors look for. Use before inspections to ensure compliance.
InputWhere to Get It
All checklist itemsWalk your facility before using this tool. Have permit, O&M manual, and lab records in hand. TCEQ inspector will ask for all of these on-site. Download current permit from TCEQ STEERS database.
TCEQ INSPECTION PREPARATION
Before the Inspector Arrives
• Have permit and all amendments readily available
• DMRs for past 3 years organized
• O&M manual accessible
• Lab QA/QC records ready
• Training records for all operators
• Calibration logs for all meters
Common Violations
• Inadequate operator coverage/licensing
• Missing or incomplete logs
• Permit limit exceedances not reported
• Bypasses not properly documented
• SSO reporting failures
• Lab certification issues
📋 Where to Get Each Input
Input Where to Find It
Checklist itemsWalk each item physically before using this tool. Have O&M manual and permit in hand.
Permit conditionsYour current TPDES permit (all pages). Download from TCEQ STEERS database.
Equipment statusDirect observation. Maintenance log. Equipment service records.
Lab recordsBench sheets, chain of custody forms, LIMS printouts. Must cover last 12 months minimum.
Operator licensesTCEQ license certificates. Expiration dates. Verify against current permit Class requirement.
📋 Pre-Inspection Checklist
PERMITS & RECORDS
OPERATOR REQUIREMENTS
LABORATORY & MONITORING
FACILITY & EQUIPMENT
COLLECTION SYSTEM
Completion
0%

114 Geometric Mean (E. coli)

Calculate geometric mean for E. coli or fecal coliform reporting. Required for DMR bacteria reporting.
InputWhere to Get It
E. coli ValuesIndividual lab results (MPN or CFU/100 mL) from the reporting period. One per sample event. Lab bench sheet or LIMS.
Permit Limit (Geo Mean)TPDES permit Page 2a. Texas standard: 126 CFU/100 mL geometric mean.
Single Sample MaxTPDES permit Page 2a. Texas standard: 394 CFU/100 mL single sample.
Enter all sample results (minimum 5 for monthly)
Typical: 126/100mL
Typical: 399/100mL
Geometric Mean: ⁿ√(x₁ × x₂ × ... × xₙ) — Used because bacteria counts are log-normally distributed
—
Enter E. coli values...
Geo Mean = (x₁ × x₂ × ... × xₙ)^(1/n) = e^[(Σ ln(x))/n]
🎓 Class A Exam — Advanced Process & Design Calculators (v9.60)

115 Activated Sludge Process Selection Matrix EXAM

Enter permit limits and influent characteristics → instantly see which activated sludge configuration can meet your permit. Critical for TCEQ Class A Design Criteria exam questions and ProCore pre-design assessments.
How to use: Enter your permit effluent limits. Tighter limits (especially TN < 10 mg/L or TP < 1 mg/L) eliminate certain configurations. The matrix scores each process and flags ✅ achievable / ⚠️ marginal / ❌ not recommended.
📋 Permit Effluent Limits
Typical: 20–30 mg/L
Typical: 20–30 mg/L
Nitrification required if < 10
Enter 999 if no TN limit
Enter 999 if no TP limit
Typical domestic: 200–300
Site & Operational Constraints
📊 Process Configuration Comparison
TCEQ Exam Tip: For TN < 10 mg/L → requires 4-stage Bardenpho or MBR. For TN < 15 mg/L → MLE sufficient. For TP < 1 mg/L without chemical → requires Bio-P (A²/O or Bardenpho). BOD/TSS ≤ 10/10 mg/L → MBR required.
🔗 RELATED CALCULATORS
CALCNAMEHOW IT CONNECTS
#116Zone HRT SplitterOnce process config is selected here, use #116 to split total basin volume into aerobic/anoxic/anaerobic zones.
#5SRT – Sludge AgeEach config has a minimum SRT requirement. Use #5 to verify your operating SRT meets the process demands.
#101Denitrification Zone SizingFor MLE/Bardenpho configs, use #101 to size anoxic zones for TN removal.
#11930-TAC Chap. 217 CheckerVerify chosen config meets TCEQ design minimum criteria before finalizing selection.

116 Anoxic / Aerobic Zone HRT Splitter EXAM

Enter total reactor volume and select process configuration → outputs zone volumes, HRTs per zone, and required recycle ratios. Critical for TCEQ Class A Design Criteria exam questions and pre-design work on biological nutrient removal systems.
Sum of all biological zones
MLE typical: 60–80%
🏗️ Zone Volume & HRT Breakdown
TCEQ Exam Tip: MLE anoxic zone = ~20–35% of total volume. A²/O: anaerobic 10–20%, anoxic 20–30%, aerobic 50–65%. 4-stage Bardenpho: first anoxic 20–35%, aerobic 40–50%, second anoxic 10–20%, reaeration 5%.
HRT (hrs) = Volume (MG) ÷ Flow (MGD) × 24 hrs/day | Recycle Ratio = Recycle Flow ÷ Influent Flow

117 Sludge Disposal Cost Comparison Matrix EXAM

Enter sludge production and characteristics → compare total annual cost and feasibility across all major disposal pathways. Addresses TCEQ Class A Sludge Disposal exam weakness. Also a ProCore consulting deliverable tool.
From Calc #43 or Calc #97
BFP: 18–25% | Centrifuge: 20–28%
Enter 0 if none available
TCEQ Exam Tip: 503 Rule Class B land application requires 400m buffer from habitation, no public access 12 months. Class A has no these site restrictions — costs more to achieve but dramatically expands disposal options. Landfill is always available but highest long-term cost.
🔗 RELATED CALCULATORS
CALCNAMEHOW IT CONNECTS
#43Sludge Volume & % SolidsSource of dry lbs/day and wet volume inputs for this calc.
#49Biosolids 503 RuleFull 503 compliance check — run alongside this calc to confirm land application eligibility.
#87Sludge Hauling CostDetailed hauling cost for a single pathway vs. this calc's multi-pathway comparison.
#97Sludge Production ModelPredicts future sludge production at design flow — use that output as input to this calc for capacity planning.

118 Collection System Design Suite EXAM

Four collection system design sub-calculators critical for TCEQ Class A Collection Systems exam section: Peaking Factor, Self-Cleaning Velocity Check, Infiltration Allowance, and Pipe Cover Depth. Select a tab to begin.
Peak flow factor multiplied by average dry weather flow gives design flow for pipe sizing. Three methods — Babbitt, Harmon, and PHF — each used in different TCEQ contexts.
For Harmon & Babbitt formulas
Harmon PF = 1 + 14/(4+√P) | Babbitt PF = 5/Q^0.2 (Q in MGD) | TCEQ PHF = 4.0 for populations <1,000
TCEQ 30 TAC §317.6: minimum velocity 2.0 fps at design flow for gravity sewers to prevent solids deposition. Maximum 10 fps to prevent scour.
Minimum 8" per TCEQ
V = Q/A | A = π(D/2)² for full flow | TCEQ min: 2 fps | Max: 10 fps
TCEQ standard infiltration allowance: 100–500 gal/inch-diameter/mile/day for design. This calc converts allowance to GPD for your collection system reach.
TCEQ design standard: 200
Add 100 GPD per manhole
I/I Design = Allowance × Diameter (in) × Length (mi) + (100 GPD × Manholes)
Minimum cover depth prevents pipe damage from traffic and thermal effects. TCEQ §317.6 requires minimum 3 ft cover under roadways; 2 ft in non-traffic areas. Maximum depth limited by trench safety.
Min cover = top of pipe to finished grade | TCEQ road min: 3 ft | Non-road: 2 ft | State hwy: 4 ft (typically)
Exam Tip: TCEQ exam commonly asks minimum sewer depth under roads (3 ft), minimum pipe size (8-inch), minimum slope for 8-inch pipe (0.40%), and minimum velocity (2 fps). Know all four numbers.

119 TCEQ 30-TAC Chapter 217 Design Standards Checker EXAM

Enter your design parameters → instantly check each against TCEQ 30 TAC Chapter 217 minimums. Red = fails minimum, Yellow = marginal, Green = passes. The most-tested TCEQ regulatory standard for Class A exam design questions.
TCEQ min: 6 hrs (conventional)
Typical: 1,500–3,000
Typical: 0.2–0.5
Min for nitrification: 8–10 days
TCEQ max: 600 gpd/sf avg
TCEQ max: 30 lbs/sf·day
TCEQ min: 2.0 mg/L
Typical: 25–100%
TCEQ min: 30 min at avg flow
TCEQ min: 1.0 mg/L prior to dechlorination
TCEQ min: 40:1
Min 30 mJ/cm² for fecal coliform
TCEQ min: 8 inches
TCEQ min: 0.40% for 8-inch
TCEQ min: 2.0 fps
TCEQ min: 3 ft
TCEQ max: 400 ft for pipes ≤15"
TCEQ min at 20°C: 20 days
TCEQ min: 1.0 mg/L
TCEQ min: 15 days at 35°C
TCEQ typical: 1–2 sf/capita
TCEQ Exam — Most-Tested Chapter 217 Values: Aeration HRT ≥ 6 hrs | SOR ≤ 600 gpd/sf | SLR ≤ 30 lb/sf·d | DO ≥ 2.0 mg/L | Cl₂ contact ≥ 30 min | L:W ≥ 40:1 | Min pipe 8" | Min slope 0.40% | Min velocity 2 fps | Min road cover 3 ft | Max manhole spacing 400 ft

120 Sludge Hauling & Disposal Cost Estimator NEW

Compare annual disposal costs across methods — landfill, land application, composting, incineration. Calculates dry tons/day, annual cost per method, cost per dry ton. Use for capital project justification and ProcessCore consulting reports.
Dewatered cake from BFP or centrifuge output
BFP: 18-24% | Centrifuge: 20-28%
Dewatered cake: 1.04-1.08
Houston metro typical: $50-90/wet ton
Texas Class B land app: $25-55/dry ton
Co-composting with green waste: $60-120/dry ton
Thermal oxidation: $150-350/dry ton
Used for transport cost note
Dry tons/day = GPD x 8.34 x SG x (%/100) / 2,000 | Wet tons/day = GPD x 8.34 x SG / 2,000 | Annual = tons/day x 365 x $/ton

121 Energy Benchmarking Calculator NEW

Compare plant energy intensity to EPA Energy Star benchmarks. Breaks down aeration as % of total plant energy. Calculates wire-to-water aeration efficiency. EPA benchmark: 1,000-2,500 kWh/MG for activated sludge. Small plants often run 2,000-5,000 kWh/MG — this calc shows exactly where savings are.
Flow meter totalizer / 1,000,000
From utility bill or SCADA kWh meter
Sum of all aeration motor nameplate kW
From SCADA runtime totalizer
From Calc #59 (AOTR) or process model
Houston avg ~$0.08-0.12/kWh
Energy Intensity = kWh / MG | Blower kWh = kW x hrs | Aeration % = blower kWh / total x 100 | Wire-to-water = lbs O2 / (blower kW x 0.7457 x hrs/day)

122 Activated Sludge Process Stability Index NEW

Composite health score 0-100 from 6 key process parameters. Flags bulking, washout, nitrification failure, and overaeration. Recommends one priority action. Score >=80 = Stable | 60-79 = Monitor | <60 = Act Now.
Good: 80-150 | Bulking: >150 | Pin floc: <50
Target: 1.5-3.0 mg/L
CAS: 5-15 | Nitrification min: 8 days
CAS: 0.2-0.5 | EA: 0.05-0.15
Compare today vs 3 days ago
Confirms nitrification occurring; enter 0 if absent
Score = SVI(25%) + DO(20%) + SRT(20%) + F/M(20%) + MLSS trend(10%) + nitrification(5%) | Each parameter 0-100 vs targets
Built by Armand Frazier Sr. | Houston, TX

👋 Welcome to WW Pro-Calc!

Quick tour to get you started

🔍
Search & Filter
Use the search bar to find any calculator instantly. Filter chips let you browse by category — try "Activated Sludge" or "Collection".
💡 Pro tip: Search works on formulas too! Try "8.34" or "MCRT".
⭐
Save Favorites
Tap the star icon on any calculator header to add it to your favorites. Access them quickly with the ⭐ Favorites filter chip.
💡 Your favorites persist between sessions!
📊
Process Diagrams
Look for the purple "DIAGRAM" badge on calculator headers. These have animated SVG diagrams showing the process flow — great for visual learners!
💡 Use the 📊 Diagrams filter to see all 20 calculators with diagrams.
📖
How to Use Guides
Many calculators have a "📖 How to Use" button. These expand to show detailed explanations, input guidance, and TCEQ exam tips.
💡 30 calculators have these guides — look for the green button!
🧙
Process Wizards
Having a process problem? The 16 Process Wizards guide you through troubleshooting step-by-step. Access them via the 🧙 Wizards filter chip.
💡 Wizards cover: Foaming, Bulking, Ammonia, Odor, Digester Upset, and more!
📝
TCEQ Exam Mode
Preparing for your operator exam? Tap the red "Exam" button for practice questions generated from your plant data. Track progress and review explanations.
💡 85 exam questions with detailed explanations!

❓ Help & Resources

Overall Progress to 70% (Passing)
📊 Category Performance
🕸️ Weakness Radar — TCEQ Class A Topics
Updates as you practice. Red = critical weakness. Target: all segments in the green zone before exam.
Complete at least one practice session to populate the radar.
📅 Recent Sessions
No sessions yet
📖 How to Use TCEQ Exam Prep
This system has six tools. Each one serves a different phase of your study. Use them in order during a study session for best results. Your exam is in June 2026 — you need 70% to pass.
STEP 1 📊 Practice Tab — Daily Math Drills
Start every session here. Select a category — your weakest ones (Collection Systems, Design Criteria, Sludge Disposal) are highlighted in red. Hit Start Practice and answer math questions one at a time. Type your answer and submit. After each question you see the correct answer, the formula used, and an explanation.

Timed Mode: Once you're consistently getting 70%+, switch to 2 min/question to simulate actual exam pressure. The real TCEQ exam allows approximately 2.5 minutes per question.

Weak Area Tags at the top auto-update based on your score history. If Collection Systems shows in red, that's where you open today.
STEP 2 🤖 AI Tutor Tab — When You Miss a Question
Whenever you miss a question in Practice, immediately go here. The 🤖 Walk Me Through This button appears on the feedback screen — tap it while you're still on that question to get a numbered step-by-step breakdown from the AI explaining exactly what went wrong and how to fix it.

Three tools in this tab:
• AI Question Generator — unlimited fresh TCEQ-style questions beyond the built-in pool. Set your category and difficulty, hit Generate.
• Smart Weakness Drill — auto-selects your 3 lowest-scoring categories and runs 10 targeted questions. Use after Practice once the weak area tags update.
• AI Session Coach — unlocks after each Practice session. Reviews your performance and tells you exactly what to study next.
STEP 3 🧙 Exam Wizards — When You Don't Know the Concept
If Practice or AI Tutor shows you that you don't understand a concept (not just made an arithmetic error), go to the 🔧 Wizards module and filter by 🎓 Exam. Six wizards cover the categories that appear most on the Class A exam:
ACTIVATED SLUDGE
🔢 RAS/WAS Math
ACTIVATED SLUDGE
🌡️ Nitrification & Temp
WEAK AREA ★
🔧 Collection Systems
WEAK AREA ★
📐 Design Criteria
WEAK AREA ★
💩 Sludge Disposal
DISINFECTION
🧫 CT & Log Inactivation
Each wizard gives you step-by-step worked examples, TCEQ exam tips, and direct links to the relevant calculators. Red ★ = your identified weak areas from the 52% attempt.
STEP 4 🎴 Flashcards Tab — Memorize Formulas and Limits
Use flashcards to lock in the numbers the exam expects you to have memorized: design limits, formula constants, regulatory thresholds, and definitions. Six decks available — Key Formulas, Definitions, Permit Limits & Standards, Collection Systems, Design Criteria, Sludge & Biosolids.

Tap the card to flip from question to answer. Use Next/Previous to move through the deck. Best used after a Practice session while the question topics are fresh — the card will reinforce what the calculation just required you to know.
STEP 5 📈 Progress Tab — Track Your Score by Category
Check this weekly — not daily. The Category Performance bars show your running score in each subject. Any category below 70% is a failing area. Your target: all categories at or above 70% before June.

Recent Sessions shows your last 5 results with date, category, and score. If a category is stuck below 60% after 3+ sessions, run the Weakness Drill and then work through the corresponding exam wizard before trying practice again.
EXAM DAY 🚨 Exam Day Drill — Final 20-Minute Review
Use this tab only on exam day — 20 minutes before your test window opens. Select Weak Areas and 20 min time limit. The drill cycles through your weakest categories first. Tap to reveal, mark cards you're unsure on — those re-queue automatically so you see them again before time's up.

Goal: arrive at your testing station with the key formulas and design limits actively loaded in short-term memory. This is not for learning new material. It is for activating what you already know.
⚡ Recommended Daily Study Flow
5 MIN
Practice Tab — Check weak area tags, select the lowest-scoring category, run 10–20 questions.
5 MIN
AI Tutor — Use Walk Me Through on any missed questions. Then run the Weakness Drill if available.
5 MIN
Exam Wizard — If you missed concept questions (not just arithmetic), open the matching wizard for a full walkthrough.
5 MIN
Flashcards — Run one deck from the same category you practiced. Flip 10–15 cards.
⏰ Study Schedule — June 2026 Target
Now – Apr Weak areas first. Collection Systems + Design Criteria + Sludge Disposal. 20 questions/day. Run matching exam wizard after each missed concept. April Full-category rotation. Cycle through all 8 categories in Practice. Run AI Question Generator for fresh questions in scoring categories. Check Progress tab weekly. May Timed pressure mode. Switch Practice to 2 min/question. All categories. Target ≥70% in every category before month end. June Final review only. Flashcards and Exam Day Drill. No new material. Confidence maintenance. On exam day: Exam Day Drill, 20 minutes, Weak Areas deck.
✅ Test-Day Strategy
Write first, calculate second. Before touching math, write down the formula for what's being asked. It takes 5 seconds and prevents formula substitution errors.
Units before numbers. Write down all given values with their units. Convert before calculating. MGD → GPD, ft → inches, % → decimal. Unit errors cause more wrong answers than formula errors.
Reasonableness check. After calculating, ask: is this a sane number? SVI of 5,000 is wrong. WAS rate of 5,000,000 GPD for a 1-MGD plant is wrong. An obviously wrong answer means recheck units.
Skip and return. If a question takes more than 3 minutes, mark it and move on. Return at the end. Never let one hard question cost you five easy ones.
8.34 rule. Only use 8.34 when converting mg/L × MGD to lbs/day. Never use it for ratios (F:M, SRT, RAS%), never use it when both sides are already in lbs or pounds.
📋 Must-Know Formulas — Write These on Scratch Paper First
// Core
lbs/day = MGD × mg/L × 8.34
% Removal = (In − Out) ÷ In × 100
DT (hrs) = Volume (gal) ÷ Flow (GPH)
// Activated Sludge
SVI = (settled mL/L × 1,000) ÷ MLSS
F:M = (Q × BOD) ÷ (Vol × MLSS)
SRT = (Vol × MLSS) ÷ (WAS × WAS conc)
RAS = Q_inf × MLSS ÷ (RAS conc − MLSS)
// Design
SOR (gpd/ft²) = Flow (gpd) ÷ Area (ft²)
SLR (lbs/day/ft²) = Solids (lbs/day) ÷ Area
Clarifier area: A = π × (D/2)²
// Collection
V = (1.486/n) × R^⅔ × S^½ [R = D/4 full pipe]
BHP = (GPM × TDH) ÷ (3,960 × eff)
// Disinfection
Dose = Demand + Residual
CT = Residual (mg/L) × T10 (min)
VS Reduction = (VS_in − VS_out) ÷ VS_in × 100

Enter your Anthropic API key to unlock all 5 AI features — saved only on this device, never sent to anyone except Anthropic.

2

3

4
Paste it below and tap Save

🧙 Process Control Wizards TROUBLESHOOT 54 WIZARDS
Guided diagnosis & corrective actions for common problems
🤖
AI Plant Advisor
Tap to open full-screen · reads your calculator values automatically
▶
Quick prompts:
Enter to send · Shift+Enter for new line · Up to 6 turns per session
🔍
No wizards match your search.
🦠
High SVI / Bulking
Diagnose filamentous bulking, rising sludge, or poor settling
💧
Effluent Quality
TSS, BOD, or ammonia violations troubleshooting
💨
DO Problems
Can't maintain DO, blower issues, aeration troubleshooting
🫧
Foam & Scum
Brown foam, Nocardia, startup foam, grease problems
🌀
Clarifier Issues
Rising sludge, floating solids, blanket depth problems
🧪
Nutrient Removal
Nitrification failure, high ammonia, phosphorus issues
⚗️
Ammonia Breakthrough
Sudden ammonia spike, nitrifier washout, permit violations
👃
Odor / H₂S Control
Rotten egg smell, headworks odor, collection system H₂S
🗜️
Dewatering Problems
Poor cake solids, high polymer use, belt press issues
🔥
Digester Upset
Low gas, high VFA, pH drop, foaming digesters
⬆️
Lift Station Issues
Pump cycling, wet well odor, float problems, alarms
💀
Septicity & Corrosion
Black wastewater, crown corrosion, force main issues
☠️
Toxic Shock
Industrial spill, sudden die-off, process upset recovery
🅿️
Phosphorus Removal
High effluent P, chemical dosing, bio-P failure
🚀
Plant Startup
New plant, seasonal restart, seeding, acclimation
⚡
Energy Optimization
High power bills, blower efficiency, pump scheduling
🚨
SSO Response
Sanitary Sewer Overflow reporting, cleanup, TCEQ notification
🐟
Fish Kill Response
Fish kill reporting, investigation, TCEQ/TPWD notification
🫁
DO Violation Response
Low DO exceedance, corrective action, DMR reporting
⚠️
Ammonia Violation
NH₃ permit exceedance, 24-hour notice, corrective action
🔀
Bypass / Upset
Emergency bypass procedures, documentation, TCEQ reporting
🛢️
Chemical Spill
Spill response, containment, agency notification requirements
🧪
Chlorine Disinfection
Gas, liquid, granular chlorine dosing, safety, and troubleshooting
💧
I&I Investigation
Inflow & infiltration sources, smoke testing, flow isolation
🌱
Land Application
Class A/B biosolids, pathogen reduction, vector attraction
🧫
Sampling Techniques
Grab vs composite, preservation, holding times, chain of custody
⛑️
Confined Space Entry
Atmospheric testing, ventilation, entry permits, rescue
🔄
Pump Troubleshooting
Centrifugal, PD pumps, cavitation, priming, performance curves
🏭
Plant Assessment
Comprehensive plant evaluation - capacity, performance, and optimization recommendations
🔬
MBR Troubleshooting
High TMP, membrane fouling, flux decline, CIP recovery, integrity testing
🪨
Trickling Filter Problems
Ponding, filter flies, odor, BOD failure, recirculation and dosing issues
🌊
Lagoon / Pond Problems
Algae blooms, stratification, effluent violations, sludge accumulation, odor
⏱️
SBR Troubleshooting
Cycle timing, decant quality, floating sludge, ammonia in effluent, MLSS control
🧬
MBBR / Biofilm Problems
Media not moving, biofilm loss, sloughing, high effluent TSS, DO in reactor
⚙️
RAS / WAS Optimization
Set daily WAS rate, RAS rate, target MLSS, and SRT for activated sludge control
🧱
Alkalinity & pH Control
Low alkalinity killing nitrification, pH drop, caustic/lime/soda ash dosing
💉
Chemical Feed Troubleshooting
Polymer, alum, chlorine, caustic — pump calibration, dose verification, clogging
🔆
UV Disinfection Problems
Lamp fouling, intensity drop, dose failure, sleeve cleaning, ballast issues
🏛️
TCEQ Inspection Prep
Guided self-audit: records, O&M manual, lab QA/QC, permit compliance, safety
📋
Permit Exceedance Response
DMR violation detected — sampling error? 24-hr notice? 5-day report? Corrective action plan
🌧️
Storm / Wet Weather Ops
High-flow event response, diluted influent adjustments, upset provision, TCEQ documentation
💨
Aeration System Failure
Blower down, diffuser fouling, mechanical aerator failure — triage and restore before biology is lost
⚙️
Clarifier Mechanical Issues
Rake drive overload, skimmer failure, weir problems, density currents — mechanical vs. process diagnosis
⚡
Influent Shock Loading
Organic surge, hydraulic surge, TSS shock — protect biology and clarifier during high-loading events
🌤️
Seasonal Transition Planning
Winter SRT adjustments, summer odor/filament prep, spring I/I dilution — proactive seasonal checklists
🔄
Operator Shift Handoff
Outgoing and incoming checklists, what to communicate, morning rounds after unattended operation
📄
DMR Completion Guidance
E. coli geometric mean, % removal calculations, submission deadlines, and amendment process
📜
Permit Renewal / Limits Review
TPDES renewal timeline, new nutrient limits, compliance history strategy, and reading your permit
🔢
Exam: RAS/WAS Math
Step-by-step WAS GPD, RAS rate, and SRT calculations — exactly how TCEQ exam questions are structured
🌡️
Exam: Nitrification & Temperature
SRT at cold temperatures, alkalinity consumption, DO requirements — high-frequency exam topic
🔧
Exam: Collection System Math
Manning's equation, pipe capacity, minimum velocity, I/I — your weakest category from the 52% attempt
📐
Exam: Design Criteria Math
SOR, SLR, WOR, clarifier sizing, aeration basin HRT — high-frequency design calculation patterns for Class A
💩
Exam: Sludge Disposal Math
VS reduction, sludge production, digester loading, dewatering cake solids — your weak area from the 52% attempt
🧫
Exam: Disinfection & CT Math
Chlorine dose/demand/residual, CT calculation, log inactivation, UV dose — appears on nearly every Class A exam
⚠️
Exam: Calculation Traps
Units, conversions, formula traps — the #1 source of lost points on the Class A exam
♻️
Exam: Biosolids & 503 Regs
Class A/B pathogen requirements, pollutant limits, PAN math, and land application rules
⚖️
Exam: Statutes & TCEQ Regs
Clean Water Act sections, 30 TAC rules, permit conditions, reporting requirements
🏭
Pretreatment / IU Violation
Slug loads, SIU exceedances, pass-through, interference, and enforcement steps
🫧
Digester Upset / Foaming
Diagnose and recover from foam, acid buildup, low gas production, and sour digester
🧫
Chlorine Demand Troubleshooting
CT not being met, high demand, permit residual failure, dechlorination overdose
📊
Permit Limit Approaching
Effluent at 80–95% of BOD, TSS, NH₃, or E. coli limit — what to do before violation
⚡
Energy & Blower Optimization
Reduce aeration energy cost — DO setpoint, VFD, diffuser efficiency, SOTR vs AOTR
🔬
Influent Characterization
Loading analysis, I/I assessment, industrial contributor identification, capacity utilization
🔥
Digester Gas Troubleshooting
Gas production drop, low methane, acid buildup, temperature effects, toxic inhibition
💉
Chemical Feed Commissioning
New system setup: chemical-specific prep, pump priming, timed calibration, dose verification, alarm setpoints, TCEQ documentation
🔬
MBR Startup & Conditioning
Basin filling, membrane wetting, biomass seeding, conditioning targets, flux baseline, first CIP protocol
🔧
Collection System Capacity
Manning's pipe capacity, peaking factors, I/I quantification, lift station adequacy, TCEQ 30 TAC 217 triggers
📋 Daily Operator Log
Record readings • Auto-populate calculators • Track history

💧 Flow Readings
Influent Flow MGD
Daily average from flow meter totalizer
Effluent Flow MGD
Effluent meter or calculated
Peak Flow (if noted) MGD
Instantaneous peak from SCADA
RAS Flow GPD
RAS pump runtime × GPD rating
WAS Flow GPD
WAS pump runtime × GPD rating
I/I Estimate GPD
Total − dry weather baseline
Aeration Basin (Fixed — update rarely)
Basin Volume MG
As-built drawings — rarely changes
Clarifier Surface Area ft²
π/4 × D² for circular clarifier
Clarifier Weir Length ft
π × D for circular clarifier
Design Flow MGD
TPDES permit Page 1
🦠 Biological Process Readings
MLSS mg/L
Typical: 1500–4000 mg/L
MLVSS mg/L
Typically 75–85% of MLSS
DO — Aeration Basin mg/L
Target: 1.5–3.0 mg/L (BOD); 2.0–4.0 (nitrif)
pH — Aeration Basin SU
Optimal: 6.5–7.8 for AS
Temperature °C
Basin water temp; affects nitrification
30-min Settled Volume (SV30) mL/L
1L settlometer, 30 min. Normal: 150–350
RAS MLSS / Conc mg/L
Typical: 6000–12000 mg/L
WAS Concentration mg/L
Same as MLSS if wasting from basin
Alkalinity mg/L as CaCO₃
Minimum 80–100 for nitrification
Influent NH₃-N mg/L
Composite sample lab result
Effluent NH₃-N mg/L
Permit limit reference — check Page 2a
OUR DO Start mg/L
DO at start of OUR aeration-off test
OUR DO End mg/L
DO at end of OUR test period
OUR Test Duration minutes
Time between DO start and DO end readings
🏗️ Solids & Sludge
Influent TSS mg/L
24-hr composite sample
Effluent TSS mg/L
Composite sample; typical permit: ≤30 mg/L
Target SRT days
Nitrification: 8–20d; BOD only: 4–8d
Min SRT at 20°C days
From design engineer / O&M manual
Influent BOD mg/L
24-hr composite BOD₅ result
Effluent BOD mg/L
Composite effluent result; typical permit ≤30
Sludge Blanket Depth ft
Sludge judge. Action level: 4–6 ft typically
Digester pH SU
Optimal 6.8–7.4; below 6.5 = upset
Digester Temp °C
Mesophilic: 35–38°C; vary <1°C/day
Digester Gas Production ft³/day
From gas meter; dropping = early upset warning
✅ Effluent Quality Parameters
Effluent BOD mg/L
Permit limit reference — check 2a
Effluent TSS mg/L
Effluent NH₃-N mg/L
Effluent pH SU
Permit: typically 6.0–9.0
Effluent DO mg/L
Some permits require min DO in effluent
Turbidity NTU
Filtered: <0.3 NTU. Unfiltered: <1 NTU
E. coli MPN/100mL
Typical permit: ≤126 MPN/100mL avg
Total Nitrogen mg/L
Total Phosphorus mg/L
⚗️ Chemical & Disinfection
Chlorine Residual — Pre-dechlorination mg/L
At end of contact chamber
Chlorine Residual — Post-dechlorination mg/L
Permit: typically ≤0.1 mg/L
Chlorine Feed Rate lbs/day
Contact Time minutes
Vol ÷ Flow rate. TCEQ: ≥30 min.
Chemical — Polymer Usage lbs/day
Chemical — Coagulant Usage gal/day or lbs/day
Chemical — Caustic / Lime Usage lbs or gal
UV Intensity (if applicable) mW/cm²
Min 38 mW/cm² for Class A reuse typically
⚡
CT value will appear here when Cl₂ residual and contact time are entered.
⚙️ Equipment & Operational Status
Aeration System
Clarifiers & Pumps
Disinfection & Chemical Systems
Monitoring & Recording
Permit Compliance Checks
Equipment Issues / Work Orders Needed
📝 Shift Notes & Observations
General Shift Observations
Process Adjustments Made This Shift
Safety Incidents / Near-Misses
Handoff Notes (for next shift)
📈 Log History & Trends (Last 30 Days)
No saved logs yet. Complete a log and press 💾 Save Log to start tracking history.
🚀 Populate Calculators from This Log
💡
Enter readings in the other tabs first, then click any button below to instantly fill that calculator's inputs and run it. The app will scroll to the calculator.
Activated Sludge & Process Control
Clarifier Loading
Organic & Hydraulic Loading
Compliance & Reporting
Populate All at Once
📋 Daily Operator Log — How to Use
Complete guide to every feature · WW Pro-Calc v9.40
JUMP TO:
📌 Overview — What Is the Daily Log? START HERE ▼

The Daily Operator Log is your shift's single source of truth. Enter your readings once — DO, MLSS, flows, effluent quality — and the log automatically computes process status, flags concerns, and can push those values directly into any of 15 calculators with one click. No more re-typing the same numbers into four different calcs.

🎯 Core Purpose
Record every operational reading for your shift in one place. The log is structured the same way a TCEQ inspector expects your records to be — organized by parameter category, date-stamped, and operator-signed.
⚡ Auto-Populate
After entering readings, go to the 🚀 Populate tab and click any calculator button. Your values flow in instantly and the calculator fires. Scroll to the calc, verify the result, and move on.
📊 Live Intelligence
The colored status chips at the top compute SVI, F:M, SRT, BOD removal efficiency, DO status, pH, Cl₂, and RAS% in real time as you type. You see process health without opening a single calculator.
📈 30-Day History
Every saved log is stored in the 📈 History tab with trend arrows (↑↓) showing whether key parameters are improving or declining compared to the previous shift. Useful for DMR prep and TCEQ inspections.
✅
Best practice: Open the Daily Log at the start of every shift. Fill in readings as you collect them throughout the shift. Save at end of shift. The draft saves automatically so you won't lose data if you close the log mid-shift.
💡
Fixed values (basin volume, clarifier area): Enter these once in the 💧 Flow tab. They persist in your draft and carry forward. You only need to update them if plant configuration changes.
🏷️ Date, Shift & Operator Fields ▼

These four fields at the top of the log are the minimum required identifiers for every shift record. They appear on the printed log sheet and in the history table.

FieldWhat to EnterWhy It Matters
📅 Date Auto-fills to today. Change if entering a historical log. Date + Shift = unique log key. Saving again overwrites same date/shift — not duplicates.
⏱ Shift Click ☀️ Day, 🌆 Eve, or 🌙 Night. Each shift is a separate log record. Day and night shift both show in history independently.
👷 Operator Your name and license number (e.g. "A. Frazier — TCEQ Class A #12345"). Required for legal operator records. The log remembers your name and pre-fills it next time.
🏭 Facility Facility name or TPDES permit number. Critical if you operate multiple plants. Also remembered between sessions.
⚠️
Overwrite behavior: If you save a log for June 14 / Day shift and then save again for the same date and shift, the second save overwrites the first. This is intentional — it prevents duplicate records when you update readings during a shift. If you need to correct a historical entry, load it from History, edit it, and Save again.
📊 Live Status Chips — Real-Time Process Health ALWAYS VISIBLE ▼

The colored chips immediately below the meta row update in real time as you type. They give you an at-a-glance process health snapshot without opening a single calculator. All calculations use the standard TCEQ/industry formulas.

ChipFormula UsedInputs NeededColor Logic
SVI SV30 ÷ MLSS × 1000 SV30 (mL/L), MLSS (mg/L) <150 150–250 >250
F:M (Flow × BOD) ÷ (Vol × MLVSS) Inf Flow, Inf BOD, Basin Vol, MLVSS 0.05–0.20 0.20–0.35 >0.35 or <0.05
SRT (Vol × MLSS × 8.34) ÷ [(WAS Q × WAS C + Eff Q × Eff TSS) × 8.34] Basin Vol, MLSS, WAS Flow & Conc, Eff Flow & TSS 5–20d 3–5d <3d
BOD Eff (Inf BOD − Eff BOD) ÷ Inf BOD × 100% Inf BOD, Eff BOD ≥90% 80–90% <80%
DO Direct reading DO — Aeration Basin 1.5–4.0 1.0–1.5 or >4 <1.0
pH Direct reading pH — Aeration Basin 6.5–7.8 outside this range
Cl₂ Direct reading Cl₂ Residual — Pre-dechlor ≥0.5 mg/L <0.5 mg/L
RAS% RAS Flow (GPD) ÷ Inf Flow (GPD) × 100% RAS Flow, Inf Flow 25–75% outside range
💡
Gray chip = missing data. A chip shows "—" and stays gray until both required inputs are entered. Enter MLSS and SV30 first — SVI appears immediately. Enter Inf Flow and BOD and Basin Vol and MLVSS to get F:M.
⚠️
SRT chip needs WAS data. The SRT chip requires WAS Flow (GPD), WAS Concentration, Effluent Flow, and Effluent TSS in addition to MLSS and Basin Volume. If WAS Conc is blank, it defaults to the MLSS value (wasting from basin assumption).
📑 The 9 Tabs — What Goes Where 9 SECTIONS ▼

The log is organized into 9 tabs. A green dot appears on a tab once you enter any data in it. You don't need to fill every field — enter what you have and skip the rest.

1
💧 Flow — Daily flow meter readings: influent, effluent, peak flow, RAS and WAS flows (in GPD), I/I estimate. Also enter fixed plant constants here: basin volume (MG), clarifier surface area (ft²), clarifier weir length (ft), and design flow. Enter the constants once — they persist across sessions.
2
🦠 Biology — The core biological process readings: MLSS, MLVSS, DO, pH, temperature, SV30. Also RAS and WAS concentrations, alkalinity, influent and effluent NH₃-N. OUR test fields (DO start, DO end, test duration in minutes) for Calc #77. These are the inputs that drive the most status chips and the most calculator auto-populates.
3
🏗️ Solids — Influent and effluent TSS, influent and effluent BOD (also used for removal efficiency), target SRT and minimum SRT at 20°C. Sludge blanket depth. Digester pH, temperature, and daily gas production. The gas production field is a leading indicator of digester health — record it daily if you have a digester.
4
✅ Effluent Quality — Dedicated effluent-side parameters with permit-limit snapshot cards. Effluent BOD, TSS, NH₃-N, pH, DO, turbidity, E.coli, total nitrogen, total phosphorus. The six snapshot cards at the top turn green (≤ limit) or red (> limit) based on typical permit limits. Live alerts appear for any potential violations.
5
⚗️ Chemical/Disinfection — Chlorine residual pre- and post-dechlorination, chlorine feed rate, contact time. A live CT value (mg·min/L) auto-calculates from Cl₂ × contact time and compares it to the Giardia target of 450. Also: polymer, coagulant, caustic usage, and UV intensity if applicable.
6
⚙️ Equipment — 17-item shift inspection checklist covering aeration, clarifiers, pumps, disinfection, monitoring systems, and permit compliance checks. Items turn green when checked. A free-text field for equipment issues and work orders. This checklist mirrors what a TCEQ inspector expects to see documented.
7
📝 Notes — Four free-text fields: General Observations, Process Adjustments Made, Safety Incidents/Near-Misses, and Handoff Notes. The handoff notes field is critical — write what the next shift operator needs to know the moment they walk in. These print on the shift report.
8
📈 History — A table of all saved logs (up to 30 shown, up to 90 stored). Each row shows key parameters with trend arrows compared to the previous entry. Click any row to load that log's values into the form. Useful for DMR preparation — all daily values in one place.
9
🚀 Populate — The power feature. After entering readings, click any calculator button to instantly fill that calculator's inputs and run the calculation. You can also click ⚡ Populate ALL Calculators to push your readings to all 15 at once. See the full Populate section below for details.
💡
Effluent BOD syncs between tabs: The Effluent BOD field appears in both the 🏗️ Solids tab and the ✅ Effluent Quality tab. They are linked — entering a value in either location updates both. Same for Effluent TSS and Effluent NH₃-N.
🚀 Populate Calculators — The Power Feature 15 CALCS ▼

This is the primary reason to use the Daily Log. Enter your shift readings once, then push them to any calculator. The log closes, scrolls to the calculator, opens it, fills the inputs, and fires the calculation — all in one click.

⚡
How it works: Click a populate button → daily log closes → app scrolls to the calculator card → card opens → inputs are filled → calculation runs → card flashes blue to show it was populated. You verify the result and move on.
ButtonCalculatorDaily Log Fields Used
SVI #2Settlability IndexSV30 → svi_set, MLSS → svi_mlss
F:M #4Food-to-Microorganism RatioInf Flow, Inf BOD, Basin Vol, MLVSS
SRT #5Solids Retention TimeBasin Vol, MLSS, WAS Flow/Conc, Eff Flow, Eff TSS, Temp, Min SRT
WAS Rate #8WAS Pumping RateBasin Vol, MLSS, WAS Concentration
RAS Ratio #9Return Activated SludgeInf Flow, MLSS, RAS Concentration
MLSS Control #96MLSS Control CenterMLSS, Basin Vol, WAS GPD/Conc, Inf Flow/TSS/BOD, Eff BOD
OUR #77Oxygen Uptake RateDO Start, DO End, OUR Duration, MLVSS
Alkalinity #44Nitrification AlkalinityAlkalinity, Inf NH₃-N, Inf Flow
SOR #11Surface Overflow RateInf Flow (→ GPD), Clarifier Area
WOR #12Weir Overflow RateInf Flow (→ GPD), Weir Length
SLR #13Solids Loading RateInf + RAS Flow (combined), MLSS, Clarifier Area
OLR #10Organic Loading RateInf Flow, Inf BOD, Basin Vol (→ 1000 ft³)
Detention Time #6HRT CalculationBasin Vol (→ gallons), Inf Flow (→ GPD)
Removal Eff #7BOD Removal EfficiencyInf BOD → eff_in, Eff BOD → eff_out
Pounds Formula #1Mass LoadingInf Flow → p_flow, Inf BOD → p_conc
DMR #91DMR CalculatorInf Flow → avg flow field
⚠️
Unit conversions are handled automatically. RAS and WAS flows in the Daily Log are entered in GPD. The populate function converts them to MGD where calculators need MGD (e.g., SRT calc needs WAS in MGD). Clarifier SOR and WOR calcs need GPD — the populate function converts Inf Flow from MGD to GPD. You never need to do unit math manually.
💡
⚡ Populate ALL: Pushes your readings to all 15 calculators simultaneously without closing the log. The populate action log at the bottom of the tab shows which fields were filled for each calculator. Use this before a DMR review or compliance audit to see all your process metrics at once.
🔴
Empty fields don't overwrite. If you haven't entered MLVSS in the Daily Log, clicking F:M populate won't clear the existing value in the F:M calculator. Only fields with actual values are pushed. This lets you partially populate — for example, update just flow and BOD without clearing a manually-set basin volume.
💾 Saving, Drafts & 30-Day History ▼

The log has two storage mechanisms: an auto-saved draft (restored on every open if it's from today) and permanent log entries you explicitly save. Understanding the difference is important.

🔄 Auto-Draft (Automatic)
Every field change auto-saves a draft. If you close the log mid-shift and reopen it, your readings are exactly where you left them — as long as it's the same calendar day. The draft does not persist to the next day intentionally.
💾 Save Log (Manual)
Clicking 💾 Save Log creates a permanent record in the 30-day history. Do this at the end of your shift, or anytime you want a snapshot. A ✓ Saved indicator appears in the meta row for 3 seconds.
📈 History Table
Up to 90 log entries are stored. The History tab shows the most recent 30. Trend arrows (↑ ↓ →) compare each entry to the previous one for key parameters. Click any row to load that log's values into the form.
🗑️ Clear History
The Clear All History button in the History tab deletes all saved logs permanently. This cannot be undone. Use only when starting fresh or changing facilities. Draft data is not affected.
⚠️
Storage location: All data is stored in your browser's localStorage. This means data is tied to this browser on this device. If you switch browsers or devices, or clear your browser data, the logs will be lost. For permanent records, use the 🖨️ Print function to create a PDF for each shift.
✅
Entering historical logs: Change the date in the 📅 Date field before saving. You can backfill a week's worth of logs by changing the date, entering readings, and saving for each day. Load any previous entry from History, change the date, modify readings, and save a new entry.
📤 Export to Clipboard & Print Shift Report ▼

Two output formats are available for every completed log. Both are accessible from the top-right buttons.

📤
Export (Copy to Clipboard) — Formats all entered values as plain text organized by category: Flow, Biological, Effluent, Notes. Paste into an email, a Teams message, a work order system, or a spreadsheet. Useful for quick shift handoff or sending readings to a supervisor. Includes a ProcessCore Systems footer with timestamp.
🖨️
Print Shift Report — Opens a formatted print-ready report in a new tab with tables for Flow, Biological Process, Effluent Quality, and Notes. The report includes operator name, date, shift, and facility. Use your browser's Ctrl+P or ⌘P to save as PDF or print to paper. This is your legal shift record.
💡
Print = PDF for your records. Print the shift report as a PDF and save to a folder named by month. This gives you a permanent copy independent of browser localStorage. Required if your facility needs to maintain printed operator logs for TCEQ compliance.
✅
DMR prep workflow: At month end, open the History tab. Each saved log shows flow, BOD, TSS, NH₃-N, Cl₂ for every shift. You can pull daily values directly from here into Calc #91 (DMR Calculator) using the 🚀 Populate → DMR button. The DMR calc then computes your monthly average and daily maximum automatically.
⚡ Recommended Shift Workflow START TO FINISH ▼

Here is the recommended sequence for using the Daily Log to run an efficient, well-documented shift. This workflow takes about 5 minutes of data entry and saves 20 minutes of repeated calculator work.

1
Start of shift — open and configure: Click 📋 Daily Log in the header. Confirm the date is correct. Click your shift button (☀️ Day / 🌆 Eve / 🌙 Night). Verify your name and facility are pre-filled (they save from last time).
2
Enter fixed plant data (first time only): In the 💧 Flow tab, scroll down to "Aeration Basin (Fixed)" and enter basin volume, clarifier surface area, clarifier weir length, and design flow. These are from as-built drawings and rarely change. They persist in your draft permanently.
3
As readings come in — enter by tab: Fill 💧 Flow readings from your flow meter totalizer. Go to 🦠 Biology and enter MLSS (as soon as your lab result is ready), DO, pH, temp, SV30. Enter 🏗️ Solids data after your TSS/BOD lab results arrive. Watch the status chips — they update live and flag anything out of range.
4
Run effluent quality check: Go to ✅ Effluent and enter your effluent parameters. The 6 snapshot cards instantly show green/red against typical permit limits. Any potential violation triggers a red alert box — address it before end of shift.
5
Log chemical readings and CT: In ⚗️ Chemical, enter Cl₂ residual pre- and post-dechlorination. Enter contact time. The CT value auto-calculates and compares to the 450 mg·min/L Giardia target. Enter chemical feed quantities for your records.
6
Complete the equipment checklist: Go to ⚙️ Equipment and work through the 17-item checklist. Check each item you've verified. Note any issues or needed work orders in the text field at the bottom. This is your shift inspection documentation.
7
Populate calculators for process control decisions: Go to 🚀 Populate. Click SRT, F:M, WAS Rate, and MLSS Control. Each calculator runs with your day's readings. If SRT is trending out of range, you have the calculated WAS adjustment right there.
8
Write handoff notes: Go to 📝 Notes and fill in at least the Handoff Notes field. What does the next operator need to know immediately? Pending results, unusual trends, equipment issues, SCADA alarms. This is a professional standard — the next operator's safety may depend on it.
9
Save and optionally print: Click 💾 Save Log. The ✓ Saved indicator confirms. If your facility requires printed records, click 🖨️ Print and save as PDF. Done — your shift is fully documented.
🎓 TCEQ Class A Exam Connections EXAM PREP ▼

Every field in this log connects to a testable concept on the Class A exam. Using the daily log in real operations — or even simulating a shift with practice data — builds the muscle memory and unit fluency that the exam tests. Here are the direct connections.

Daily Log FieldExam TopicWhat the Exam Tests
MLSS + SV30 → SVIActivated SludgeSVI formula, acceptable ranges, what high SVI means (bulking)
F:M chip calculationActivated SludgeF:M formula, MLVSS vs MLSS distinction, starvation vs overload
SRT chip calculationActivated SludgeSRT formula (all three terms), effect of temperature on min SRT, nitrification SRT threshold
DO — Aeration BasinActivated Sludge / DesignAcceptable ranges, effect on nitrification, SOTR vs AOTR
RAS Flow ÷ Inf Flow = RAS%Activated SludgeRAS ratio formula, typical range (25–75%), mass balance implications
WAS Flow × WAS ConcActivated SludgeSolids wasting calculations, lbs/day formula
Cl₂ × Contact Time = CTDisinfectionCT concept, Giardia 450 mg·min/L requirement, T10 baffling factor
Inf Flow ÷ Clarifier Area = SORDesign CriteriaSOR limits (600–800 GPD/ft²), clarifier sizing, peak vs average flow
Eff BOD + Eff TSS limitsCollection / ComplianceTPDES permit limits, daily max vs monthly average, violation reporting
Digester pH + gas productionSludge DisposalVA/Alk ratio, digester upset indicators, PFRP/PSRP requirements
Alkalinity + NH₃-NActivated SludgeAlkalinity consumed per mg NH₃ nitrified (7.14 mg/mg), minimum 80 mg/L
OUR DO Start/End/Time → MLVSSActivated SludgeOUR formula, biology health assessment, respiration rate interpretation
🎓
Practice drill: Use the Daily Log with simulated data from a TCEQ Class A practice problem. Enter the given values (flow, MLSS, SV30, BOD, WAS) into the log. Watch the chips compute SVI, F:M, SRT, and BOD removal — then verify your mental math against the chips. This is faster than working each formula by hand and reinforces unit fluency.
✅
Status chip ranges are exam ranges: The color boundaries in the status chips match TCEQ-standard acceptable operating ranges. SVI <150 (excellent), 150–250 (moderate bulking), >250 (severe bulking). SRT 5–20 days for AS. F:M 0.05–0.20 for conventional activated sludge. These thresholds appear directly on Class A exam questions.
WW Pro-Calc v9.40 · Daily Log Help Guide · ProcessCore Systems · Houston, TX
